L0_description: The narrative explicitly states strong winds were present and blew the jumper into a guy wire. Jumping an antenna in strong wind conditions is a decision error — the domain reference states that wind evaluation is mandatory for antennas, few objects are good in winds over 10 mph, and none are good over 20 mph. The jumper's decision to jump in strong winds directly led to the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump from a guyed antenna in medium-to-strong wind conditions. Strong crosswinds blew him into a guy wire under canopy, causing canopy collapse at 300 feet. The decision to jump in winds strong enough to drift a canopy into guy wires from a three-guy-wire antenna — which inherently restricts safe heading range — was clearly and specifically inappropriate, and this decision was the producing mechanism for the fatal object strike.

L0_description: The narrative states only that the jumper hit the wall after opening and came to rest in an inaccessible position. There is no information about experience level, equipment details, exit quality, body position, off-heading occurrence, packing method, or site conditions beyond 'light and variable' winds. The cause of the object strike cannot be determined from the available information, and there is no positive evidence of a non-human cause either.

L0_label: Pull-up cord left in closing loop
L0_description: The narrative explicitly states a pull-up cord was left in the closing loop of the container, which is a rigging/packing error that caused a total malfunction on a static line jump from 330 feet. This is a human error in equipment preparation that directly caused the fatality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Rigging error: pull-up cord left attached
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Frank left a pull-up cord in the closing loop of his container, which prevented the static line from extracting the canopy, resulting in a total malfunction. This is a memory/attention failure during the rigging process — he intended to complete the rigging correctly but failed to remove a tool that blocked container opening. The omission directly produced the total malfunction and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: No pin check was performed prior to exit that would have detected the pull-up cord left in the closing loop. The narrative explicitly states this event 'made everyone realize even the experts required a pin check prior to launch,' indicating the absence of a gear check that accepted practice requires and that would have caught the rigging error.

L0_description: The jumper conducted a short-delay cliff jump in gusty mid-day conditions with crosswinds, resulting in an off-heading opening and fatal object strike. The decision to jump in strong, gusty wind conditions is a human error, as the domain reference establishes that few objects are good in winds over 10 mph and wind at opening altitude usually affects openings negatively.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in gusty conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a short delay cliff jump in gusty mid-day conditions with crosswinds at the exit. The narrative explicitly identifies strong/medium winds and crosswinds as possible factors, and gusty mid-day conditions are clearly inappropriate for a slider-down cliff jump where an off-heading opening leaves minimal time and separation to avoid object strike. The decision to jump in these conditions was the producing mechanism: the gusty crosswinds caused the 180° off-heading opening that put the jumper into the wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Gusty crosswinds degraded opening heading
L2_preconditions.description: The gusty mid-day conditions with crosswinds at the exit directly degraded opening heading performance. Wind at opening altitude is explicitly identified as a factor affecting openings, and the crosswinds mechanically contributed to the 180° off-heading that resulted in object strike.

L0_description: The jumper used a single loose knot to connect the static line break cord to the parachute, whereas other jumpers used double knots. This inadequate knot caused the connection to break prematurely, preventing the static line from extracting the canopy. Combined with using a slow-opening system from a 170m object, the canopy had insufficient altitude to inflate after the rigging failure converted the jump to an unplanned freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate static line attachment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Pauli used a loose single knot to connect the static line break cord to the parachute, while other jumpers used double knots. This inadequate knot broke prematurely during deployment, causing the static line to disconnect before it could extract the pilot chute from the bag. The jump became an unassisted freefall from a 170m antenna using a slow-opening system with insufficient altitude for inflation, resulting in impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Pauli did not have his own parachute gear and borrowed a slow-opening Unit system. This system required significantly more altitude to fully deploy than a standard BASE configuration, meaning the inadequate static line knot left zero margin for recovery from the 170m antenna.

L0_label: Reserve deployment and water egress errors
L0_description: The jumper deployed his reserve too quickly during a slow main opening, resulting in two canopies out and a water landing. After landing in the river and signaling he was okay, he made no attempt to get out of his gear, allowing the reserve canopy to catch the swift current and pull him under. The premature reserve deployment and failure to disconnect from gear in moving water represent identifiable human errors contributing to the fatal drowning.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to disconnect from gear in water
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After landing in the river and signaling he was all right, the jumper made no attempt to get out of his gear. His reserve canopy caught the swift current and pulled him under the surface, causing drowning. The failure to disconnect from the parachute system after a water landing was the direct mechanism that produced the fatal outcome.

L0_description: Carl Boenish chose to jump from a new, unfamiliar launch point rather than the one used all week. This resulted in insufficient separation from the cliff, and after a 180° off-heading opening, his canopy's known severe forward surge drove him into the wall before he could correct. The decision to jump from an unevaluated exit point and the use of an unmodified canopy with a known surge problem constitute identifiable human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to correct off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a clean 180° off-heading opening, Carl was unable to turn the canopy away from the wall before striking it. The canopy's severe forward surge upon opening (due to unmodified brake settings lacking a deep brake setting) combined with the 180° off-heading resulted in the canopy flying him into the wall before he could execute a heading correction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped unmodified canopy without DBS
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Carl refused to allow modification of his canopy with a deep brake setting or modern tail pouch before the trip, despite a known severe surge characteristic on opening. The absence of a DBS meant the canopy had excessive forward speed upon inflation, directly producing the fatal wall strike when the 180° off-heading occurred. A properly tuned DBS would have reduced forward speed and provided additional time for heading correction, which is the explicit purpose of the DBS for object-proximate jumps.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Carl's canopy was unmodified — lacking a deep brake setting and modern tail pouch — despite a known severe surge characteristic. JD Walker offered to perform these modifications before the trip, but Carl refused. This inadequate equipment configuration directly enabled the excessive forward speed on opening that produced the fatal wall strike after the 180° off-heading.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known surge problem
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple people had jumped the Goliath canopy and observed its severe surge on opening. JD Walker specifically offered to install a deep brake setting before the Norway trip. Carl's sustained refusal to address this known, recurring equipment deficiency over time constitutes a failure to correct a known problem that directly contributed to the fatal outcome.

L0_description: The jumper exited head first, went into a spin, lost control, and struck the cliff face at subterminal altitude without deploying a canopy. The head-first exit leading to an unrecoverable spin represents a human error in exit technique, resulting in impact with the object.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jorgen exited head first, went into a spin, and lost control, impacting the cliff face at subterminal altitude near the exit. The head-first exit resulted in an unrecoverable spin, indicating a technique/execution error in the exit rather than a decision error. The fatal outcome was produced by the inability to recover a stable body position and deploy the parachute.

L0_description: The narrative states only that the jumper 'lost control' and hit the cliff face, with 'unstable exit' listed as a possible factor. There is no information about the jumper's experience level, equipment details, or specific circumstances that would allow clear identification of a human error pathway versus other causes. The subterminal impact near the exit suggests a very rapid sequence of events, but the minimal narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether this resulted from a specific human error or from factors outside the jumper's control.

The narrative describes an unstable exit leading to the jumper catching her feet in lines during deployment, resulting in an off-heading opening and fatal object strike. An unstable exit from a technically difficult object with resulting line entanglement and inability to correct heading represents identifiable human error in exit execution and/or object selection.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper 'went off unstable' from a technically difficult waterfall. This unstable exit led to catching her feet in lines during deployment, producing a 180 off-heading opening and fatal object strike. The execution failure on exit was the producing mechanism for the chain of events that caused the fatality.

L0_description: The jumper launched head down, lost control, and deployed unstable from a cliff on only his first BASE jump. This represents a human error pathway: a poor exit leading to unstable deployment and subsequent object strike. Despite 2700 skydives, the jumper had no BASE experience to manage the critical exit and deployment sequence at a terminal cliff object.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper launched head down and lost control, then deployed while unstable. This execution failure — an unstable exit leading to unstable deployment — resulted in an off-heading opening and fatal object strike under canopy. Despite 2,700 skydives, this was his first BASE jump, and the physical execution of the exit was insufficient for the cliff environment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first BASE jump, attempted from Trollveggen — a terminal cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking and object avoidance skills. Accepted progression norms call for beginning BASE at simple objects (spans) and building exit and deployment skills before progressing to cliffs. A first-ever BASE jump from a terminal cliff represents a clear insufficiency in BASE experience for the task attempted, directly contributing to the unstable exit and deployment.

L0_description: The jumper used skydiving gear rather than BASE-specific equipment for a BASE jump. The domain reference establishes that BASE canopies are specifically designed for heading performance, and that skydiving gear (sail slider, skydiving container) is not appropriate for BASE applications. The use of skydiving gear with a sail slider is explicitly contraindicated in the domain reference, as sail sliders grossly reduce heading performance. This equipment choice directly contributed to the slow opening and 180-degree off-heading that resulted in landing in rapids and drowning.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient information on error type
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a slow opening and 180-degree off-heading, resulting in landing in rapids and drowning. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., the choice to use skydiving gear with a sail slider) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., poor body position causing the slow opening and off-heading, or failure to correct heading). Multiple possible factors exist but the narrative lacks detail to discriminate between them.

L0_description: The jumper used skydiving equipment for a BASE jump from a 500-foot antenna, resulting in a total malfunction (impact with nothing out). The domain reference establishes that BASE jumping requires dedicated equipment with appropriately sized pilot chutes; using skydiving gear with an inadequate pilot chute for a short-delay BASE jump constitutes a human error in equipment selection and rigging.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydiving gear for BASE jump
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used skydiving equipment rather than dedicated BASE equipment for a 500-foot antenna jump. Skydiving gear has smaller pilot chutes and deployment systems designed for terminal velocity freefall from altitude, not the low-airspeed, low-altitude conditions of a slider-down BASE jump from 500 feet. This equipment selection decision resulted in a total malfunction (impact with nothing out), as the skydiving pilot chute was inadequate to extract and deploy the canopy in the available time and airspeed.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only five or six previous BASE jumps, which is very early in BASE progression. At this stage (1986, before dedicated BASE equipment was widely established), the jumper lacked sufficient experience to recognize that skydiving equipment was inadequate for a 500-foot slider-down antenna jump. This inexperience directly contributed to the decision to use skydiving gear with an undersized pilot chute for a low-airspeed BASE application.

L0_description: The static line parted prior to extracting the canopy, resulting in a total malfunction. The cause of death is listed as malfunction/misrigging, and this was the jumper's first solo pack job with only 4 BASE jumps. The static line failure indicates a rigging error in the deployment system setup, constituting human error in equipment preparation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Static line misrigging
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The static line parted prior to extracting the canopy, resulting in a total malfunction. This was Mike's first solo pack job, and the cause of death is listed as malfunction/misrigging, indicating a rigging execution error in the static line setup that caused it to fail before canopy extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Mike had only 4 BASE jumps and this was his first solo pack job. Static line rigging is a critical skill requiring proficiency; with minimal BASE experience and no prior solo packing experience, he lacked the practiced competence to execute the rigging correctly.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate gear check
L2_preconditions.description: An experienced BASE jumper checked Mike's pack job but failed to detect the static line rigging error that caused the line to part before canopy extraction. This gear check failure allowed the misrigging to go undetected.

L0_description: The jumper used skydiving gear for a BASE jump, tied the slider down (incorrect rigging for a 6-second delay from a cliff), and had only one prior BASE jump which was itself unstable. These equipment selection and rigging decisions, combined with minimal experience, constitute clear human errors that led to the off-heading opening, line twists, and fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a history of unstable exits (head-down launch on his previous jump) and the narrative lists unstable exit as a possible factor. The canopy opened with line twists and an off-heading orientation resulting in object strike, indicating poor body position at deployment produced the fatal conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate equipment selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used a skydiving rig with a skydiving canopy (Pegasus) configured slider-down for a 6-second delay from a cliff. Skydiving equipment is not designed for BASE applications - skydiving canopies have poor heading performance compared to BASE canopies, and skydiving containers are not optimized for the deployment dynamics of BASE jumping. Using skydiving gear for a slider-down cliff jump with only one prior BASE jump represents a clearly inappropriate equipment decision that was a producing mechanism for the fatal off-heading opening and object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only one previous BASE jump, which itself was unstable. With this minimal experience, he lacked the knowledge and judgment to recognize that skydiving equipment is inappropriate for slider-down cliff jumping and to properly configure gear for a 6-second delay from a cliff. This inexperience directly contributed to the inappropriate equipment selection.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only one prior BASE jump — which was itself an unstable head-down launch — the jumper had not developed the exit skills necessary for stable cliff exits. This lack of experience and training directly contributed to the unstable exit producing the off-heading opening.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's previous BASE jump one week earlier resulted in an unstable head-down launch from the same site. Despite this direct experience of exit instability, he returned to the same site without evidence of having corrected the problem, and the same failure mode (unstable exit) contributed to the fatal outcome.

L0_description: The jumper used a skydiving pilot chute and skydiving bridle for a BASE jump despite being advised by Bridge Day Jumpmasters that this was not the correct setup. The skydiving PC failed to inflate properly (appeared inverted), which is consistent with an undersized/inappropriate PC unable to function at sub-terminal BASE airspeeds. Using skydiving deployment equipment for a BASE jump constitutes a clear equipment selection error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydiving deployment system for BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to use a skydiving pilot chute and skydiving bridle for a BASE jump despite being advised by Bridge Day Jumpmasters that this was not the appropriate setup. The skydiving PC failed to inflate (appearing inverted/mesh-side-out in video), which is consistent with the known inadequacy of skydiving PCs for BASE applications — they are too small and lack the drag characteristics needed at sub-terminal airspeeds. This equipment decision directly produced the total malfunction that led to impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: When Bridge Day Jumpmasters advised Steve that his skydiving PC/bridle setup was not appropriate, he responded 'It's always worked before,' indicating he was aware others considered it deficient but chose not to correct it based on past success. This reflects a sustained pattern of using inadequate equipment despite feedback, which directly enabled the L1 decision error.

L0_description: The narrative reports medium to strong winds at the time of the jump, and the jumper struck a seawall on final approach under canopy. The domain reference establishes that few objects are good in winds over 10 mph and none over 20 mph, and that wind conditions must be evaluated before jumping. The decision to jump in strong winds is a human error, as these conditions were observable and forecastable, and the resulting inability to reach a safe landing area is a foreseeable consequence of jumping in excessive wind.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative reports medium to strong winds at the time of the jump. The jumper died by striking a seawall on final approach under canopy, indicating wind conditions prevented a safe landing. Jumping in strong winds is a clear departure from accepted practice, as the domain reference states few objects are good in winds over 10 mph and no objects are good over 20 mph. The strong winds were the producing mechanism that made a safe landing approach impossible, resulting in the fatal seawall strike.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'over-delayed' and extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in impact at line stretch. This is a human error in deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper over-delayed freefall and deployed the pilot chute too late, resulting in impact with the talus at line stretch. The jumper failed to deploy at the correct altitude, indicating an execution/attention failure in monitoring altitude or timing rather than a deliberate decision to pull at an inadequate altitude.

L0_description: The jumper used skydiving gear (a skydiving reserve canopy, the Vega 180, packed in a deployment bag) for a BASE jump from a cliff. The cause of death is explicitly listed as 'Skydive Gear Used,' and the domain reference establishes that BASE-specific equipment is required for BASE jumping due to critical differences in heading performance, opening characteristics, and deployment configuration. Using a skydiving reserve canopy for a cliff jump represents a human error in equipment selection that contributed to the 180° off-heading, line twists, wall strike, and fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Skydive gear for BASE jump
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jean-Marc used a skydiving reserve canopy (Vega 180) packed in a deployment bag for a BASE jump from a cliff (Angel Falls). Using skydive gear for BASE jumping is a clearly inappropriate equipment decision: skydiving reserve canopies lack the heading performance characteristics of BASE-specific canopies, and deployment bag configurations at sub-terminal or low airspeeds yield poor heading performance. The cause of death is explicitly attributed to 'Skydive Gear Used,' and the resulting 180 off-heading with line twists led to wall strike and fatal injuries.

L0_label: Premature PC inflation from improper technique
L0_description: The jumper exposed too much of the pilot chute (top plus several inches of mesh) while hand-held, causing it to partially inflate before release. This tipped him backwards and torqued him, producing line twists he could not clear before striking the wall. The improper PC hold technique resulted from a misunderstanding of advice about PC exposure, constituting a human error in deployment execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Improper PC hold technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David misunderstood advice about hand-held PC technique and exposed several inches of mesh in addition to the top of the pilot chute. This caused the PC to partially inflate before release, which tipped him backwards and torqued him during opening, producing multiple line twists he could not clear before striking the wall. The narrative explicitly states he misunderstood the instruction — he intended to hold the PC correctly for faster inflation but executed the technique incorrectly by exposing too much mesh.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states David misunderstood a highly experienced jumper's written advice about hand-held PC technique, exposing mesh in addition to the PC top. This knowledge gap — confusing the correct amount of PC to expose — directly produced the improper hold that caused premature partial inflation. The misunderstanding of correspondence indicates he lacked the hands-on training or mentorship that would have corrected this specific technique error.

L0_label: Off-heading with line twists into building
L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening with line twists from a PCA deployment on a building, resulting in object strike. The narrative identifies contributing factors including line twists preventing heading correction, a toggle that released during cable strike (due to a non-standard tape wrap modification), and the absence of a helmet. The combination of inadequate toggle securing method and lack of protective equipment reflects human error in equipment configuration and preparation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Off-heading with line twists cause unclear
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening with line twists from a PCA deployment on a building, resulting in object strike. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the off-heading and line twists resulted from a decision error (e.g., inadequate planning) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., poor body position at deployment, packing error). The root cause of the off-heading opening cannot be determined from the available information.

L0_description: The narrative reports associates' belief that the bridle became lodged under the container corner causing a PC in tow, but the account is speculative ('associates believe'), key details are uncertain (nationality, exact span, experience level), and there is insufficient information to determine whether this resulted from a human error (e.g., improper bridle routing by the jumper) or an equipment design deficiency independent of user error. The entry itself notes this event prompted manufacturers to address bridle routing, suggesting a possible design-level issue, but the evidence is too ambiguous to classify definitively as either human error or other cause.

The jumper used a skydiving container (Wonderhog) for a BASE jump rather than a purpose-built BASE container. The narrative indicates the main closing pin was likely over-tensioned by tightening the belly band after the final gear check, and this combined with an early/weak pilot chute throw at low airspeed resulted in a pilot chute in tow total malfunction. Using skydiving equipment with a curved pin system not designed for BASE deployment conditions constitutes a clear human error in equipment selection and rigging.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydiving container for BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to use a skydiving rig (Wonderhog) with a curved pin closure system for a BASE jump instead of a purpose-built BASE container. The skydiving container's curved pin closure was susceptible to pin lock under tension, and this equipment choice was the producing mechanism for the fatal pilot chute in tow. The narrative explicitly notes both jumpers had discussed purchasing proper BASE containers but declined due to cost, establishing this was a conscious equipment decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Belly band tightened after gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative speculates that Robert tightened his belly band after the final gear check performed by the first jumper. This post-check modification increased pin tension on the curved-pin closure system, directly contributing to the pin lock that caused the pilot chute in tow. The gear check that had been performed could not catch a problem introduced after it was completed.

L0_description: The jumper exited very head-down, causing the canopy to deploy between his legs and open 180 degrees off-heading, resulting in a surge into the building. He had been repeatedly advised to correct his head-down exits after three prior jumps with the same problem. Additionally, he jumped a building described as being for more experienced jumpers, went without his mentor, used a borrowed skydiving rig with a modified canopy, and carried a prop (box of chocolates) — all indicating multiple human errors in exit technique, progression decisions, and equipment selection.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Head-down exit causing 180 off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Darren exited very head-down, causing the canopy to deploy between his legs and open 180 degrees off-heading, surging into the building. He had been advised multiple times to correct his exits as his previous three jumps were all head-down, indicating a persistent technique error in exit body position. The head-down exit directly produced the off-heading opening and fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Darren had been advised multiple times to correct his head-down exits, and his previous three jumps all exhibited the same head-down exit problem. Despite this recurring issue and explicit feedback, he failed to take corrective action before attempting a more demanding object.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience for object
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the hotel was 'for the more experienced,' and Darren had only a few prior BASE jumps with a persistent unresolved exit technique problem. He went without his mentor and had not yet demonstrated basic exit proficiency, indicating insufficient experience for this building jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment for the jump
L2_preconditions.description: Darren used a modified skydiving canopy (Pursuit 230) in a borrowed skydiving rig rather than purpose-built BASE equipment. A skydiving rig and modified canopy may have contributed to the severity of the surge and opening characteristics following the off-heading deployment.

L0_description: The narrative provides a secondhand account suggesting line twists combined with turbulent anabatic/waterfall-generated airflow caused repeated object strikes. However, the line twists are speculative ('the jumper's evaluation was'), there is no direct observation confirming line twists occurred, and the actual cause of the canopy repeatedly flying into the cliff is unclear. It cannot be determined whether this was an unforeseeable environmental event (002OC) or a human error in site/conditions assessment (001HE), as the severity of the turbulent conditions relative to what could reasonably have been anticipated is unknown.

The fatality resulted from a premature deployment caused by the pilot chute sliding from its BOC pouch during freefall. The jumper was using a brand-new container system with a BOC configuration he had never used before, and the pouch was reportedly too large for the pilot chute, allowing fabric pulled out during pin checks to catch air. Using an unfamiliar container system without verifying proper PC/pouch fit constitutes a human error in equipment setup and pre-jump verification.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature PC deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jonathan's pilot chute inadvertently deployed from the BOC pouch during freefall tracking, causing a premature opening. He had never used this BOC style before, and the pouch was reportedly too large for the pilot chute, allowing fabric to catch air. The premature deployment was an execution failure - the PC was not adequately secured, and his unfamiliarity with the system contributed to improper stowage (pulling fabric out of the pouch before exit).

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Jonathan used a brand-new container system with a BOC pouch that was reportedly too large for his 38" pilot chute, and he had never used this BOC style before. The pouch-to-PC size mismatch created the condition for the PC to inadvertently extract during freefall. Additionally, he pulled fabric out of the pouch during pin checks to have 'plenty to grab,' further compromising the stowage security. This inadequate equipment configuration directly enabled the premature deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Unmodified canopy for slider-down
L2_preconditions.description: The PD 230 was a brand-new, unmodified skydiving canopy jumped without the standard BASE modifications discussed (deep brake setting, tail pouch with locking stow, relocated steering lines). Without a deep brake setting, the canopy surged violently upon premature opening, directly contributing to the collision and fatal outcome. While the premature deployment was the primary failure, the unmodified canopy's violent surge behavior amplified the consequences.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening and failed to adequately respond with heading correction, resulting in object strike under canopy. The possible factors explicitly identify insufficient forward separation on exit and the 180° off-heading. The failure to correct heading after an off-heading opening near a cliff wall constitutes identifiable human error in the deployment and canopy control sequence.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate off-heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening and failed to adequately respond with heading correction, resulting in object strike under canopy. The narrative explicitly states he 'failed to adequately respond' to the off-heading, indicating a performance/execution failure in the critical task of heading correction after opening.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient forward separation on exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The possible factors identify 'not enough forward separation on exit' as a contributing factor. Insufficient push or exit technique resulted in inadequate object separation, reducing the time and distance available to survive the subsequent off-heading opening. This is a technique/execution error in performing the exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Joe was so nervous about the jump that friends later remarked about it. This level of nervousness, notable enough for others to comment on, suggests the jumper may have been exceeding their readiness for this specific jump, and proceeding despite this state likely degraded exit and response performance.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Joe 'failed to adequately respond' to the off-heading opening, suggesting a possible freeze or inadequate reaction under the stress of an unexpected 180° off-heading facing the wall. His notably high pre-jump nervousness may have compounded this inability to execute a timely correction.

L0_label: Altitude awareness loss and insufficient training
L0_description: The jumper held a head-high attitude for too long, lost altitude awareness, and impacted the wall in terminal freefall without deploying. The narrative explicitly identifies altitude awareness failure and insufficient training/experience as contributing factors, and notes the jumper was given a jump beyond her abilities.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of altitude awareness
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper held a head-high attitude for too long during freefall from El Capitan, lost altitude awareness, and backed into the wall in her 15th second of freefall. The head-high body position and failure to monitor altitude and proximity to the cliff constituted a performance/execution error that directly produced the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states this fatality 'began the process of insisting people who begin BASE jumping have at least 150 previous parachute jumps,' and references 'people without much experience being handed BASE jumps beyond their abilities.' This directly indicates the jumper lacked sufficient skydiving experience to manage the freefall body position and altitude awareness demands of a terminal slider-up cliff jump from El Capitan.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly identifies the issue of 'people without much experience being handed BASE jumps beyond their abilities.' El Capitan is a terminal slider-up cliff requiring competent sub-terminal/terminal tracking, altitude awareness, and body position management. The jumper's inability to maintain proper body position and altitude awareness during a 15-second delay indicates experience insufficient for this object.

L2_preconditions.label: Loss of Altitude Awareness
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'lost altitude awareness' while holding a head-high attitude. This failure to maintain situational awareness of proximity to the wall during freefall directly enabled the skill-based error of backing into the cliff.

L0_description: The narrative provides conflicting accounts of the fatality. It is unclear whether the jumper fell before setting up for the jump (a pre-jump slip/fall from the building without the rig on) or attempted a jump with a tied-shut rig. Key evidence conflicts: one account says the rig was found on the body tied shut, another says the rig was found at the top of the building. The narrative itself states 'It's inconclusive today whether Jason fell with his rig on and tied shut or without it on.' Without resolving this fundamental ambiguity, neither a specific human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be reliably determined.

The jumper used skydiving equipment for a BASE jump from an antenna, which is explicitly identified as the cause of death. The skydive gear sniveled to impact - skydiving equipment is not configured for the rapid deployments required in BASE jumping. There is no evidence of BASE training or experience, indicating fundamental decision errors in attempting a BASE jump without proper equipment or preparation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydive gear for BASE jump
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used skydiving equipment for a BASE jump from an antenna. Skydiving gear is designed for terminal-velocity deployments with thousands of feet of altitude margin, not for the sub-terminal airspeeds and minimal altitude of BASE jumping. The slider-up skydiving canopy sniveled (prolonged opening sequence) at sub-terminal airspeed, consuming all available altitude and resulting in impact. This was a fundamental equipment selection decision error that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted BASE with no training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had no apparent BASE training, no BASE experience, and held only an expired USPA A license (the lowest skydiving certification). Attempting a BASE jump from an antenna without a First Jump Course, mentor guidance, or any understanding of BASE-specific equipment, packing, and deployment requirements represents a decision that was clearly and specifically inadequate. This decision directly enabled the use of inappropriate equipment and configuration.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper held only an expired USPA A license, the lowest skydiving certification, indicating minimal skydiving experience far below the accepted 200+ skydive minimum for beginning BASE. This insufficient foundation meant the jumper lacked the knowledge to understand why skydiving equipment is inappropriate for BASE jumping and why BASE-specific training is required.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states there is nothing to suggest any previous BASE experience or training. The jumper attempted a BASE jump without a First Jump Course, mentor guidance, or any BASE-specific education, which would have taught that skydiving gear cannot be used for BASE and that BASE requires specialized equipment, packing, and deployment configurations.

L0_description: The narrative describes a spinning main canopy malfunction followed by a cutaway and reserve deployment with line twists, but provides no information about the jumper's experience level, packing practices, body position, or the specific cause of the initial malfunction. Without details on what caused the spinning main or whether any human error contributed to the malfunction sequence, neither 001HE nor 002OC can be adequately supported.

L0_label: Inexperienced jumper, inadequate equipment and technique
L0_description: The jumper was an experienced skydiver but had no BASE jumping experience, making his first BASE jump after joining a trip as ground crew. He used an unmodified square canopy without deep brake settings on a sheer cliff that required freefall and ideally a round canopy. The combination of jumping without proper BASE training/experience, using inappropriate equipment configuration for the object, and the resulting 180° off-heading with line twists leading to object strike constitutes clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate equipment and technique for object
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used an unmodified square canopy with no deep brake settings for a sheer cliff that was best suited for freefall delay and a round canopy. A square canopy without DBS produces significant forward speed on opening, and on a sheer wall this directly creates the pendulum/object strike failure mode. The equipment and technique selection was clearly inappropriate for the object's characteristics.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was an experienced skydiver who came along as ground crew and only decided to make a BASE jump later in the week. There is no indication of prior BASE experience, a first jump course, or mentorship. This lack of BASE-specific training and experience directly contributed to the inappropriate equipment and technique selection—an unmodified square canopy with no DBS on a sheer cliff requiring freefall delay and a round canopy.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper used an unmodified square canopy with no deep brake setting or other modifications appropriate for a slider-down cliff jump. Proper pre-jump preparation requires configuring the canopy with DBS to minimize forward speed on opening near a sheer wall. This configuration failure directly enabled the object strike.

L0_description: The narrative indicates a rocket/parachute system attached to a life jacket failed to deploy during a jet ski stunt over Niagara Falls, with 'Gear failure' listed as a possible factor. However, there is insufficient information to determine whether the deployment failure resulted from human error (improper rigging, packing, or equipment selection) or from an equipment malfunction independent of human error. The jumper's experience level, equipment details, and specific failure mechanism are not reported.

The jumper stowed the pilot chute in the BOC pouch during a static line jump, contrary to accepted practice which states the PC must never be stowed during static line jumps. This incorrect rigging caused the break cord to part prematurely at a right angle before extracting the canopy, resulting in a total malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Stowed PC on static line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided at the last moment to stow the pilot chute in the BOC pouch instead of leaving it out as required for a static line deployment. This decision directly caused the malfunction: when the static line became taut, it applied force at a right angle to the stowed PC, causing the break cord to part prematurely before extracting the PC and bridle, resulting in a total malfunction. The domain reference explicitly states that the PC must never be stowed during a static line jump.

L0_label: Canopy control error in tight landing
L0_description: The jumper flew himself into a corner in the canyon landing area and performed a panic hook turn into the canyon wall. With only 30 skydives, the jumper was severely under the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, indicating grossly inadequate canopy piloting skills for the tight landing area. The fatal outcome resulted from identifiable human errors in canopy control and decision-making.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Canopy control error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Don flew himself into a corner after the bridge jump and panic-hooked into the canyon wall, resulting in fatal head trauma. The fatal outcome was produced by inadequate canopy piloting execution in the tight landing area — specifically a panic hook turn into the canyon wall rather than a controlled approach.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Don had only 30 skydives, far below the accepted minimum of 200-250 before beginning BASE. This grossly insufficient skydiving experience means he lacked the foundational canopy piloting skills needed to manage a tight landing area, directly contributing to the panic hook turn into the canyon wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Don was 'an idiot when it came to assessing risks,' supported by a pattern of behavior including forging a logbook for an untrained friend, taking that friend on a two-way skydive that 'almost killed them both,' and subsequently taking the same friend off a tower. This pattern of grossly overestimating his own capability and disregarding risk directly contributed to attempting a jump at a site with a tight landing area that exceeded his canopy piloting ability.

L0_description: The jumper prioritized closing the gap with his filming partner rather than maintaining adequate forward separation from the cliff wall. This decision resulted in insufficient object separation at deployment time, leaving him only ~2 seconds from impact when he pitched, leading to a fatal wall strike during deployment. The narrative explicitly identifies focusing on filming and not enough separation from the object as contributing factors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation during freefall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Sébastien was tracking on his back to film the second jumper. Instead of prioritizing forward separation from the cliff, both jumpers focused on closing the gap between them for video purposes. This resulted in inadequate separation from the wall, leaving them approximately 2 seconds from impact when it was time to separate and deploy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment due to task saturation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: By the time Sébastien recognized the insufficient separation and attempted to deploy, he was too close to the wall. He pitched immediately upon realizing the situation but impacted the wall during deployment, indicating the deployment was initiated too late to achieve a survivable outcome. The filming task degraded his monitoring of separation and altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Sébastien was tracking on his back to film the second jumper and focused on closing the gap between them for video purposes. This channelized attention on the filming task diverted his awareness from monitoring object separation and terrain proximity, directly contributing to insufficient separation from the wall and late recognition of the need to deploy.

L0_description: The narrative provides conflicting witness accounts - some describe an unstable exit and deployment leading to wall strike, while others speculate the harness was connected backwards. A rigger's investigation was prevented by Park Service officials, so the actual cause was never determined. There is insufficient information to definitively identify a human error pathway or confirm a non-human cause.

The canopy deployed successfully ('perfectly deployed'), yet the jumper died from object strike under canopy. The narrative provides no information about what caused the object strike — whether it was an off-heading opening, navigational error in night conditions, canopy control failure, or another factor. While several speculative possibilities exist (night visibility, unfamiliar canopy characteristics, equipment choice), the narrative does not contain sufficient detail to clearly identify a human error pathway or to positively establish a non-human cause.

The narrative reports a 180 off-heading opening from a 300-metre cliff resulting in object strike. However, there is insufficient information about the jumper's experience level, equipment configuration details, packing practices, site evaluation, or specific conditions to determine whether a human error pathway caused the off-heading, or whether this resulted from factors outside reasonable control. The companion also experienced an off-heading on the same jump, which could suggest environmental or site factors, but this is speculative. Neither 001HE nor 002OC can be adequately supported.

The jumper had an unstable exit and took 12 seconds to recover stability before deploying, resulting in impact at 12.7 seconds. Deploying too late after failing to prioritize pulling over stability recovery is a clear human error. Additionally, the jumper reportedly refused offered instruction prior to the jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with late recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had an unstable launch and took 12 seconds to recover stability before deploying the pilot chute, with impact occurring at 12.7 seconds. The execution failure was the unstable exit combined with the failure to deploy the parachute despite instability — the jumper prioritized regaining stability over deploying, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to open. Regardless of the exit quality, deploying earlier (even from an unstable position) was the critical execution failure that produced the fatal low pull.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Stability
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper spent 12 seconds attempting to recover stability before deploying, indicating channelized attention on regaining a stable body position to the exclusion of altitude awareness. This fixation on stability recovery directly delayed the deployment that produced the fatal low pull.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports the jumper was offered instruction prior to the jump and refused it. The failure to deploy despite prolonged instability — prioritizing stability over pulling — reflects a knowledge gap regarding the fundamental BASE principle that deployment must occur regardless of body position when altitude is critical. Accepted BASE training explicitly teaches that deploying unstable is preferable to impact.

L0_description: The jumper struck the cliff under canopy after opening near an underhung wall section with insufficient forward separation. The narrative identifies multiple human error pathways: the jumper had been repeatedly advised to apply deeper brake settings to a non-BASE-specific canopy to reduce surge/drive, and had been repeatedly told to be more responsive during the opening sequence. The possible factors include insufficient forward separation on exit and possible line twists. Whether the primary cause was inadequate exit separation from a wall that becomes underhung, failure to configure appropriate brake settings on a non-BASE canopy, or delayed canopy input, human error contributed to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to execute evasive maneuvers
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to apply immediate heading correction or evasive maneuvers after canopy opening near the cliff face. The original eyewitness account states he had ample room and time to employ commonly practiced evasive maneuvers but allowed his canopy to fly into the cliff. Even accounting for the update suggesting limited time due to the underhung wall geometry, the jumper had been repeatedly advised to be more responsive during the canopy opening sequence, indicating a pattern of inadequate execution of post-opening canopy control skills.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate brake setting on non-BASE canopy
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was repeatedly advised to apply deeper brake settings to his non-BASE specific canopy to avoid its excessive surge and drive, but did not do so. Using a canopy with excessive forward surge without appropriate deep brake settings at a cliff where the wall becomes underhung and openings occur directly beside the wall was a decision that directly contributed to the canopy flying into the cliff rather than flying slowly enough to allow heading correction.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known brake setting deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been repeatedly advised by other jumpers to apply deeper brake settings to his non-BASE specific canopy to avoid its excessive surge and drive. This represents a sustained pattern of failing to correct a known equipment configuration problem despite repeated feedback from peers.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known canopy response deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been repeatedly told by other jumpers to be more responsive during the canopy opening sequence. This indicates a recurring performance issue that the jumper was aware of through direct feedback but failed to correct over time, directly contributing to the failure to execute evasive maneuvers.

L0_description: The jumper used a skydiving rig not modified for BASE jumping, which included a bungee-controlled collapsible pilot chute unsuitable for low-airspeed BASE deployments. He had no prior BASE training or experience. These equipment selection and preparation errors directly caused the fatal total malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydiving gear for BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used an unmodified skydiving rig for a BASE jump. A skydiving collapsible pilot chute with bungee retraction is designed for terminal airspeeds and is known to be ineffective at the sub-terminal airspeeds of a BASE jump from a 364-foot antenna. This equipment selection decision directly produced the total malfunction that caused the fatality.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had no prior BASE training or experience. Accepted BASE education covers the critical differences between skydiving and BASE equipment, including the unsuitability of collapsible pilot chutes at sub-terminal airspeeds. This knowledge gap directly enabled the decision to use an unmodified skydiving rig.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states alcohol may have been a contributing factor. While qualified as possible, this is explicitly noted in the report as a potential contributor to the jumper's impaired judgment in deciding to use skydiving gear for a BASE jump.

L0_description: The narrative and structured fields indicate the jumper extracted the pilot chute too late (over-delay) from a 1249-foot antenna, resulting in a fatally low pull. Delaying deployment beyond the altitude required for safe canopy inflation is a human error in judgment or execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper delayed too long before extracting the pilot chute from the BOC, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to fully deploy. The possible factors note 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late' and the cause of death is listed as 'low pull,' indicating the jumper failed to deploy at the correct altitude despite presumably intending to do so.

L0_description: The jumper jumped into a fog bank (low-level clouds), which resulted in loss of spatial orientation under canopy and fatal object strike. Jumping into conditions with obscured visibility is a decision within the jumper's control; low-level clouds were observable before exit, making the decision to jump into them a human error rather than an unforeseeable event.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flight into obscured conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper jumped into a fog bank (low level clouds), which eliminated visual references needed for canopy navigation near a cliff. Flying into conditions with no visibility near terrain is a clearly inappropriate decision, as it removed the ability to maintain object separation. The cause of death was object strike under canopy after reportedly losing bearings in the fog.

L2_preconditions.label: Obscured visibility from fog
L2_preconditions.description: Low level clouds/fog bank eliminated visual references during canopy flight near the cliff face. This directly impaired the jumper's ability to perceive terrain proximity and maintain object separation, resulting in object strike under canopy.

L0_label: Unstable exit leading to object strike
L0_description: The narrative explicitly describes an unstable head-down launch followed by insufficient separation from the object, resulting in the jumper striking the wall at eight seconds. The unstable exit is a human performance failure that directly led to inadequate object separation and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had an unstable head-down launch from the cliff. This unstable exit compromised his ability to establish an effective track away from the wall, resulting in insufficient object separation. He struck the wall at eight seconds, leading to canopy deflation and fatal impact.

L0_description: The narrative provides minimal technical detail about the jump. The 'Possible Factors' field lists extracted pilot chute too late and insufficient forward separation, but these are speculative attributions without supporting evidence in the narrative. There is no information about the jumper's experience level, equipment configuration details, the specific exit point characteristics, or what occurred during freefall and deployment. The cause of death (object strike under canopy) is established, but the chain of events leading to it cannot be reliably determined from the available information.

The narrative and possible factors indicate the jumper clipped a ledge after exiting, did not achieve enough forward separation, and extracted the pilot chute too late. These factors point to human errors in exit technique and deployment timing that resulted in insufficient object separation, leading to fatal wall impact. The jumper also was not wearing a helmet, though the primary cause was impact in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff on exit, as indicated by the possible factors noting clipped ledge after exiting and not enough forward separation on exit. This insufficient separation, combined with late pilot chute extraction, resulted in the canopy opening with the jumper too close to the wall, leading to fatal wall impact with no time or distance for heading correction.

L0_description: The jumper used a system with a leg-mounted pilot chute pouch but instinctively reached for the BOC location she was accustomed to. After failing to locate the pilot chute handle, she ran out of time to deploy, resulting in impact without canopy extraction. This represents a human error in both the decision to jump an unfamiliar deployment system and the failure to execute the correct deployment procedure.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a normal delay, the jumper instinctively reached for the BOC position she was accustomed to rather than the leg-mounted pilot chute pouch on the system she was actually jumping. She failed to locate the pilot chute handle and ran out of time before correcting, resulting in a no-pull impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper instinctively reached for the BOC position she was accustomed to, indicating her habitual motor pattern dominated her attention. This channelized focus on the familiar BOC location prevented her from redirecting to the actual leg-mounted pouch location before running out of altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment familiarization
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper switched to a system with an older-style leg-mounted pilot chute pouch that she was not accustomed to jumping, having habitually used a BOC pouch system. Adequate pre-jump preparation for an unfamiliar deployment system would include practice touches and deliberate rehearsal of the different pull location to override habitual motor patterns.

L0_description: The jumper's companions explicitly warned him that conditions were unsafe: too dark, wrong wind, and water too high. Despite these warnings, the jumper elected to jump, landed in moving water he could not escape, and drowned. The decision to jump in clearly identified adverse conditions constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in clearly adverse conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's companions explicitly warned him that it was too dark, the wind was wrong, and the water was too high, yet he elected to jump. This was a conscious decision to proceed despite multiple clearly identified hazards (night, adverse wind, high water) that collectively made the jump inappropriate. The decision to jump in these conditions — particularly the high, moving water that directly caused his drowning — was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Improper slider configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The updated narrative indicates Roger was packed slider-up after jumping the higher Perrine Bridge all weekend, and it is unknown whether staying slider-up for the significantly lower Hansen Bridge (350 ft) was intentional. Slider-up configuration on a 350 ft bridge would produce a slower, higher opening consuming more altitude, directly reducing canopy flight time and glide available to reach the landing zone — contributing to his inability to make the planned LZ and landing in moving water instead.

L0_description: The jumper used a skydiving rig (Racer container with deployment bag, 30-inch F111 pilot chute, and 4-foot bridle) rather than proper BASE equipment. The domain reference establishes that BASE jumping requires specific equipment configurations—longer bridles (~9 feet), appropriately sized pilot chutes, and BASE-specific containers. The short bridle and small pilot chute were insufficient to extract the canopy from the deployment bag at sub-terminal/low airspeeds, resulting in a closed-container or near-closed-container impact with the canopy only at line stretch and slider still at the top of the lines. These were human errors in equipment selection and rigging.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate equipment selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Trevor used a skydiving container (Racer) with a skydiving-style deployment system: a 30-inch F-111 pilot chute, a 4-foot bridle, a deployment bag, and a sail slider. This equipment was fundamentally inappropriate for BASE jumping. The 30-inch F-111 PC was grossly undersized for a slider-up BASE deployment, the 4-foot bridle was far too short to clear the burble (BASE standard is 9 feet), and the sail slider would have severely degraded heading performance and produced dangerously slow openings. The combination of these equipment choices was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome — the canopy was found at line stretch with the slider still at the top of the lines, indicating the system never completed deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that Trevor 'was caught in a time warp as far as BASE equipment is concerned,' indicating he lacked current knowledge of proper BASE equipment requirements. His use of a skydiving container with a grossly undersized PC, short bridle, deployment bag, and sail slider reflects a fundamental knowledge gap about BASE-specific equipment configuration that accepted BASE education would have corrected.

L0_description: The jumper over-delayed while flying in a back-to-earth position filming other jumpers, resulting in a low pull and ground impact during canopy opening. The back-to-earth position is a known cause of altitude awareness loss, and focusing on filming other jumpers rather than monitoring altitude constitutes human error. The domain reference explicitly identifies loss of altitude awareness as a serious failure mode for back-to-earth exits and identifies video as a psychological distraction and risk factor.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment due to altitude awareness loss
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Andrea over-delayed while flying in a back-to-earth position filming two other jumpers, failing to deploy his pilot chute at a survivable altitude. He impacted the talus as his canopy was still opening, indicating the deployment was initiated too late. The back-to-earth position is a known cause of loss of altitude awareness, and the filming task divided his attention from monitoring altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Andrea was filming two other jumpers during freefall, which diverted his attention from monitoring altitude. The narrative explicitly identifies 'focusing on filming' as a possible factor and notes this is the second BASE fatality involving flying back-to-earth with a camera, indicating the filming task channelized his attention away from altitude awareness.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Andrea was flying in a back-to-earth position to film the other jumpers. This orientation deprived him of visual ground references needed to assess altitude, contributing to vestibular and visual spatial disorientation that directly enabled the over-delay.

L0_description: The jumper had only 8 BASE jumps and the video shows trouble with directional control and tracking, resulting in a 180-degree off-heading opening that caused fatal object strike. With only 8 BASE jumps, the jumper lacked the experience and skill to manage tracking and deployment at a slider-up cliff object, representing inadequate progression. The poor tracking and resulting off-heading deployment constitute identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor tracking and deployment technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Video evidence shows Terry having trouble with directional control and tracking during freefall. He deployed with a 180° off-heading, resulting in his canopy flying him directly into the cliff wall. With only 8 BASE jumps, his sub-terminal tracking and body position at deployment were insufficient to achieve adequate object separation and on-heading opening, constituting a performance/skill-based error in execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Terry had only 8 BASE jumps total and was attempting a terminal slider-up cliff jump requiring sub-terminal tracking and reliable deployment technique. Accepted progression norms require extensive experience at simpler objects before progressing to slider-up cliffs, and video evidence confirms his tracking and directional control skills were inadequate for this object. His experience level was clearly insufficient for the task attempted.

L0_description: The jumper launched unstable and never recovered, impacting in terminal freefall. The narrative explicitly states the jumper refused offered instruction prior to the jump. The unstable exit without recovery and refusal of instruction constitute clear human errors contributing to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit no recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper launched unstable and remained unstable throughout freefall until impact. This is a performance/execution failure: the jumper failed to recover a stable body position and failed to deploy the parachute despite having terminal freefall time available (slider-up cliff, impact at terminal velocity). The inability to stabilize and/or deploy constitutes a skill-based execution error that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper was offered instruction prior to the jump and refused it. This indicates the jumper lacked the training and mentorship that accepted practice requires, which directly contributed to the inability to recover from an unstable exit or deploy the parachute during freefall.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper refused offered instruction before attempting a terminal slider-up cliff jump. Refusing instruction for a task that clearly exceeded the jumper's demonstrated capability (as evidenced by the inability to recover from instability or deploy) indicates the jumper unreasonably overestimated their own ability to manage the jump without guidance.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly describes poor conditions: rain, strong gusty crosswinds, and katabatic airflow. The group recognized conditions were 'pretty bad' and 'not good' but decided to jump anyway. Jorge deployed higher than the others, placing himself outside the wind shadow and in the turbulent zone near the wall, resulting in canopy collapse and fatal object strike. The decision to jump in clearly adverse conditions and the deployment altitude choice constitute identifiable human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in clearly unsuitable weather
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly describes conditions as 'pretty bad' with rain, strong and gusty winds, and crosswinds from the right. The group acknowledged conditions were 'better, but not good' and that everyone would have left if anyone had suggested it. The jumper chose to jump in conditions that were clearly inadequate for safe cliff jumping - strong gusty crosswinds, rain, and turbulence near the wall. These conditions directly produced the fatal outcome: the crosswind pushed the jumper into the wall corner despite the group's plan to fly in the wind shadow.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Deployed too high above wind shadow
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The group agreed on a plan to deploy low enough to remain in the wind shadow of the wall but high enough to handle heading problems. Jorge deployed his canopy higher than all the other jumpers, placing himself above the protective wind shadow and into the turbulent crosswind zone near the wall. This decision directly resulted in his canopy being exposed to the strong crosswinds and turbulence, causing him to fly parallel to the wall until impacting the corner.

L0_description: The jumper had no previous BASE jumps, no training, no mentor, and no experienced jumper present. He selected an under-hung (positive slope) exit point, executed an unstable exit that put him on his back, and struck the cliff in freefall. The complete absence of training, progression, and mentorship constitutes clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping with no training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had no previous BASE jumps, no training, no mentor, and no experienced jumper present. He purchased BASE gear and attempted a BASE jump from a sub-optimal exit point with zero preparation. This represents a clear and specific decision error — attempting a BASE jump without any of the foundational skills, training, or mentorship required, which directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper pivoted off the launch, went on his back, and struck the cliff in freefall. The execution of the exit was fatally flawed — he failed to achieve any stable body position or forward separation from the cliff, directly resulting in object strike at sub-terminal altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: No skydiving or BASE training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had no previous BASE jumps, no training, and no experienced jumper present. Without any skydiving or BASE training, he lacked the fundamental skills for exit technique, body position control, and object separation, directly contributing to the unstable exit and cliff strike.

L2_preconditions.label: No skydiving or BASE training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had zero BASE jumps, no first jump course, no mentor, and no experienced companion. This complete absence of training and education meant he lacked the knowledge framework to recognize that attempting a BASE jump without foundational skills was a fatal-level decision error.

L0_description: The jumper converted from slider-down to slider-up configuration but neglected to route the steering lines back through the slider grommets. This misrigging caused the canopy to open in a turn, resulting in object strike and fatal injuries. The rigging error is a clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Rigging omission during conversion
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper converted from slider-down to slider-up configuration but neglected to route the steering lines back through the slider grommets. This omission is a memory/procedural execution failure — he knew the correct procedure (having packed slider-up configurations before) but failed to complete a necessary step during the last-minute conversion. The misrouted steering lines caused the canopy to open in a turn, resulting in object strike before full inflation.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform gear check after conversion
L2_preconditions.description: After converting from slider-down to slider-up configuration at the last minute, the jumper did not perform a gear check that would have caught the misrouted steering lines. A systematic inspection of the rigging after the conversion — tracing brake lines through slider grommets as accepted practice requires — would have detected the omission before the jump.

L0_description: The jumper exited on his back and took too much time to recover to a belly-to-earth position facing away from the wall. He attempted a quarter turn and deployment but impacted before the canopy could fully deploy. The back-to-earth exit without sufficient altitude margin, combined with inadequate object separation and delayed recovery, constitutes human error in exit technique and deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Back-to-earth exit at cliff
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Thierry chose to exit on his back from a cliff with only ~6 seconds of pure vertical. A back-to-earth exit is an aerobatic maneuver that inherently costs time and altitude to recover to a belly-to-earth orientation, and the domain reference explicitly identifies loss of altitude awareness and delayed deployment as known failure modes of back-to-earth exits. At a cliff requiring mandatory tracking for separation, this decision consumed the altitude and time needed for recovery, tracking, and deployment. The execution proceeded as intended (he exited on his back as planned), but the decision itself was the producing mechanism — choosing an aerobatic exit from a cliff with limited vertical relief and insufficient margin for the recovery time required.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate object separation and delayed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the back-to-earth exit, Thierry took too long to recover to a belly-to-earth position facing away from the wall. He failed to achieve adequate forward separation through tracking before needing to deploy. His attempted quarter-turn and deployment came too late, resulting in ground impact before canopy deployment was complete. The execution of the recovery and tracking was insufficient for the altitude available.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses attested that Thierry was 'really very tired' after completing ten jumps in the valley over the two days prior to his fatal jump. This fatigue likely degraded his judgment when deciding to perform an aerobatic back-to-earth exit from a cliff with limited vertical relief.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: Thierry had only ~100 skydives, which is below the accepted minimum of 200-250 before beginning BASE jumping. With 240 BASE jumps but a limited skydiving foundation, his core freefall body control skills — particularly recovery from non-standard orientations — may have been insufficient, directly contributing to his slow recovery from the back-to-earth exit position.

L0_description: The jumper had only 3 BASE jumps and experienced an unstable exit with insufficient forward separation from a slider-up cliff object, resulting in object strike under canopy. With only 2-3 BASE jumps, jumping a slider-up cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking and object avoidance skills represents a clear progression violation. The unstable exit and inadequate separation indicate human error in both the decision to jump this object and execution of the exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had an unstable short delay from a slider-up cliff object, resulting in insufficient forward separation. With only 3 BASE jumps total (second or third jump), she lacked the sub-terminal tracking skill and exit technique needed to achieve adequate object separation from a slider-up cliff, leading to a wall strike under canopy.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 3 total BASE jumps and was attempting a slider-up cliff jump. Accepted progression norms establish that slider-up cliffs require sub-terminal tracking skill, refined exit technique, and object avoidance skills developed over many jumps at simpler objects. With only 2-3 BASE jumps, she lacked the experience necessary to execute a stable exit with adequate separation from a cliff object.

L0_description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall with the parachute never opening. The narrative states 'the parachute did not open' and lists 'unstable exit' as a possible factor, but provides no details about the jumper's experience level, equipment condition, what specifically failed in the deployment sequence, or whether the jumper attempted deployment at all. There is insufficient information to determine whether this was a human error (e.g., failure to deploy, unstable exit leading to entanglement) or an equipment malfunction independent of human error.

The jumper slipped on a wet launch ramp, lost control of freefall, deployed on his back, and struck a tree-filled outcropping with a partially inflated canopy.
