Multiple human errors are identifiable: jumping in rain on a wet ramp despite recognizing the hazard, insisting on going first against mentor advice, and attempting a first cliff jump from a 700-foot object with an outcropping at halfway with only 16 prior BASE jumps (all towers). The decision to jump in these conditions and the resulting unstable exit constitute clear human error contributing to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit from wet ramp
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper slipped on the wet launch ramp, resulting in loss of control during freefall. He deployed on his back with a partially inflated canopy and struck the tree-filled outcropping. The execution failure on exit — slipping — produced the unstable freefall, back-to-earth deployment, and fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Wet exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: It had been raining and the wooden launch ramp was wet. The wet surface directly caused the jumper to slip during his exit run, producing the unstable freefall and back-to-earth deployment that led to the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience for cliff
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first cliff jump after only 15 tower jumps (16 total BASE jumps) with no reported prior cliff experience. Accepted progression norms require developing object avoidance skills at spans before progressing to cliffs, and sub-terminal tracking is mandatory for slider-up cliffs. The jumper's limited experience base left him without the skills needed to recover from the unstable exit on a 700-foot cliff with a mid-face outcropping.

L0_description: The jumper used modified skydiving gear for a solo first BASE jump, including a PO-9 skydiving canopy known in the Russian BASE community as unsuitable for BASE jumping. Using skydiving equipment not designed or appropriate for BASE jumping constitutes a clear human error in equipment selection. The jumper had only 1 BASE jump (this was their first), and no reported skydiving experience, indicating a severe departure from accepted progression norms.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate gear selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used modified skydiving gear including a PO-9 skydiving canopy known in the Russian BASE community as unsuitable for BASE jumping. Using a skydiving canopy and modified skydiving equipment for a slider-down BASE jump from a building is a fundamentally inappropriate equipment decision. The gear selection itself was the producing mechanism: a skydiving canopy lacks the design features (reinforcement, heading performance, appropriate opening characteristics, deep brake settings) required for low-altitude fixed-object deployments, and this inadequacy directly produced the fatal outcome regardless of execution quality.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 BASE jump (this fatal jump was their first), no reported skydiving experience, and no evidence of a first jump course or mentorship. This lack of BASE-specific training and knowledge directly contributed to the inappropriate gear selection, as accepted BASE education would have corrected the decision to use a modified skydiving rig with a canopy known to be unsuitable for BASE.

L0_description: The jumper went head down on exit, tumbled, and struck the wall before achieving canopy deployment. With only 2 prior BASE jumps, the unstable exit from a cliff object represents a human error in execution. The canopy only partially deployed from the impact itself, indicating subterminal impact near the exit point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper went head down on the launch and tumbled, failing to achieve a stable exit position. This unstable exit resulted in the jumper striking the wall before any effective deployment could occur, with the canopy only partially deploying from the force of wall impact. The execution of the exit was the direct producing mechanism of the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 2 prior BASE jumps and approximately 200 skydives. With minimal BASE experience, the jumper lacked the practiced exit skills needed for a slider-down cliff jump, where a stable exit is critical and there is virtually no margin for recovery from an unstable body position. This level of experience is clearly insufficient for the task attempted, directly contributing to the failed exit execution.

L0_description: The jumper continued filming on his back for approximately 3 seconds beyond the planned deployment time of the second jumper, then took additional time to transition to belly-to-earth before deploying. The narrative explicitly identifies extracting the pilot chute too late and focusing on filming as factors. The jumper deployed too low, impacting the ground under a partially inflated canopy, with fog obscuring altitude references.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / low pull
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Lee continued filming on his back for approximately 3 seconds beyond the second jumper's 8-second deployment, then took an additional second to transition to face-to-earth before disappearing into fog. He deployed too late to achieve a fully inflated canopy before ground impact. The narrative indicates he intended a 10-second delay but the total elapsed time before deployment initiation exceeded what the available altitude could support, constituting a failure in execution — specifically an attention failure in monitoring altitude/time while focused on filming.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Lee was filming the other jumpers while on his back, channelizing his attention on the camera task to the exclusion of altitude/time awareness. The narrative explicitly identifies 'focusing on filming' as a possible factor, and describes him continuing to film on his back for 3 full seconds beyond the 8-second deployment point before transitioning to face-to-earth.

L2_preconditions.label: Fog Obscuring Altitude Cues
L2_preconditions.description: A layer of fog approximately 300 feet off the ground eliminated visual altitude references. The fog degraded Lee's ability to perceive his proximity to the ground, directly impairing the altitude awareness needed to execute a timely deployment. The second jumper believed Lee was expecting to come out of the fog before deploying, suggesting the fog actively removed the visual cues he relied upon.

L0_description: The jumper had only 12 BASE jumps and was going stowed (BOC deployment) for the very first time, using a 38-inch pilot chute with a 6'9" bridle on a 1.5-second delay during a complex 8-way coordinated jump. This configuration and jump complexity were clearly inappropriate for his experience level. The result was a PC in tow / PC-bridle entanglement total malfunction, with the canopy never extracting from the container. Multiple human errors in equipment selection, deployment method choice, and jump planning are identifiable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment from stow
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a pilot chute in tow, meaning the PC failed to extract the canopy from the container. He was going stowed for the very first time, using a 38-inch PC with a 6'9" bridle on a 1.5-second delay from a span. The PC opened only 0.4 seconds before impact, indicating it remained entangled or ineffective throughout the brief freefall. The execution of the stowed PC deployment was inadequate - whether through weak throw, late deployment, or improper technique - resulting in the PC failing to catch clean air and extract the canopy.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 12 BASE jumps and was attempting a stowed deployment for the very first time. Stowed deployment requires practiced technique to cleanly extract and throw the PC from the BOC into clean air, a skill distinct from hand-held deployment. With no prior stowed BASE deployments, the jumper lacked the trained proficiency needed to execute this critical task reliably, directly contributing to the failed PC deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration for task
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper used a 38-inch PC with a 6'9" bridle on a stowed 1.5-second slider-down deployment from a span. Per accepted practice, a 46-inch PC is standard for 0-1 second slider-down delays, and 42-46 inches for ~2-second delays. A 38-inch PC is undersized for this low-airspeed application, producing less drag force and requiring more time to inflate and extract the canopy. Combined with a shorter-than-standard bridle (6'9" vs. standard 9'), the PC had less distance to clear the burble. This configuration was inadequate for the planned jump and directly contributed to the PC-in-tow malfunction.

L0_label: Unstable exit leading to cliff impact
L0_description: The jumper lost stability on exit from a cliff and struck the cliff wall in freefall at approximately 11 seconds without deploying her parachute. The narrative indicates she did not maintain adequate separation from the object and lost altitude awareness, both of which are human performance failures. Her limited BASE experience (five bridge jumps) on her first cliff jump suggests she was at a progression stage where the required skills for cliff jumping (sub-terminal tracking, exit-to-track transition) had not been sufficiently developed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Lori launched and lost stability on her first cliff jump. She was unable to recover a stable body position or achieve adequate separation from the cliff, resulting in impact with the cliff wall in freefall at approximately 11 seconds. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating she never deployed her parachute, likely due to loss of altitude awareness and/or proximity to the wall resulting from the sustained instability.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Lori had only five previous Perrine bridge jumps before attempting her first cliff jump. Slider-up cliff jumping requires sub-terminal tracking skills and the ability to recover from unstable exits near terrain — capabilities that develop over substantially more BASE experience than five bridge jumps. This insufficient cliff-specific experience directly contributed to her inability to recover stability and achieve separation after losing control on exit.

L0_description: The jumper launched head-down on a planned 9-10 second delay, deployed before achieving complete stability, and the canopy opened facing the cliff with insufficient separation. The unstable exit, premature deployment before recovery, and inadequate forward separation from the object constitute identifiable human errors that directly led to the off-heading opening and fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and premature deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Nik launched head-down and deployed before achieving complete stability. This unstable body position at deployment directly contributed to the off-heading opening (canopy opened facing the cliff), which produced the fatal object strike. The execution of the exit and the timing of deployment were insufficient — he intended a 9-10 second delay but deployed while still unstable, indicating a performance failure in maintaining stability and/or deploying at the appropriate time.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient tracking separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: With most previous BASE jumps being low objects, Nik was actively working on tracking skills but did not achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff during freefall. The insufficient object separation meant that when the canopy opened off-heading (facing the cliff), there was not enough distance to complete the heading correction before striking the ledge. This is an execution/technique error in sub-terminal tracking performance.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that most of Nik's previous BASE jumps had been low objects and that he was actively working on his tracking skills. This indicates his tracking proficiency was still developing and insufficient for the demands of a 9-10 second terminal cliff delay requiring effective sub-terminal/terminal tracking for object separation. This training gap directly contributed to both the insufficient separation and the unstable exit/premature deployment on a long-delay cliff jump.

L0_description: The jumper was performing camera duties on a two-way jump and attempted to film his partner's opening after a 5-second delay. This task caused him to extract his pilot chute too late, resulting in a low pull and ground impact with the canopy still deploying. The jump partner explicitly identified the plan as flawed, noting that filming a 5-second delay opening at Staubbach was not feasible, and the unfamiliar camera system may have further degraded performance.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Wolfgang extracted his pilot chute too late after attempting to film his jump partner's opening. The canopy lines were completely out of the tail pocket but the canopy only opened after impact, indicating deployment was initiated at an altitude insufficient for completion. The filming task degraded his tracking and delayed his pull, resulting in a fatal low deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Wolfgang was focused on filming his partner's opening, which diverted his attention from tracking performance and altitude awareness. The partner's account confirms that the filming task degraded his tracking ('doing camera = bad (not perfect) tracking') and that attempting to film a 5-second delay opening at Staubbach was not feasible, indicating his attention was channelized on the camera task at the expense of monitoring his own deployment altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate jump plan
L2_preconditions.description: The jump partner explicitly states the cause was 'stupid plan / not thinking' and that filming a 5-second delay including opening at Staubbach was not possible. The plan required the cameraman to track while filming, then deploy with adequate altitude, but the task was incompatible with the available altitude. Both jumpers had enough experience to recognize this beforehand but failed to identify the plan as unworkable during pre-jump planning.

L2_preconditions.label: Unfamiliar camera equipment
L2_preconditions.description: Wolfgang was jumping a borrowed TV station camera system (bicycle helmet with front-mounted camera and cable to belly recorder) instead of his own superior helmet setup. The partner noted the system looked 'a little bit dangerous' and was not very stable on the head. This unfamiliar, suboptimal equipment configuration may have further degraded his ability to manage the filming task efficiently while maintaining altitude awareness and tracking performance.

L0_description: The narrative describes Christophe's canopy being out but not pressurized, with him found dead 80 meters up on the wall. The 'Possible Factors' field mentions 'clipped ledge after exiting,' but the narrative does not provide clear evidence of what caused the fatal outcome. The cause of death is listed as 'impact in terminal freefall,' yet the canopy was described as out. There is insufficient information to determine whether this was a human error (poor exit, inadequate tracking, packing issue) or another cause, as the sequence of events between exit and the final outcome is unclear.

The jumper never gained control after exit and impacted subterminal near the exit point. With only 16 BASE jumps, jumping from a slider-up cliff with a sketchy launch point as part of a four-way indicates human error in exit execution and/or object selection relative to experience level.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited from a sketchy launch point and never gained control from exit through impact. With only 16 BASE jumps at a slider-up cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking, the jumper failed to execute a stable exit and was unable to recover a flyable body position, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit point.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 16 BASE jumps, the jumper attempted a slider-up cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking and a stable exit from a sketchy launch point. This is well below the experience level accepted practice requires for terminal/sub-terminal slider-up cliff objects, where exit technique, sub-terminal tracking, and recovery from poor exits are critical skills developed over many more jumps. This inexperience directly contributed to the inability to execute a stable exit and recover control.

L0_description: The jumper deployed too late to survive, resulting in ground impact shortly after canopy opening. Whether the cause was a stability problem from suit failure (original report) or poor flight performance (update), the jumper failed to deploy at a survivable altitude. With only 2 wingsuit BASE jumps and 20 wingsuit skydives (well below the recommended 100 wingsuit skydives minimum), the jumper lacked the experience to manage the situation, representing a human error in both the decision to attempt wingsuit BASE at this experience level and the failure to deploy in time.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment during wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Kirill failed to deploy his parachute at a survivable altitude during his wingsuit BASE flight. The update clarifies the canopy was fully open but he 'hooked ground' immediately after opening, indicating deployment occurred too late for the canopy to arrest his descent before terrain contact. With only 2 wingsuit BASE jumps and 20 wingsuit skydives, his altitude awareness and deployment timing execution were insufficient for the demands of wingsuit BASE flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Kirill had only 2 wingsuit BASE jumps and 20 wingsuit skydives. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps before attempting wingsuit BASE. With such minimal wingsuit BASE experience, the altitude awareness and deployment timing skills required for wingsuit terrain flight were underdeveloped, directly contributing to the fatally late deployment.

L0_description: The jumper chose to exit from a site with only 5 seconds of rock drop time that multiple experienced wingsuit pilots assessed as not achievable. Despite repeated warnings that the jump was not feasible, the jumper proceeded with a reverse gainer exit and impacted a talus ledge after 7 seconds of freefall without attempting deployment. This represents a clear decision error in selecting an object that exceeded what could be managed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose unachievable exit/line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Rob selected an exit point ('RT Hjjrner') with only a 5-second rock drop and a ledge that multiple experienced wingsuit pilots, including his jumping partner, assessed as not achievable. Despite repeated warnings that the jump could not be made, he committed to the exit including a reverse gainer. The decision to jump this specific site was the producing mechanism: the object's geometry did not provide sufficient clearance for a wingsuit flight path, resulting in impact with the talus ledge at subterminal speed with no possibility of parachute deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Rob was repeatedly told by his jumping partner and other experienced wingsuit pilots that the jump was not achievable, yet he remained convinced he could make it, including adding a reverse gainer. His belief that he could 'out fly the ledge right up until he died' despite unanimous expert assessment to the contrary demonstrates an unreasonable overestimation of his own capability relative to the specific task, directly driving the decision to attempt the jump.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a PC/bridle entanglement leading to total malfunction. While the narrative describes the mechanism (PC spinning in the burble and entangling with the bridle), there is insufficient information to determine whether this resulted from human error (e.g., PC deployment technique, equipment selection, or configuration) or from factors outside the jumper's control (e.g., equipment failure independent of user action). The narrative states he had a 'powerful pull' and was described as very experienced, but critical details about PC type, handle weight, deployment technique specifics, and equipment configuration are not reported, making it impossible to distinguish between human error and non-human cause.

The jumper experienced a 90-degree off-heading opening that progressed to wall impact. Off-headings can result from multiple factors (packing, body position, PC stability, weather, equipment configuration), and the narrative does not provide sufficient information to identify which specific human error caused the off-heading. Body position was reported as 'good,' weather was light and variable winds, and gear appeared in working order. The absence of helmet and protective gear is noted but the cause of death was object strike, not inadequate protection. Without evidence identifying why the off-heading occurred or why the canopy turned further into the wall, neither a specific human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be established.

The jumper failed to complete the lark's head knot securing the bridle to the canopy attachment ring during rig assembly, likely due to distraction and not restarting the procedure upon return. This rigging error resulted in a total malfunction where the pilot chute and bridle separated from the canopy, which is a clear human error in equipment assembly.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Incomplete bridle attachment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Bill passed the bridle through the canopy attachment ring but failed to complete the lark's head knot that would secure the connection. The narrative speculates he was distracted during rigging and did not finish the procedure, resulting in the bridle not being connected to the canopy. This rigging execution error directly produced a total malfunction — the pilot chute and bridle extracted cleanly but the canopy remained in the container, causing closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction during rigging
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Bill was speculated to have been distracted while putting his rig together, interrupting the bridle attachment procedure. This distraction caused him to leave the lark's head knot incomplete without restarting the procedure upon return, directly enabling the incomplete bridle attachment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A pre-jump gear check tracing the deployment chain from pilot chute through bridle to canopy would have revealed that the bridle was not secured to the canopy with a completed lark's head knot. The bar tack catching in the ring gave the appearance of a correct attachment, but a hands-on inspection of the connection point would have detected the incomplete attachment.

L0_description: The jumper deployed after a 5-6 second delay in medium to strong winds at a site where locals recommend 8-9 seconds minimum, resulting in insufficient object separation and a 180° off-heading opening into the wall. Questions about the jumper's experience and preparation level, combined with jumping in high wind conditions at an advanced site with an inadequate delay, indicate human error in planning and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate delay for conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used a 5-6 second delay at a site where locals recommend at least 8-9 seconds for adequate object separation. Combined with medium to strong winds and crosswinds at exit, this short delay left the jumper too close to the wall at opening, directly producing the conditions for the fatal object strike when the 180° off-heading occurred. The decision to use an insufficient delay in strong/crosswind conditions was the producing mechanism: more delay would have provided the tracking time and separation needed to survive the off-heading.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswinds degraded separation
L2_preconditions.description: Medium to strong winds with crosswinds at exit actively degraded the jumper's ability to achieve adequate object separation during the 5-6 second delay. Locals specifically cited the high winds as a contributing factor, indicating the wind conditions mechanically reduced the effectiveness of whatever tracking and separation the jumper achieved, compounding the insufficient delay.

L0_description: The narrative states 'no one knows exactly what went wrong.' The possible factors listed (emergency low pull, extracted pilot chute too late, loss of speed-glide) are speculative and not substantiated by clear evidence in the narrative. While the canopy began deploying at a very low altitude over the talus, the reason for the late deployment is unknown — it could reflect a human error (delayed pull, loss of altitude awareness, tracking problem) or another cause, but the narrative does not provide sufficient information to distinguish. The single released toggle and possible 180-degree off-heading are post-impact observations that do not clarify why deployment was initiated so late.

The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening with line twists resulting in object strike. However, the narrative provides no information about the specific cause of the off-heading and line twists — body position at deployment, packing quality, PC condition, tracking performance, exit quality, and equipment details are all unreported. While off-headings can result from human error (poor body position, poor packing, inadequate tracking), they can also occur without identifiable human error. The possible factors listed (180 off-heading, line twists) describe what happened but not why. Without evidence identifying the root cause, neither 001HE nor 002OC can be supported.

The jumper was proximity flying a wingsuit near terrain and struck a ledge she could not outfly, resulting in impact in terminal freefall without attempting to deploy. Choosing a flight line that required clearing a ledge with insufficient margin represents a decision error in line selection and terrain assessment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew line requiring terrain clearance without margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flying line that required clearing a ledge, at a site known by only a very few jumpers. She was unable to outfly the ledge with her wingsuit, resulting in terrain impact in terminal freefall. The decision to fly a line that committed her to clearing specific terrain features without adequate margin was the producing mechanism — she did not attempt to pull, indicating she was in a no-pull zone when the terrain strike became inevitable. This represents a flight planning decision that was clearly inadequate for the terrain, independent of execution quality.

L0_description: The jumper was performing aerials from a span (slider-down) and either over-delayed or experienced a pilot chute/bridle entanglement resulting in the canopy not inflating before impact. Aerials exponentially increase danger through loss of altitude awareness, instability, and bridle entanglement risk. The PC deployed but the canopy did not inflate, consistent with either excessive delay or deployment complications arising from the aerial maneuver, both of which constitute human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of non-deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper was performing aerials and either over-delayed or had a pilot chute/bridle hang-up, with the PC deploying but the canopy not inflating prior to impact. The narrative explicitly presents two competing explanations (over-delay vs. PC/bridle entanglement) without resolving which occurred. There is insufficient information to determine whether this was a decision error (choosing excessive delay for aerials) or a performance/skill-based error (execution failure during aerials leading to late deployment or entanglement).

L0_description: The jumper dropped the hand-held pilot chute while rushing to escape law enforcement, picked it up in an entangled state, and jumped anyway. The PC/bridle entanglement prevented deployment, resulting in a total malfunction and fatal impact. Jumping with a compromised deployment system is a clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped with entangled PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper dropped the hand-held pilot chute, and it became entangled with him during pickup. He then jumped anyway with the entangled PC, resulting in a PC in tow / bridle entanglement and total malfunction. The decision to jump with a compromised deployment system — regardless of whether he was fully aware of the extent of the entanglement — was the producing mechanism for the fatality. Jumping with a known-disturbed PC configuration under pressure from law enforcement was a clearly inappropriate decision; aborting and accepting arrest was the obvious alternative.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Dropped hand-held PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper dropped the hand-held pilot chute while rushing to the exit point under pressure from highway patrol. This execution error created the entangled PC condition that led to the fatal total malfunction, though the subsequent decision to jump with the compromised PC was the primary producing mechanism.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Highway patrol was actively attempting to arrest the jumpers, creating acute pressure to jump immediately rather than abort. This law enforcement pressure drove the decision to jump with a compromised PC rather than accept being caught, which would have been the safe alternative.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was running toward the exit point to escape highway patrol, and this haste directly caused him to drop the hand-held pilot chute. The rushing was driven by the pressure of imminent arrest rather than normal jump preparation.

L2_preconditions.label: Night conditions degraded handling
L2_preconditions.description: The jump occurred at night, which would have degraded the jumper's ability to securely handle the pilot chute while running to the exit point and to visually assess the entanglement after dropping and picking up the PC.

L0_description: The narrative indicates the jumper either extracted the pilot chute too late or experienced pilot chute hesitation, resulting in a fatal low pull. The jumper had only 129 skydives (well below the accepted minimum of 200) and only 3 BASE jumps, indicating insufficient experience for a slider-up antenna jump requiring proper delay management and deployment technique. This points to human error in deployment execution and/or progression decisions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late or failed deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative indicates a deployment problem—either the jumper initiated the deployment sequence too late or experienced pilot chute hesitation, resulting in a low pull and ground impact. There is insufficient detail to determine whether this was a decision error (consciously choosing to delay deployment) or a performance/skill-based error (failing to execute a timely pull or poor PC deployment technique).

L0_description: The jumper had only 30 BASE jumps and was on his 2nd jump from this cliff (the Nose). Fellow jumpers reported he was very nervous at exit points and may have been jumping above his limits. With limited experience at slider-up cliff objects, he experienced a 180° off-heading opening and was unable to correct before striking the cliff, indicating insufficient progression and object avoidance skills for the object being jumped.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to correct off-heading opening
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a 180-degree off-heading opening approximately 200 meters off the ground while facing the cliff. He struck the cliff approximately 3 seconds after opening without successfully correcting the heading. The narrative indicates this was his first off-heading opening, suggesting he lacked the practiced muscle memory for instant heading correction (rear riser or brake input) needed to turn away from the wall within the available time window.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 30 BASE jumps and this being only his 2nd jump from the Nose (a slider-up cliff), the jumper had very limited experience for terminal cliff jumping. The narrative explicitly states this was his first off-heading opening, meaning he had never practiced the critical skill of instant heading correction in a real BASE scenario. Accepted progression norms emphasize extensive practice of off-heading corrections at simple spans before progressing to cliffs, precisely because the muscle memory must be automatic when facing the wall. His experience level was clearly insufficient for the task of recovering from an off-heading at a cliff with limited separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Jumping Above Recognized Limits
L2_preconditions.description: Fellow jumpers reported that the jumper was very nervous at exit points on numerous occasions and may have been jumping above his limits. Persistent nervousness recognized by peers suggests the jumper was repeatedly operating beyond his comfort and capability level. This pattern of proceeding despite signals of unreadiness placed him in situations where he lacked the composure and practiced capability to handle emergencies such as the 180-degree off-heading that killed him.

L0_description: The jumper failed to clear an outcropping during wingsuit flight, resulting in terrain impact in freefall. However, the narrative provides no information about the jumper's experience level, the specific flight line chosen, exit quality, equipment condition, or what caused the failure to clear the terrain feature. Without details on whether this resulted from a decision error (e.g., choosing an inappropriate line), a skill-based error (e.g., poor exit or flight technique), an environmental factor (e.g., unexpected sink or wind), or equipment issue, there is insufficient information to distinguish between human error and other causes.

Multiple witnesses confirm the jumper located the pilot chute handle at a reasonable altitude but kept his hand there and continued flying without deploying, ultimately pulling at approximately 30 feet AGL. This delayed deployment constitutes a human error that directly caused the fatal low-altitude impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment after locating PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: All witnesses agree that the jumper reached for and located the pilot chute at a reasonable altitude but then kept his hand there and continued in freefall without deploying. He finally deployed at approximately 30 feet above ground, far too late for the canopy to inflate. This represents a failure in execution — the jumper found the handle but failed to extract and deploy the PC in a timely manner, likely due to an attention/perception failure (misreading altitude due to unfamiliar groundrush characteristics in wingsuit flight). The intended action was correct but the execution was fatally delayed.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states speculation that the lack of normal groundrush — unlike what the jumper was accustomed to without a wingsuit — may have fooled him into misjudging his altitude. Wingsuit flight produces a fundamentally different visual speed picture (higher horizontal speed, lower vertical speed) than non-wingsuit freefall, creating a visual illusion that degraded his ability to perceive true altitude and proximity to the ground. This perceptual failure directly explains why he held his hand on the PC handle without extracting it.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first wingsuit BASE jump and his first jump ever with a leg-mounted pilot chute pouch. He had only 50 wingsuit skydives (below the accepted minimum of ~100) and 2 balloon wingsuit jumps. The combination of a novel deployment system, unfamiliar altitude perception in wingsuit flight near terrain, and insufficient wingsuit experience directly contributed to his failure to execute a timely deployment despite having located the handle.

L0_description: The jumper experienced line twists after wingsuit deployment leading to object strike on an adjacent rooftop. However, the narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the line twists resulted from human error (packing, body position asymmetry at deployment, equipment selection) or from factors outside the jumper's control. The jumper was highly experienced with 1200 BASE jumps, and the narrative describes a 'good flight' before deployment, but the cause of the line twists and inability to clear them or avoid the structure cannot be determined from the available information.

The jumper performed a front somersault that over-rotated, delaying the start of his track at a site requiring mandatory tracking for wall separation. He extracted his pilot chute too late and impacted the underhung section before full deployment. The over-rotation and delayed track/deployment constitute human errors contributing to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Over-rotated aerial exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jason performed a single front somersault that over-rotated, which delayed the start of his track. This execution error directly reduced the time and distance available for tracking away from the wall, resulting in insufficient separation to clear the underhung section before impact.

L0_description: The narrative indicates the jumper extracted his pilot chute too late, resulting in a low pull with only a partially open canopy at ground impact. The planned deployment sequence involved the jumper signaling by reaching for his pilot chute and then delaying one additional second, and the companion observed the jumper impact with a partially opened canopy, indicating insufficient altitude remained for full canopy inflation. This represents a human error in deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted his pilot chute too late, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to fully open before ground impact. The narrative states he was observed with only a partially open canopy at impact, and the possible factors field identifies 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late.' The planned deployment sequence (reach for PC, delay one second while partner deploys) was executed too late in the freefall, constituting a performance error in altitude awareness and deployment timing.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions
L2_preconditions.description: Low-level clouds and foggy conditions degraded the jumper's ability to visually assess altitude and proximity to the ground. The narrative explicitly notes this is 'the third BASE fatality that is fog related,' indicating fog directly impaired altitude perception, contributing to the late pilot chute extraction.

L0_label: Aerials leading to unstable deployment and object strike
L0_description: The jumper performed aerials from a cliff, which led to stability problems and a late deployment. The canopy opened with a 180° off-heading, resulting in object strike. Aerials exponentially increase danger and are recognized as requiring careful planning and forgiving objects; performing them in medium-to-strong wind conditions with crosswinds compounds the risk. The decision to perform aerials and the resulting instability and late deployment constitute identifiable human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit from aerials
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed aerials and subsequently encountered stability problems. This instability led to a late deployment, which combined with an off-heading opening resulted in insufficient separation from the wall and a fatal object strike under canopy. The execution of the aerials produced the instability that cascaded into the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswind degraded canopy performance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports medium to strong winds with crosswinds at the exit. The press translation states a squall of local wind caused loss of canopy control. This wind condition actively degraded the jumper's ability to maintain heading and fly away from the wall after the already-late, off-heading opening that resulted from the unstable exit.

L0_description: The jumper launched from the second level of the Eiffel Tower at 370 feet AGL with only 170 feet of clearance to the first level at 200 feet AGL. This created an extremely constrained environment where any delay in deployment or imperfect exit trajectory would result in object strike. The decision to jump from this level after aborting higher options, combined with the resulting subterminal impact with the first level structure, indicates human error in object evaluation and jump planning.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped from inadequate altitude
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump from the second level of the Eiffel Tower at 370 feet AGL with only 170 feet of clearance to the first level at 200 feet AGL. With a slider-down BOC deployment from a structure where the jump path passes directly over a protruding lower platform only 170 feet below, the decision to jump from this level created an inadequate margin for canopy deployment and object clearance. The jumper impacted the first level of the tower, indicating the 170-foot window was insufficient for the deployment sequence and any deviation in trajectory.

L0_label: Multiple human errors in wingsuit flight
L0_description: Multiple human errors are identifiable. The jumper flew a new wingsuit model (S3) for the first time in BASE with only 11 prior wingsuit BASE jumps, well below accepted progression norms. He jumped in medium/strong crosswind conditions that contributed to instability. He was unable to locate and deploy his pilot chute, resulting in closed-container terminal impact. The combination of insufficient experience on the suit type, jumping in adverse wind conditions, and failure to deploy constitutes clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with the pilot chute still stowed in the leg pouch. He was unable to locate or extract the pilot chute, resulting in a total malfunction (no deployment). This is an execution failure — the jumper intended to deploy but failed to accomplish the physical act of extracting and deploying the PC.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew unfamiliar wingsuit in adverse conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew a Birdman S3 wingsuit for the first time in BASE, having only 11 prior wingsuit BASE jumps (all on a different, smaller suit — the BM GTI), in medium-to-strong crosswind conditions. Accepted practice requires 40-60 skydives in a specific suit before BASE jumping it, and the jumper's total wingsuit BASE experience (11 jumps) was well below the 100-jump minimum recommended before first wingsuit BASE. Flying an unfamiliar higher-performance suit in wind created the instability that prevented deployment, making this decision the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 11 wingsuit BASE jumps, far below the accepted minimum of 100 BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE. This insufficient experience base directly contributed to the decision to fly an unfamiliar, higher-performance wingsuit in adverse wind conditions — a decision a more experienced wingsuit BASE jumper would likely not have made.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswind destabilized exit/flight
L2_preconditions.description: Medium-to-strong crosswinds reportedly contributed to the jumper's stability problems during flight. This wind-induced instability directly degraded his ability to maintain a stable body position and locate/extract the pilot chute from the leg pouch, contributing to the failure to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation from Instability
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports the jumper had stability problems, likely from the combination of an unfamiliar suit and crosswinds. An unstable or tumbling body position in a wingsuit would degrade spatial orientation and the ability to execute the practiced BOC reach motion, directly contributing to the failure to locate and extract the pilot chute.

L0_description: The narrative reports a 180° off-heading opening and object strike under canopy, but provides no information about the jumper's experience level relative to the site, packing details, body position at deployment, exit quality, or specific site conditions that would allow identification of a specific human error pathway. The off-heading itself is a known inherent risk of BASE jumping, and without evidence of what caused it (poor body position, packing error, inappropriate equipment, poor site selection, etc.), neither a human error classification nor a non-human cause classification can be adequately supported.

The jumper failed to recognize how low he was due to reduced ground rush perception while tracking in a tracking suit, then experienced instability at deployment time and delayed his pilot chute extraction while attempting to regain stability. This sequence of errors—deploying too late and prioritizing stability over immediate deployment at critically low altitude—resulted in impact near line stretch. The narrative explicitly identifies extracting the pilot chute too late and loss of speed/glide as contributing factors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment due to instability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced instability at deployment time while wearing a tracking suit, tumbled, and then attempted to regain stability before deploying the pilot chute. This delay in extracting the PC—prioritizing stability recovery over immediate deployment at an altitude where margins were already thin—resulted in impact at approximately line stretch. The execution failure was in not deploying promptly despite the low altitude, and in the technique breakdown that caused the instability and tumble during the deployment sequence.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's exceptionally effective tracking suit performance along the talus created an unusual visual environment where normal ground rush cues were absent. The narrative explicitly states he 'probably didn't experience normal ground rush as his track was really going at that point' and 'didn't notice just exactly how low he was.' This visual illusion caused him to misjudge his altitude, leaving him with thinner margins when the instability and delayed deployment occurred.

L0_description: The narrative is absent and structured fields provide only minimal information: 31 BASE jumps, antenna object, slider-up, unstable exit as a possible factor, and death by object strike under canopy. Without a narrative or further detail, there is insufficient information to reliably distinguish between human error pathways (e.g., decision error, skill-based error) and other causes. The 'possible factor' of unstable exit is suggestive but not confirmed, and the causal chain from exit to object strike cannot be established with confidence.

The jumper was highly experienced (1869 BASE jumps), equipment was checked post-accident and found properly configured with nothing wrong, and the possible factor listed is 'extracted pilot chute too late,' but the narrative explicitly states 'no clear conclusions can be made as to why' the pull was low. The night/new moon conditions and tracking suit are noted, but there is insufficient information to determine whether a human error caused the late deployment or whether some other factor was responsible. Without clear evidence supporting either a human error pathway or a non-human cause, this is best classified as insufficient information.

L0_label: Packing aid left on pilot chute
L0_description: The jumper failed to remove a pull-up cord used as a packing aid from the pilot chute prior to jumping. This restricted the pilot chute from functioning properly, resulting in a pilot chute in tow and total malfunction. Leaving a packing tool on the equipment is a clear human error in rigging/preparation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to remove packing aid
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to remove the pull-up cord used as a packing aid from the pilot chute prior to jumping. This memory/attention failure during the packing and pre-jump process resulted in the pilot chute being unable to extract the canopy, causing a total malfunction and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Gear check omission
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to perform a final gear check that would have detected the pull-up cord still attached to the pilot chute. A pre-jump equipment inspection is accepted practice and would have caught this rigging error before exit.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening with possible line twists, resulting in object strike. Witnesses reported he appeared to have enough time to turn but did not, suggesting 'maybe line twists or some other problem not apparent from the top.' The narrative does not provide sufficient information to determine whether the failure to correct heading resulted from a human error (such as inadequate reaction or skill deficit) or from a factor outside the jumper's control (such as severe line twists preventing correction). The cause of the correction failure remains speculative.

L0_label: Inexperienced jumper poor exit and off-heading
L0_description: The jumper had only one previous BASE jump and attempted a slider-up cliff jump, representing a severe progression violation. He over-rotated head-down before deploying, resulting in a 180° off-heading opening facing the cliff. Despite riser input, he was unable to avoid object strike. The poor body position at deployment and the decision to jump a slider-up cliff with minimal experience constitute clear human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and poor body position
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper over-rotated head down before deploying the pilot chute, resulting in an unstable body position at deployment. This unstable deployment produced a 180° off-heading opening facing the cliff. Despite attempting riser corrections, the jumper was unable to clear the wall in time, resulting in fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only one previous BASE jump (two total including the fatal jump). Slider-up cliff jumping requires sub-terminal tracking ability, refined exit technique, and practiced object avoidance skills that are developed over many jumps at progressively more demanding objects. With only one prior BASE jump, the jumper lacked the experience to execute a stable exit and deployment from a slider-up cliff object, directly contributing to the over-rotation and unstable body position at deployment.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening resulting in object strike. However, the narrative provides no information about the jumper's experience level, the specific cliff characteristics, body position at deployment, packing quality, equipment condition, or any other factors that would allow identification of a specific human error pathway versus an inherent risk outcome. Off-headings can result from multiple causes including body position, packing, PC stability, and weather, but no specific cause is identifiable from the available information.

The jumper was known for flying close to the wall and was performing proximity wingsuit/tracking suit flight near terrain. Witness reports indicate he impacted the wall with his canopy out at approximately 300 meters from the bottom, suggesting loss of separation from terrain during proximity flying. The possible factors of loss of speed-glide and proximity flying indicate the jumper flew too close to terrain without adequate margin, which is a human error in flight path management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of wall impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall and was found hung up on the wall with his canopy out at approximately 300 meters from the bottom. He was known for flying close to the wall and was wearing a tracking suit on a slider-up cliff jump. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the fatal outcome resulted from a specific decision error (e.g., choosing a line too close to terrain) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., loss of control, failed execution of proximity flight, delayed deployment). The possible factors listed include loss of speed-glide and proximity flying, but the mechanism connecting these to the outcome is not clearly established.

L0_description: The jumper failed to successfully extract and deploy the pilot chute from the BOC pocket. Witnesses report she either missed the handle or lost her grip, and her final attempt resulted in the PC ending up on her back and hesitating, leading to a total malfunction and impact. The failed PC deployment from a stowed BOC on a span jump represents a human error in the deployment process.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Shannon reached for her stowed pilot chute and either missed it or lost her grip during extraction from the BOC. Witnesses report her last attempt resulted in the pilot chute ending up on her back and hesitating, leading to a total malfunction and impact with nothing out. The intended action (deploying the PC) was correct, but the physical execution failed.

L0_description: The jumper turned the wrong direction after hooking up his static line pilot chute, wrapping the bridle around his lower body without noticing before launching. This rigging error caused the bridle to foul around his body, preventing normal canopy deployment and resulting in impact death.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Turned wrong direction wrapping bridle
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After hooking up his static line pilot chute and turning to face away from the tower, Mario turned the wrong way, wrapping the bridle around his lower body. This execution error — turning in the incorrect direction — directly produced the bridle entanglement that prevented normal canopy deployment and caused the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failed pre-jump bridle trace
L2_preconditions.description: After turning to face away from the tower, Mario did not notice the bridle wrapped around his lower body prior to launching. Accepted practice for static line jumps requires tracing the bridle by hand from shrivel flap to anchor before jumping, and having a knowledgeable person verify the jumper is clear of rigging. Mario was alone on the tower and failed to perform this check, which would have detected the entanglement caused by the wrong-direction turn.

L0_description: The narrative states the jumper 'experienced some problems, the cause of which is unknown' and went too close to the wall, resulting in cliff strike during freefall. While proximity flying and loss of speed/glide are listed as possible factors, the narrative explicitly acknowledges the cause is unknown. There is insufficient information to determine whether the cliff strike resulted from a human error (poor exit, poor tracking technique, decision error in line selection) or from an unforeseeable environmental factor, making it impossible to reliably distinguish between 001HE and 002OC.

The jumper attempted a front loop aerial, over-rotated into a head-down position, and spent nearly the entire freefall attempting to recover. He deployed his pilot chute too late to extract the canopy before impacting a ledge. The decision to perform an aerial and the resulting loss of altitude awareness and body position control constitute clear human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Over-rotation of front loop
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a front loop aerial but over-rotated, going fully head-down for almost the entire freefall while trying to correct. This execution failure consumed all available altitude and directly resulted in the fatal low deployment. The jumper intended to complete the aerial successfully but failed in the physical execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation from Tumble
L2_preconditions.description: The over-rotation sent the jumper fully head-down, and he spent almost the entire freefall trying to correct. The disorienting head-down attitude from the failed aerial likely degraded his ability to perceive altitude and orientation, contributing to the inability to recover and the extremely late deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Aerial Dependency
L2_preconditions.description: The companion jumper's account reveals that gainers had become a 'crutch move' used to manage exit anxiety, and that both jumpers had developed a pattern of defaulting to aerials rather than maintaining flat-exit proficiency. This normalized reliance on aerials without addressing the underlying skill deficiency contributed to attempting the front loop from this object.

L0_description: Video evidence shows Brian deployed his pilot chute into his chest rather than into clean air, where it became trapped against his body for approximately 2 seconds. This PC deployment failure resulted in a PC in tow condition, and the parachute had insufficient time to fully deploy before impact. The failed deployment technique constitutes a human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment into clean air
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Video shows Brian deployed his pilot chute into his chest at the 5-second mark rather than into clean airflow to the side. The PC became trapped against his body for approximately 2 seconds, and when it finally released at the 7-second mark, the canopy had insufficient time to fully deploy before impact at 8.5 seconds. The jumper intended to deploy but failed in the execution of throwing the PC into clean air, which is the fundamental technique requirement for PC deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: Brian had not maintained his BASE jumping or skydiving in the decades since his 1966 El Capitan jump. Despite receiving refresher training prior to Bridge Day 2006, the narrative explicitly states he had not kept up either discipline. This extended absence from active jumping left him without the practiced motor skills and muscle memory needed for a clean PC deployment under real conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Task Saturation
L2_preconditions.description: Video shows Brian performed an unintentional backflip after exit, requiring him to recover stability before deploying. Managing recovery from an unexpected unstable exit on a slider-up span jump likely consumed cognitive and physical capacity, contributing to the poor PC deployment technique (deploying into his chest rather than into clean air to the side).

L0_description: The narrative describes a pilot chute in tow with the bridle tightly wound around the jumper's ankle, resulting in a closed-container impact. The bridle entanglement around the ankle indicates the PC was not deployed into clean air effectively, likely compounded by a slightly head-low body position at deployment. The cause of death is listed as malfunction/misrigging, and the deployment failure chain represents identifiable human error in the deployment process.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of total malfunction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a total malfunction with the container remaining closed and the bridle tightly wound around his ankle. Witnesses reported the pilot chute appeared too small (suggesting a possible retained packing aid), but post-accident examination found the pilot chute free of any packing aids. The bridle-ankle entanglement could have resulted from a deployment technique error (e.g., the slightly head-low body position at pitch contributing to the bridle wrapping around the ankle) or from a rigging/packing error, but the narrative does not provide sufficient detail to distinguish whether this was a decision error or a performance/skill-based error.

L0_description: The jumper did not begin tracking until very close to the outcropping, resulting in insufficient forward separation from the cliff. He then deployed too late to avoid cliff strike during canopy opening. The delayed initiation of tracking and late deployment are identifiable human errors that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late tracking initiation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper launched back-to-earth and did not initiate tracking until very close to the outcropping. The narrative indicates he failed to begin tracking in time to achieve adequate separation from the wall, which is a performance/execution failure in the timing and initiation of a known required action. By the time he recognized the problem and deployed, he had insufficient separation and struck the cliff during canopy opening.

L0_label: Late deployment from unstable back-to-earth filming exit
L0_description: The jumper exited on his back to film another jumper, became very unstable while attempting to turn face-to-earth, and extracted his pilot chute too late. This resulted in insufficient separation from the cliff, leading to object strike under canopy. The back-to-earth exit for filming, unstable body position, and late deployment constitute identifiable human errors per the domain reference's warnings about aerials, video as risk factor, and deployment from non-standard body positions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment due to delayed recovery from back-to-earth position
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Edgar exited on his back to film the other jumper and then attempted to turn face-to-earth while very unstable, resulting in a late and likely poorly executed deployment. The witness observed the unstable transition, and the delayed extraction of the pilot chute left insufficient altitude and separation for the canopy to fly clear of the wall. The domain reference explicitly identifies back-to-earth exits as having 'loss of altitude awareness' as a known serious failure mode, and deploying from unstable/non-belly-to-earth positions risks poor openings and entanglements.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Back-to-earth filming exit from cliff
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Edgar chose to exit a slider-up cliff on his back to film the other jumper. This decision was clearly and specifically inappropriate: the domain reference identifies back-to-earth exits as inherently dangerous due to loss of altitude awareness, and aerials from any object exponentially increase danger. Combining a back-to-earth exit with the task of filming another jumper at a cliff requiring object separation created a situation where even competent execution was unlikely to produce adequate separation, as the jumper sacrificed tracking time and object-avoidance capability for camera work. The narrative notes this was the fifth fatality involving flying back-to-earth with a camera.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Edgar was focused on filming the other jumper during freefall, which diverted his attention from altitude awareness, body position management, and timely deployment. The narrative explicitly lists 'Focusing on filming' as a possible factor, and the domain reference identifies back-to-earth exits as producing loss of altitude awareness — a problem compounded by the cognitive demands of operating a camera during the critical freefall phase.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Exiting on his back and attempting to transition to face-to-earth while very unstable would have degraded Edgar's ability to perceive his orientation, altitude, and proximity to the wall. The domain reference identifies loss of altitude awareness as a known serious failure mode of back-to-earth exits, and the witness described the transition as very unstable, consistent with vestibular disorientation contributing to the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate Equipment Configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The witness noted Edgar was not wearing a jumpsuit or tracking clothes. For a slider-up cliff jump requiring tracking for object separation, the absence of tracking-appropriate clothing reduced his ability to generate separation from the wall after recovering from the back-to-earth position, directly contributing to insufficient clearance at deployment.

L0_description: The narrative provides very few details about what actually occurred during the jump. While the possible factors list suggests off-heading, ledge strike, late deployment, and line twists, the narrative itself states details are few and the circumstances were initially unclear. The cause of death was heart failure following a severe leg injury, but there is insufficient information to determine whether human error or other causes led to the injury-producing event. The specific sequence of events during the jump cannot be reliably reconstructed from the available information.

The narrative reports this was a low pull, with the possible factor being that the pilot chute was extracted too late. Deploying too late from a 1000-ft antenna constitutes a human error in deployment timing that directly caused the fatal ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to fully deploy before ground impact. Associates confirmed this was a low pull. The jumper had prior experience on this object and conditions were good, indicating the intended action (deploying the PC) was correct but execution was fatally delayed.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 90+ degree off-heading opening followed by further canopy surge into the dam wall, resulting in fatal object strike. The narrative reports good weather conditions and two prior successful jumps from the same object. No information is provided about the jumper's experience level, equipment details, packing quality, body position at deployment, or any other factor that would allow identification of a specific human error or non-human cause for the off-heading. Off-headings can result from multiple causes, and the evidence is insufficient to determine which applied here.

The jumper opened his canopy but was unable to reach the planned dry landing zone, landing in cold water approximately 100 feet from shore. This failure to reach the LZ suggests errors in jump planning, wind assessment, or canopy flight that led to a water landing without adequate preparation for water survival, resulting in drowning from hypothermia.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient information on cause
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper opened his canopy but was unable to reach the planned dry landing zone and landed in cold water approximately 100 feet from shore, ultimately drowning from hypothermia. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the failure to reach the LZ resulted from a decision error (e.g., inadequate wind/conditions assessment, poor jump planning) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., poor canopy piloting, delayed heading correction after an off-heading opening). Multiple possible factors exist — wind conditions (light and variable with snow), possible off-heading opening, unfamiliar new container, canopy performance — but the narrative does not establish which was the producing mechanism.

L0_description: The jumper chose to jump in 32-39 mph winds described as 'very strong and extremely turbulent' — conditions so severe that experienced local jumpers considered it impossible anyone would jump. The domain reference establishes that few objects are good above 10 mph and none above 20 mph. The fatal PC/bridle entanglement was wind-induced, resulting from jumping in conditions far exceeding accepted practice. Additionally, the bridle was found wrapped around the jumper's hand, indicating a deployment technique error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in extreme winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump in 32-39 mph winds described as 'very strong and extremely turbulent' — conditions so severe that all local experienced jumpers considered it impossible anyone would jump. The domain reference states few objects are good above 10 mph and no objects are good above 20 mph; these winds were roughly double that threshold, representing a clear and specific departure from accepted practice that directly produced the fatal conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Evidence indicates the bridle was found wrapped around the jumper's hand, and impact occurred with a closed container but fully deployed PC. This indicates the jumper failed to cleanly release the pilot chute into airflow — holding the PC too long or failing to execute a clean throw in the extreme wind conditions, resulting in a wind-induced bridle entanglement and total malfunction (PC in tow).

L2_preconditions.label: Extreme wind degraded PC deployment
L2_preconditions.description: The 32-39 mph winds with extreme turbulence directly degraded the jumper's ability to execute a clean pilot chute deployment. The high tailwind blew the bridle back under the arm/body, contributing to the entanglement. While the decision to jump in these winds is coded at L1, the wind also mechanically impaired the deployment execution itself.

L0_description: The jumper exited head-high and flew in a near-stall configuration for approximately 7 seconds, losing critical altitude during the start arc. This resulted in the remaining 22 seconds of flight occurring too low to deploy safely. The pilot chute was extracted too late, and the jumper impacted terrain during canopy extraction. The head-high exit, sustained near-stall flight, and resulting loss of altitude represent clear human errors in wingsuit technique.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor wingsuit exit and flight execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jimmy exited head high and remained in a near-stall configuration for 7 seconds, failing to achieve efficient flight promptly. This poor exit technique and slow-speed flight consumed critical altitude during the start arc, placing him too low to recover. The domain reference explicitly identifies exiting too head-high and flying too slowly/near-stall as common wingsuit execution errors that degrade start performance and kill available lift.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued flight at unsurvivable altitude
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the poor start consumed his altitude margin, Jimmy flew for approximately 22 seconds just above the snow in the couloir at an altitude too low to deploy safely, rather than deploying his parachute when he still had usable altitude. He matched his glide path to the terrain in a no-pull zone for 20 seconds before finally deploying, at which point impact was immediate. The decision to continue flying at an altitude where deployment could not produce a survivable outcome, rather than deploying earlier when altitude may still have permitted it, was the proximate mechanism of the fatal ground impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Jimmy had approximately 45 wingsuit BASE jumps and 700 skydives. The domain reference specifies a minimum of 100 wingsuit skydives (including 40-60 in the specific suit) and 100 BASE jumps before a first wingsuit BASE jump, plus extensive progression requirements. With 45 WS BASE jumps, he was still in early wingsuit BASE progression. The narrative describes classic early-progression wingsuit errors—exiting too head-high and flying in a near-stall configuration for 7 seconds—which the domain reference identifies as common mistakes stemming from insufficient skill development. This experience level directly contributed to the poor exit and inefficient flight execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: Jimmy flew for approximately 22 seconds just above the snow in the couloir at an altitude too low to pull safely, yet continued flying and only deployed after 20 seconds of terrain-matching flight. This sustained flight in a no-pull zone without earlier deployment is consistent with the jumper failing to accurately perceive his altitude and proximity to terrain relative to minimum deployment altitude. The snow-covered couloir environment may have contributed to visual misperception of terrain proximity, impairing his ability to recognize he was already below survivable deployment altitude.

L0_description: The jumper successfully deployed and flew his canopy but drowned after landing in water when the parachute pulled him under. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether landing in water resulted from a human error (e.g., poor landing approach, inadequate planning, failure to cut away) or from factors outside reasonable control (e.g., unforeseeable wind shift, unavoidable water landing at this site). Without information on the landing area characteristics, whether water landing was planned or unplanned, whether the jumper had flotation, or why he was unable to separate from the canopy, neither 001HE nor 002OC can be adequately supported.

The jumper struck the cliff under canopy 230 meters below the exit point, indicating insufficient object separation. Multiple human error factors are identifiable: the jumper exited in marginal wind conditions after a 3+ hour weather hold, had minimal experience at slider-up cliffs (65 total jumps mostly from short antennas), extracted the pilot chute too late, and did not achieve enough forward separation on exit. The decision to jump in marginal crosswind conditions with limited cliff experience represents identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in marginal wind conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The group waited over 3 hours due to very strong headwinds, and when they jumped the winds were described as only 'marginally ok' at 4-5 mph. Given the jumper's limited cliff experience (only 3 cliff jumps, with remaining 62 jumps from 60-80 meter antennas), jumping a slider-down cliff in marginal wind conditions after prolonged strong winds represented a clearly inappropriate decision. The crosswinds at exit contributed to the fatal off-heading and object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient exit separation and late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff and extracted the pilot chute too late (1-2 second delay with insufficient push). With only 3 prior cliff jumps and 62 jumps from short antennas, the jumper lacked the cliff-specific skills to execute the exit and deployment with sufficient object separation. The result was an object strike under canopy only 230 meters below the exit, with the jumper found hung up with toggles in hands, indicating the canopy opened but with insufficient separation to avoid the cliff face.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient cliff experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 3 prior cliff jumps, with the remaining 62 BASE jumps from 60-80 meter antennas. Antenna jumps of that height provide no sub-terminal tracking practice, no experience managing object separation from terrain, and no experience with the exit-to-track skills required for slider-down cliff jumping. This insufficient cliff-specific experience directly contributed to the failure to achieve adequate forward separation and the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste after long weather hold
L2_preconditions.description: The group waited over 3 hours due to very strong headwinds before jumping in conditions described as only 'marginally ok.' The prolonged weather hold created psychological pressure to jump when conditions finally improved slightly rather than continuing to wait or walking down, contributing to the decision to jump in marginal conditions.

L0_label: Wingsuit terrain flight loss of control
L0_description: The jumper flew into a narrow valley requiring a sharp turn to exit, lost stability during the aggressive turn (right arm wing folded under), and was unable to recover before impacting terrain. The decision to fly into the valley and the resulting loss of control during the turn constitute identifiable human errors in flight path selection and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of control during turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: While executing a sharp right turn to exit the 3rd valley during proximity wingsuit flight, the jumper's right arm wing folded under and he lost stability. Despite attempting to correct, he remained unstable and impacted terrain approximately 2 seconds later. The execution of the turn itself was the producing mechanism — the hard bank caused the wing to collapse, leading to unrecoverable instability and fatal impact.

L0_description: The jumper had insufficient forward separation from the wall, requiring an early pitch between 5-7 seconds instead of reaching full terminal airspeed. He then experienced a 180-degree off-heading opening facing the wall, resulting in fatal object strike. The insufficient separation indicates a human error in exit/tracking performance, and the decision to never wear a helmet removed a protective factor that could have mitigated the outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff during freefall, resulting in being too close to the wall at deployment time. The narrative states he 'was too close to the wall to continue freefall' and had to pitch early. This insufficient separation — whether from a poor exit, inadequate tracking, or both — meant that when the canopy opened with a 180° off-heading, he had no margin to avoid object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Off-heading not corrected
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper opened with a 180° off-heading facing the wall and was unable to correct heading before striking the wall. While the lack of separation was the primary producing mechanism, the inability to correct the off-heading in the available time contributed directly to the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: No helmet worn
