L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper made four attempts to locate and extract the BOC-stowed pilot chute but failed on each attempt, resulting in ground impact in a stable box position with no canopy deployment. The execution of the pull sequence failed despite the jumper's clear intent to deploy, constituting a performance/skill-based error in extracting the PC from the BOC pocket.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'was not well rested, had only a few hours of sleep.' Sleep deprivation degrades motor coordination and reaction time, directly contributing to the inability to locate and extract the pilot chute across four attempts.

L2_preconditions.label: Winter clothing restricted BOC access
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'had winter clothing on him, which made reaching PC more difficult.' The bulky winter jacket physically impeded the jumper's ability to reach the BOC pocket, directly degrading execution of the pull sequence.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known BOC access problem
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'already had problems with reaching his PC in that jacket before once, but got lucky that time.' The jumper was aware of a prior near-miss involving the same jacket restricting BOC access and failed to take corrective action such as switching to hand-held deployment or changing clothing.

L0_description: The jumper had a slightly unstable exit that turned his body to the right, remained in this poor position for several seconds before recovering and tracking, then deployed at approximately 7 seconds on a wall where the recommended freefall time was around 5 seconds due to an un-outtrackable ledge at 8 seconds. The late deployment after prolonged failure to recover from the unstable exit constitutes clear human error that directly produced the fatal impact with the ledge.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with delayed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Sergio had a slightly unstable exit that turned his body to the right. He remained in this off-axis position for several seconds before slowly recovering and initiating his track, consuming critical freefall time needed for object separation. This delayed recovery and late initiation of tracking directly resulted in insufficient separation from the cliff when he deployed at approximately 7 seconds, leading to impact with the ledge during canopy opening.

L0_description: The jumper with reportedly 20-50 BASE jumps attempted a slider-up cliff exit classified as requiring wingsuit or experienced tracking skills. The jumper failed to achieve sufficient forward separation from the cliff, experienced a 180-degree off-heading opening, and struck the wall under canopy. This represents a clear progression error—jumping an object beyond the jumper's skill level—combined with insufficient tracking performance.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped object beyond experience level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had only 20-50 BASE jumps and attempted a slider-up cliff exit (High Nose) that is classified as requiring wingsuit or experienced tracker skills. The narrative explicitly states this exit requires experienced tracking ability, and the jumper's low jump count indicates insufficient experience for this object. The decision to jump this specific object was clearly inappropriate for the jumper's experience level, and this decision produced the fatal outcome through insufficient object separation leading to object strike after a 180° off-heading.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking and separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve sufficient forward separation from the cliff through tracking, and experienced a 180° off-heading opening, resulting in object strike under canopy. The insufficient separation meant that even a moderate off-heading would have been life-threatening, and the 180° off-heading was fatal.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 20-50 BASE jumps and attempted a slider-up cliff exit classified as requiring wingsuit or experienced tracker skills. Accepted progression norms require substantial cliff experience and proven tracking ability before attempting objects with mandatory high-performance tracking. This insufficient experience level directly contributed to the decision to jump an object beyond the jumper's capability.

L0_description: The jumper became incapacitated during flight and tumbled to impact with no deployment attempt. Two plausible explanations exist: a sudden medical event rendering him unconscious, or a dynamic stall during a turn that flipped him and caused loss of consciousness. The narrative explicitly states the cause of the abrupt nosedive will never be known exactly. Neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be established with sufficient confidence over the other.

The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening resulting in object strike on a slider-down cliff jump. The narrative explicitly states the jumper did not have enough experience for slider-down jumping and did not know his limits, indicating human error in the decision to attempt a jump beyond his skill level. Insufficient forward separation on exit further contributed to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve sufficient forward separation on exit from the cliff, as noted in the possible factors. This insufficient push/separation, combined with a 180-degree off-heading opening, resulted in object strike with the wall. The jumper survived the initial impact but died when the canopy dislodged after 30 minutes, causing a fall of up to 60 metres.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'was reported to not have enough experience jumping slider down.' This insufficient experience with slider-down cliff jumping directly contributed to the inadequate exit technique that failed to generate sufficient forward separation from the object.

L0_label: Loss of glide leading to terrain impact
L0_description: The jumper was wingsuit proximity flying near terrain and experienced a loss of performance (glide) in the second part of the flight, leaving insufficient altitude to clear a plateau. Despite having time to deploy, the jumper either pulled too late or failed to pull, resulting in terminal freefall impact. The decision to continue flying toward terrain without adequate margin and the failure to deploy the parachute when the performance dropped constitute human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of no deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted terrain in terminal freefall while flying a wingsuit. His canopy was found with slider attached and brakes stowed, and witnesses could not confirm whether the impact occurred before any pull attempt, during a late pull, a failed pull attempt, or due to another malfunction. The narrative explicitly states 'we don't know what exactly happened: no pull / late pull / failed attempt to pull / other malfunction,' making it impossible to distinguish between a decision error (e.g., delayed pull decision due to loss of altitude awareness) and a performance error (e.g., failed PC extraction or late execution).

L0_label: Wingsuit terrain strike during proximity flight
L0_description: The jumper was flying a tight proximity line near terrain with a wingsuit (Vampire 4) he had limited experience on (~12 jumps). He misjudged his ability to clear a large ledge, attempted a sharp turn he had not practiced in this suit, and impacted the ledge in terminal freefall. The decision to fly a tight proximity line with insufficient experience on the specific wingsuit, and the misjudgment of clearance over the terrain feature, constitute identifiable human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Misjudged terrain clearance during turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a tight proximity line and attempted to clear a large, wide ledge. Witnesses believe he realized at the last second he would not clear it and initiated a sharp turn. The turn was insufficient to avoid the ledge, resulting in terrain impact. The jumper had only ~12 jumps on the Vampire 4 wingsuit and had not practiced sharp turning maneuvers in it, despite being highly skilled in his previous S-Fly suit. The execution failure was in misjudging the clearance margin and being unable to execute the emergency turn with adequate precision in the relatively unfamiliar suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience on wingsuit type
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had approximately 12 jumps on the Vampire 4 wingsuit after transitioning from an S-Fly, which had a completely different flying style. He had not practiced sharp turning maneuvers on the V4. This insufficient experience on the specific suit directly contributed to his inability to execute the emergency turn needed to avoid the ledge, a maneuver he could perform 'on a dime' in his previous suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from prior suit mastery
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the jumper as 'one of the fiercest wingsuit pilots on the planet' in his S-Fly suit, and witnesses believe that because his flight was going well he 'went back into hardcore mode' and chose to fly a tight proximity line. His established identity as an elite proximity pilot in a different suit appears to have driven him to fly at a level inconsistent with his limited experience on the V4, overestimating his capability in the new platform.

L0_description: The jumper exited in a wingsuit and turned left into the cliff wall after approximately 4 seconds, impacting subterminal near the exit point. The narrative identifies an unstable exit as a possible factor, and the immediate leftward deviation into terrain indicates a failure to maintain proper exit trajectory and flight control. This represents a human error in exit execution and/or flight management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to cliff strike
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and turned left into the cliff wall, impacting after approximately 4 seconds of freefall. The narrative and possible factors indicate an unstable exit that resulted in the jumper failing to establish controlled flight away from the wall. The canopy opened only after impact, confirming the jumper never recovered from the failed exit to achieve adequate separation from the terrain.

L0_description: The narrative describes a wingsuit proximity flight resulting in terrain impact, with possible factors listed as katabatic airflow, loss of speed-glide, and proximity flying. However, the narrative lacks sufficient detail to determine whether the impact resulted from a human error (such as a decision error or skill-based error) or from an unforeseeable environmental event (such as unexpected katabatic flow). The vague statement that the jump 'can catch even the most experienced out by losing your margin for safety in a few small moments' does not establish what specifically caused the loss of margin. Without information on the jumper's experience level, specific flight decisions, or whether conditions were forecastable, neither 001HE nor 002OC can be adequately supported.

The jumper was proximity flying in 'attack mode' aggressively near terrain and impacted the ground in terminal freefall. The aggressive proximity flying near terrain features, including executing a turn around a corner with insufficient margin, constitutes a human error in flight path management and risk assessment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Terrain strike during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying aggressively in 'attack mode' during a proximity wingsuit flight along a technical left-hand line near terrain. While executing a left-hand turn around a terrain feature, he impacted a grass field near a chalet, dying from terminal freefall impact. The cause of death (impact in terminal freefall with no parachute deployment) indicates a failure in execution during the proximity flight — either misjudging clearance from terrain, losing adequate speed/lift margin during the turn, or encountering conditions that degraded flight performance during the maneuver. The jumper chose a line he was experienced with and conditions were good; the fatal outcome resulted from how the flight was executed rather than from the decision to fly the line.

L0_description: The jumper attempted proximity flying for the first time at an unfamiliar cliff, despite deliberating extensively about whether to do it. Multiple decision errors are identifiable: flying proximity on a first jump at the site without having seen the line from the landing area, leading to deployment at a point too close to terrain. The resulting 180° off-heading with line twists left no margin for recovery, causing fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Off-heading opening with line twists
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed his parachute and experienced a 160° off-heading opening with multiple line twists. While under canopy with line twists preventing effective heading correction, the canopy flew toward the wall resulting in multiple object strikes and fatal impact. The off-heading with line twists was the direct mechanism producing the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature deployment near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed his parachute at a point approximately 30m after and 30m below a ledge while flying proximity to terrain on his first jump at this cliff. Witnesses assessed that the deployment location was driven by panic or stress, and that deploying at this point left insufficient clearance from the wall to survive an off-heading. The decision to deploy at this specific point near the wall, rather than continuing flight to gain more separation, was the producing mechanism that placed him in a position where the off-heading was fatal rather than recoverable.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses reported the jumper was thinking about whether to fly proximity to the ledge the entire evening before and during the hike, indicating unresolved ambivalence about the task. His companions assessed the premature deployment was driven by panic or stress. The narrative also references the motivation of capturing a nice video as a factor in the decision-making chain.

L2_preconditions.label: Task Saturation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was flying proximity for the first time, at an unfamiliar cliff he had never seen from the landing area, in a wingsuit. The combination of novel proximity flying, unfamiliar terrain, and the need to manage flight path, terrain clearance, and deployment timing simultaneously likely exceeded his cognitive capacity, contributing to the panic-driven premature deployment near the wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 35 wingsuit BASE jumps on a Phantom 2, the jumper attempted proximity flying past a ledge at an unfamiliar cliff for the first time. Accepted progression norms require extensive wingsuit BASE experience before terrain flying, including intimate knowledge of the specific line. His limited wingsuit BASE experience was insufficient for the demands of proximity flight near terrain, contributing to the panic-driven premature deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Site Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first jump at Kandersteg. He had not seen the landing area or the ledge from below. The domain reference emphasizes that terrain flying requires intimate knowledge of the entire line, reconnaissance from multiple perspectives, and gradual progression at a specific site. His lack of site familiarity directly contributed to deploying at an unsafe point near the wall.

L0_description: The updated report identifies that the pilot chute was entangled with a stash bag compression cord, resulting in PC in tow and only partial canopy extraction. A stash bag compression cord interfering with the deployment system indicates a rigging or preparation error - foreign equipment (the compression cord) was left in a position where it could entangle with the pilot chute during deployment. This represents a human error in equipment preparation, specifically failing to remove or properly secure the stash bag compression cord before jumping.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown deployment failure cause
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a fatal deployment malfunction where the pilot chute extracted only approximately 10 feet of canopy that remained long and thin without inflating, resulting in full-speed impact. The updated report identifies the pilot chute was entangled with a stash bag compression cord. While this points to either a packing/rigging error or a gear check omission, the narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the jumper made a specific decision error (e.g., deliberately rigging the stash bag cord in a hazardous configuration) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., failing to properly secure or remove the compression cord during packing). The jumpers did not perform mutual gear checks, but a gear check omission is a failure to detect an existing problem rather than the producing mechanism itself.

L0_description: The jumper was unable to locate and deploy the pilot chute at sufficient altitude while flying a wingsuit, resulting in impact with no canopy deployed. The narrative identifies the inability to pull the PC as the cause, with the contributing factor that the suit did not fit well. A poorly fitting wingsuit affecting BOC access represents a human error pathway involving equipment selection/configuration and deployment execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and deploy PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate the pilot chute from the BOC pocket in time and impacted the ground with nothing deployed. The narrative states she was unable to pull the PC at sufficient altitude, indicating an execution failure during the deployment sequence rather than a decision not to deploy. The ill-fitting Vampire 4 wingsuit likely contributed to difficulty reaching the BOC, but the fatal mechanism was the failed execution of the pull.

L2_preconditions.label: Ill-fitting wingsuit not corrected before jump
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had told people that her new Vampire 4 wingsuit did not fit well. An ill-fitting wingsuit can restrict movement and impair the ability to reach the BOC pocket, directly contributing to the failed PC deployment. Jumping with equipment known to fit poorly constitutes inadequate equipment configuration for the planned jump.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying close to the wall and lost his safety margin, impacting talus. The narrative describes him 'slowly closing his window of safety fast' while flying close to terrain, indicating a loss of speed/glide during proximity flight. This represents a human error in flight management — flying too close to terrain without adequate margin, consistent with the identified possible factors of loss of speed-glide and proximity flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient speed/glide near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit in proximity to terrain and was 'slowly closing his window of safety fast,' indicating a progressive loss of speed and glide performance during the flight. He impacted the talus he was attempting to fly over, demonstrating that his execution of the proximity line failed to maintain adequate speed and separation from terrain. The narrative describes a gradual degradation in flight performance rather than a flawed plan — he was attempting a line that was within his intended progression but failed to maintain the energy management required to clear the terrain.

L0_label: Loss of speed leading to low pull
L0_description: The jumper lost speed and was sinking out during the second half of his wingsuit flight, consistent with flying too slowly (near stall). He had recently changed wingsuits and was not maximizing the new suit's performance. He deployed his pilot chute too late, resulting in an off-heading opening with insufficient altitude, leading to impact. The loss of speed/glide and late deployment constitute identifiable human errors in wingsuit management and deployment decision-making.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide in wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper lost speed and began sinking out during the second half of his wingsuit flight, indicating he was not maintaining adequate airspeed and flew into or near a stall condition. Fellow jumpers had noted in weeks prior that he was not maximizing the performance of his recently changed wingsuit. This loss of speed/glide resulted in insufficient altitude for canopy deployment and flight, leading to what was effectively a terminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After losing speed and sinking out, the jumper deployed his pilot chute too late. While the canopy did inflate, it opened with a 140+ degree off-heading at an altitude insufficient to correct or land safely, resulting in impact into a gully behind a cliff band.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience with new wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: Sean had recently changed wingsuits and fellow jumpers had noticed in the weeks prior that he was not maximizing the new suit's performance. This indicates he had not yet developed proficiency with the specific suit, which directly contributed to his loss of speed/glide during the flight. The domain reference states a minimum of 40-60 skydives with a specific suit before BASE use, and that upgrading wingsuits before fully utilizing current suit performance is a recognized error.

L0_label: Unstable wingsuit exit from technical cliff
L0_description: The jumper exited a highly technical, underhung cliff (High Ultimate) in a wingsuit and immediately went head-down with excessive amplitude, lost control, and impacted terrain near the exit. The narrative identifies an overly forceful push resulting in an unrecoverable head-down position, constituting an exit execution error. Additionally, the jumper's experience level (approximately 105 BASE jumps, limited wingsuit BASE experience) at an exit recommended only for experienced tracking jumpers represents a decision error in object selection relative to skill level.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper pushed off harder than normal from the ramp, resulting in a head-down position with feet exceeding vertical with great amplitude. He was unable to recover stable flight from this unstable exit, entered a spin, and impacted a ledge. The exit technique execution was the direct producing mechanism — the intended action was a controlled wingsuit exit, but the physical execution (excessive push force, resulting head-down attitude) was inadequate.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had approximately 105 BASE jumps over 1-2 seasons and 70 wingsuit BASE jumps. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 regular BASE jumps with a long record of perfect exits before first wingsuit BASE, and the narrative notes the exit (High Ultimate) is recommended for experienced tracking only. The jumper's relatively rapid progression through wingsuit BASE at a demanding exit point indicates insufficient experience for the task attempted, directly contributing to the inability to manage the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Underhung exit geometry
L2_preconditions.description: High Ultimate is described as underhung, meaning the terrain below the exit extends outward, reducing the margin for recovery from a poor exit. The ramp was introduced that same day. This terrain geometry directly reduced the time and space available to recover from the head-down position, converting a recoverable exit error into a fatal impact with a ledge.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying along a wall in a wingsuit and impacted a ledge after 10 seconds of flight, with loss of speed-glide identified as a possible factor. Proximity flying deliberately reduces margin from terrain, and loss of speed/glide during terrain flight represents a failure to maintain adequate energy management — a human error pathway clearly identifiable from the domain reference's extensive discussion of wingsuit terrain flying, available lift, and the dangers of slow flight near terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to maintain adequate speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was proximity flying along a wall in a Vampire 4 wingsuit and impacted a ledge after 10 seconds of flight. The possible factors list 'Loss of speed-glide,' indicating the jumper failed to maintain adequate airspeed and glide performance during execution. The jumper's flight path intersected terrain due to insufficient speed management, resulting in terminal freefall impact with a ledge.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a significant loss of wingsuit performance (very little forward speed, high fall rate) yet failed to deploy his parachute despite having plenty of altitude at that point. He continued flying until he was very low over trees, deploying too late for the canopy to fully open before impact. This late deployment decision constitutes a clear human error. Additionally, his progression profile (70 BASE jumps, only 10 wingsuit BASE jumps, first jump at this technical cliff) falls well below accepted wingsuit BASE progression norms, indicating inadequate experience for the jump undertaken.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment after performance degradation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Carl's wingsuit performance rapidly degraded with very little forward speed and high fall rate, yet he had plenty of altitude to deploy at that point and did not. He continued flying and eventually extracted the pilot chute very low over trees at approximately 100m above ground, resulting in insufficient altitude for canopy inflation and terminal impact. The failure was in execution: he failed to deploy the parachute when altitude was available despite the clearly degraded flight conditions, indicating an attention/monitoring failure regarding altitude and the need to abandon the flight plan.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Carl had approximately 70 BASE jumps and only 10 wingsuit BASE jumps. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 regular BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE, and 100 wingsuit skydives (including 40-60 in the specific suit). With only 10 wingsuit BASE jumps, Carl lacked the experience to recognize and respond to degrading flight performance by immediately deploying. The narrative explicitly highlights his low jump numbers and insufficient wingsuit BASE experience as a contributing factor.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention
L2_preconditions.description: Despite having plenty of altitude to deploy when his performance degraded, Carl continued flying and did not pull until approximately 100m above trees. This pattern is consistent with channelized attention on the flight path or terrain ahead (including the power lines he passed over) rather than monitoring altitude and recognizing the need to abandon the flight. His fixation on continuing the flight despite clearly inadequate performance delayed the deployment decision until it was too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Carl was flying a wingsuit over a long talus slope on his first jump from this cliff. The gradually sloping terrain beneath him during degraded flight performance likely impaired his ability to accurately perceive his altitude and closure rate with the ground, contributing to his failure to deploy when he still had adequate altitude. Unfamiliarity with the visual references at this site compounded the difficulty of altitude assessment during a high-sink-rate flight.

L0_description: The narrative states the jumper was found at the bottom of the antenna with the canopy extracted but not inflated, and the possible factors field identifies 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late.' The cause of death was low pull, indicating the jumper failed to deploy the pilot chute at a survivable altitude. This constitutes a human error in deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was found at the base of the antenna with the canopy extracted but insufficient altitude remaining for inflation. The possible factors indicate the pilot chute was extracted too late, constituting a performance/execution error in deployment timing from a slider-down antenna jump.

L0_description: The jumper impacted the cliff wall shortly after exit on his second wingsuit BASE jump of the day, indicating a failure to achieve adequate flight performance or separation from the object. With only 10 wingsuit BASE jumps and 40 wingsuit skydives (well below the accepted minimum of 100 wingsuit skydives), the jumper was significantly under-experienced for wingsuit BASE jumping. A witness observed him hit the cliff near the exit point, and the narrative notes a suspected problem with the exit, consistent with a poor exit leading to insufficient flight initiation and subterminal wall impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable/poor wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted the wall near the exit point at subterminal speed, indicating a failed wingsuit start. On his first jump he had been noted for 'slightly bent legs' which was discussed as a concern, and on the fatal second jump witnesses saw him hit the cliff shortly after exit. This indicates a performance failure during the exit — likely an insufficient or unstable launch that prevented the wingsuit from generating adequate lift and separation from the cliff face.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only ~10 wingsuit BASE jumps and ~40 wingsuit skydives. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps with perfect exits before first wingsuit BASE. With 40 WS skydives (well below the 100 minimum) and only 10 WS BASE jumps, the jumper lacked the experience to reliably execute wingsuit exits from cliffs, directly contributing to the exit performance failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Exit Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: On the first jump of the day, the jumper exhibited bent legs during the exit — a recognized wingsuit exit error that degrades start performance. This was discussed between jumps and the jumper was reminded ('Think of your legs!') before the fatal second jump, yet the same or a related exit performance failure occurred. The jumper proceeded without adequately correcting a deficiency identified minutes earlier.

L2_preconditions.label: Snow-covered exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports 120 cm of snow at the top. A deep snow layer on the exit surface can degrade footing and launch stability, directly impairing the jumper's ability to execute a strong, stable wingsuit exit push — contributing to the failed start.

L0_description: The jumper exited unstably from a cliff requiring a decent push to clear a small outcropping, clipped a bush/ledge after approximately 1 second, tumbled into the wall, and impacted fatally. The unstable exit and insufficient push to clear known terrain features represent identifiable human errors in exit execution and/or site selection relative to skill level.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with insufficient push
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited unstably and failed to generate sufficient push to clear a known small outcropping approximately 1 second after exit. She clipped a bush/ledge, tumbled into the wall, and impacted the cliff face at subterminal speed. The exit required a decent push to clear the outcropping, and the execution was insufficient to achieve the necessary separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Terrain outcropping near exit
L2_preconditions.description: A small outcropping with bush/vegetation existed immediately below the exit point, requiring a decent push to clear. This terrain feature provided essentially zero margin for an imperfect exit, meaning the skill-based execution error directly resulted in cliff strike within approximately 1 second.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a sudden jolt and uncontrolled oscillation during wingsuit flight for unknown reasons. Possible factors listed include gear failure and proximity flying, but the narrative cannot confirm either. The body was recovered weeks later in extremely difficult terrain, and the cause of the oscillation remains undetermined. There is insufficient information to establish a human error pathway or to positively identify a non-human cause.

L0_label: Wingsuit terrain flight in adverse conditions
L0_description: Multiple human errors are clearly identified in the narrative: jumping in strong winds whose importance was 'trivialized by confidence,' flying an unflown proximity line decided moments before exit without adequate planning, insufficient experience on the specific wingsuit (only 10 jumps on the suit), physical fatigue from extended hiking, and flying proximity terrain without adequate speed/glide margin. The jumper impacted terrain in terminal freefall without attempting deployment, consistent with loss of speed and glide in headwind conditions that were known but dismissed. These represent clear decision errors and preconditions within the jumper's control.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flying proximity line in excessive wind
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper committed to a proximity flying line along a ridge in strong headwind conditions that reduced airspeed and available lift, making terrain clearance physically impossible. The narrative explicitly states the wind's importance was 'trivialized by our confidence in our big suits,' the line had never been flown before, and the co-jumper directly experienced the 'strong head wind and subsequent lack of speed' that forced them to bail into the canyon. Flying a never-flown proximity line in strong winds that degraded wingsuit performance below what was needed to clear terrain was a decision that was clearly and specifically inadequate for the conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper did not appear to have attempted to pull his pilot chute despite approximately 30 seconds of flight time before impacting the ridge. Whatever the reason for the non-deployment — loss of altitude awareness, task saturation, or inability to recognize the need to abort — the failure to deploy the parachute was the direct mechanism of the fatal impact in terminal freefall.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the wind's importance was 'trivialized by our confidence in our big suits.' This overconfidence in equipment capability directly drove the decision to proceed with the proximity line despite strong winds that objectively degraded wingsuit performance below terrain-clearance requirements.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion
L2_preconditions.description: Fernando had hiked twice the day before and commented on 'feeling that second hike' during the 3.5-hour approach on jump day, which involved the entire altitude of the jump done on foot. This cumulative physical fatigue may have degraded his capacity to recognize the need to deploy or execute a deployment during the flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate flight planning
L2_preconditions.description: The three-way formation and flight path were decided at the exit moments before jumping without time for mental preparation. The line had never been flown before. This failure to adequately plan and brief the flight — especially a novel proximity line in strong winds — directly enabled the decision to commit to an unvetted line without proper assessment of terrain clearance requirements.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit experience on type
L2_preconditions.description: Fernando had only 10 total jumps (BASE and skydive combined) on the X Three wingsuit. With so few jumps on this suit, he lacked the experience to understand its performance envelope in adverse conditions, directly contributing to committing to a proximity line that required performance he could not reliably extract from the suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong headwind degraded wingsuit performance
L2_preconditions.description: The strong headwind reduced airspeed and available lift during flight, as directly experienced by the co-jumper who noted 'strong head wind and subsequent lack of speed.' This environmental degradation of wingsuit performance compressed the time and altitude available for recognizing the need to deploy and executing a pull, contributing to the failure to attempt deployment.

L0_label: Unstable wingsuit exit from technical object
L0_description: The jumper exited a technical, under-hung exit point in a wingsuit and went head-down immediately after leaving the ramp, impacting a ledge below the exit. The narrative identifies an unstable exit, insufficient forward separation, and clipping a ledge as factors. The exit technique (pushed off rather than running, resulting in immediate head-down attitude) represents a human error in execution at a site that preferably required a running tracking exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited from a technical under-hung exit point using a wingsuit and went head-down immediately after leaving the ramp, failing to achieve a stable flying position. This unstable exit resulted in insufficient forward separation and direct impact with a ledge below the exit point. The narrative indicates the exit was a push-off rather than the preferred running/tracking exit for this technical site, and the jumper failed to maintain the body position necessary to initiate wingsuit flight.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying a wingsuit over a ridge and was visibly considerably lower than another jumper flying the same line, indicating loss of speed/glide performance. He impacted terrain in freefall before deploying his canopy. Flying a line over a ridge with insufficient altitude/energy margin to clear it represents a decision and/or execution error in wingsuit terrain flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient speed/glide over ridge
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper selected a line over a nearby ridge but was visibly considerably lower than a second jumper flying the same line, indicating he failed to maintain adequate speed and glide performance during the flight. The possible factors list 'Loss of speed-glide' and the cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, consistent with the wingsuit losing performance and the jumper being unable to clear or separate from terrain. The decision to fly the ridge line was shared by at least one other jumper who completed it successfully, indicating the line itself was flyable and the failure was in execution rather than plan selection.

L0_description: The jumper attempted a double gainer, under-rotated, and deployed while mostly on his back. The canopy snagged on his belly camera during extraction from this non-belly-to-earth body position, resulting in a PC in tow / failure to inflate. The decision to perform an aerial that resulted in an unstable/back-to-earth deployment, combined with the presence of a snag-hazard camera, constitutes identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Under-rotated aerial causing unstable deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Luke attempted a double gainer but under-rotated, completing only approximately 1.5 rotations. This left him in an unstable, back-to-earth position from which he pitched the pilot chute while twisting his torso to face the ground. The resulting body orientation and deployment from his back led to the canopy snagging on his belly camera, causing a total malfunction (failure to inflate) and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Belly camera as snag hazard
L2_preconditions.description: Luke was wearing a belly-mounted camera that created a snag point during deployment. The canopy or bridle snagged on the camera during extraction while he was on his back, converting the under-rotated aerial into a total malfunction. A camera mounted on the belly is a known entanglement hazard during non-standard body orientations at deployment, and its presence enabled the fatal outcome from the skill-based error.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states the bridle was routed through the lateral flap of the harness, which is an incorrect rigging setup that caused a pilot chute in tow and prevented canopy deployment. This bridle misrouting is a human error in rigging/equipment assembly that directly caused the total malfunction and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Incorrect bridle routing
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper routed his bridle through the lateral flap of his harness (the strap connecting the leg harness to the back of the container), which prevented the canopy from deploying when the PCA was executed. This rigging error during setup produced a pilot chute in tow / total malfunction that was unrecoverable at the jump altitude, directly causing impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The bridle was visibly routed through the lateral flap of the harness, and after the PCA tightened the bridle, the bottom pin opened with canopy partially visible inside the container. A thorough pre-jump gear check — tracing the bridle from anchor to container as accepted PCA practice requires — would have detected this incorrect routing before exit.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper's exit was unstable and to the left, putting him too close to the wall, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit. An unstable exit in wingsuit BASE is a human performance failure that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's exit was unstable and deviated to the left, putting him too close to the wall. This execution failure directly resulted in subterminal impact with terrain near the exit point a few seconds later.

L0_description: The jumper was performing proximity wingsuit flying and impacted terrain in terminal freefall. The narrative explicitly states the jumper was flying too close to terrain without adequate experience in proximity flight, was fairly new to wingsuiting, and was rushing progression. This indicates human error in the decision to fly a proximity line beyond the jumper's skill and experience level.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity flying beyond experience level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit proximity line too close to terrain, resulting in terrain impact in terminal freefall with no deployment attempt. The narrative explicitly states the jumper had no experience in proximity flight and was fairly new to wingsuiting, making the decision to fly a close proximity line a clear and specific departure from accepted progression norms. The decision to fly that line at that distance from terrain was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit and proximity experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had 'no experience in proxy flight and fairly new to wingsuiting,' and describes the jumper as 'rushing to base jump and getting into the wingsuit far too early.' This indicates the jumper's training and experience were clearly insufficient for the proximity flying task attempted, directly contributing to the decision to fly a line beyond their capability.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying and flew too low through a turn, losing adequate altitude and separation from terrain. He clipped an outcrop, was flipped upside down, and impacted in terminal freefall despite deploying his PC. Flying too low to safely navigate the terrain constitutes a human error in flight path management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying very low to make the first turn and after the turn had put himself too low to fly out safely, clipping an outcrop that flipped him upside down. This indicates a failure in execution of the proximity line — specifically, inadequate speed/glide management through the turn resulted in insufficient altitude and separation to clear terrain. The jumper's flight technique failed to maintain the energy and trajectory needed to safely navigate the chosen line.

L2_preconditions.label: Anabatic/thermal airflow degradation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly notes this was the fourth jump of the day in the afternoon, and states that all fatalities at this site occur in the afternoon when airflow has changed and cold air moves into the valley. Anabatic flow and shifting valley wind conditions in the afternoon would have reduced effective glide performance and altered the aerodynamic environment along the flight path, directly degrading the jumper's ability to maintain speed and glide through the turn and subsequent terrain clearance.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying a wingsuit and attempted to clear a ridge/ledge ('First House of Card') but impacted terrain in freefall with nothing deployed. The narrative indicates loss of speed-glide as a possible factor, and the jumper failed to clear terrain he was attempting to fly over, suggesting a misjudgment of performance margin relative to terrain — a decision to fly a line that exceeded available performance.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew proximity line beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flight line that required crossing over a ridge ('the First house of card'). He impacted the terrain in terminal freefall with nothing deployed, indicating he committed to a line that required more glide performance than was available. The possible factors of 'loss of speed-glide' and 'proximity flying' combined with the narrative's emphasis on knowing limits indicate the chosen flight path was clearly inappropriate given the performance margin available.

L0_description: The narrative states the jumper impacted a ledge during wingsuit proximity flight. However, there is no information about the jumper's experience level, the specific line chosen, conditions along the flight path, what caused the impact (misjudgment, environmental factor, equipment issue, medical event), or any other details that would allow identification of a specific human error pathway versus a non-human cause. 'Proximity flying' is listed as a possible factor but the mechanism of the terrain impact is unspecified, and proximity flying alone is an activity choice, not evidence of a specific error without further context about the line, conditions, or the jumper's qualifications.

The jumper misjudged height relative to terrain ('wrong appreciation of the height') while flying a wingsuit proximity line over a grassy dome, resulting in insufficient clearance and fatal impact. This indicates a human error in altitude/terrain assessment during proximity flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Altitude/terrain misjudgment during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew a known wingsuit proximity line but had a 'wrong appreciation of the height' over a grassy dome feature along the flight path, resulting in insufficient clearance. This was an execution-level error in terrain awareness and altitude management during proximity flying, not a flawed decision about which line to fly. The misjudgment led to terrain impact at terminal speed.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had a 'wrong appreciation of the height' over a grassy dome, indicating a visual illusion or misperception of terrain proximity. Featureless grassy terrain is a recognized cause of visual illusions that impair altitude/distance judgment, directly contributing to the altitude/terrain misjudgment identified at L1.

L0_description: The jumper impacted terrain during a wingsuit proximity flight that was not witnessed by any BASE jumper, and no information was gained from rescue personnel. The weather was reported as ideal, the line was familiar and not considered challenging for his skill level. Without witness accounts, video evidence, or any information from rescue personnel, there is insufficient information to determine whether human error or another cause led to the fatal impact.

The narrative describes the jumper flying a wingsuit near the wall in proximity, coming too close to a prominent section of terrain, making a sharp turn to avoid it that caused loss of control (going head down), and being unable to recover before impact. This represents a human error in flight path management and terrain clearance judgment during proximity flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed terrain avoidance maneuver
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying near the wall and encountered a more prominent section of terrain. He initiated a turn to avoid it but executed the turn too sharply, causing the wingsuit to roll and pitch head-down. He was unable to recover from this attitude and impacted the slope in terminal freefall. The intended action (turning to avoid terrain) was correct, but the execution was flawed — the turn was too aggressive, inducing loss of control.

L0_description: The jumper was flying a proximity line and failed to clear trees, impacting in terminal freefall with no pull attempt. The narrative explicitly states he was a novice proximity flyer attempting a proximity line beyond his ability to safely execute, indicating human error in the decision to fly that line and/or in flight execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew proximity line beyond skill level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Dan was described as 'only a novice wingsuit Proxy flyer' who attempted a proximity line he failed to clear. The decision to fly a proximity line near trees as a novice proxy flyer was clearly and specifically inappropriate given his acknowledged lack of proximity flying experience, and this decision directly produced the fatal terrain impact. He was found in flight mode with no pull attempt, indicating the terrain strike resulted from the chosen flight path rather than an execution error during deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Proximity Flying Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Dan was 'only a novice wingsuit Proxy flyer' despite being a good wingsuit BASE jumper. Proximity flying near terrain requires substantially greater experience than standard wingsuit BASE, and his novice-level proximity skills were insufficient for the line attempted, directly contributing to his decision to fly a line he could not clear.

L0_description: The jumper flew a wingsuit proximity line into a crack in the terrain that was a dead end, mistaking it for a different feature approximately 20 meters further down the valley. This was a decision/execution error in terrain flying — flying into a blind feature without certainty of the exit route, resulting in impact with terrain at terminal velocity.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew proximity line into unknown terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The deceased chose to fly a proximity line into a crack/gully without adequate knowledge of where it led. He turned right into a dead-end crack that was approximately 20 meters from the correct line. Flying proximity to terrain features that the jumper cannot see around ('turning around blind features') without knowing exactly what is around the corner is a clearly inappropriate decision, as the jumper committed to a line with no escape route into terrain he had not verified, directly producing the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The deceased flew into the wrong crack, approximately 20 meters from the correct line. He misidentified the terrain feature he was flying into, believing it was the intended line when it was actually a dead-end crack. This geographic misidentification directly enabled his commitment to the fatal proximity line.

L0_description: The jumper was proxy flying and lost height awareness, resulting in impact with talus in terminal freefall. Loss of altitude awareness during proximity wingsuit flight is a human error in situational awareness and flight management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of altitude awareness during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was proxy flying and lost height awareness, resulting in impact with talus in terminal freefall without deploying the parachute. The narrative explicitly states he 'lost height awareness,' indicating an attention/monitoring failure during execution of the proximity flight rather than a flawed decision to attempt the flight itself.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Proxy flying demands intense focus on the terrain feature being flown past, which channels attention toward maintaining the flight line at the expense of monitoring altitude and terrain clearance. The narrative states the jumper 'lost height awareness,' consistent with attention fixated on the proximity task to the exclusion of altitude monitoring.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper's exit was 'too steep (unskilled)' and he was unable to get his wingsuit flying in time, resulting in subterminal impact with nothing deployed. The jumper attempted one of the most advanced wingsuit BASE exits in the world without the requisite skill, constituting clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose exit beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump an exit described as one of the top 3 most advanced wingsuit jumps in the world, which has killed even highly experienced wingsuit pilots with hundreds of WS BASE jumps. The narrative explicitly states the exit is 'not forgiving' and requires flawless exit and flying skills. The decision to attempt this specific exit was clearly and specifically inappropriate given the jumper's apparent skill level, as evidenced by the 'too steep (unskilled)' exit characterization. The decision itself — selecting this particular exit — was the producing mechanism, as even correct execution of a too-steep exit from this unforgiving launch would leave insufficient margin for recovery.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unskilled steep exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly states the exit was 'too steep (unskilled)' and the jumper was unable to get the wingsuit flying in time. The jumper failed to execute a proper wingsuit exit — exiting at too steep an angle prevented building sufficient airspeed for flight before terrain impact, resulting in subterminal impact with nothing deployed.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the exit as one of the top 3 most advanced wingsuit jumps in the world, one that has killed even advanced pilots with hundreds of WS BASE jumps. The jumper's exit is characterized as 'unskilled,' directly indicating that the jumper's experience and skill level were insufficient for this specific task. This insufficiency in capability contributed to the decision to attempt an exit that was clearly beyond the jumper's level.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS Exit Skill
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly characterizes the exit as 'too steep (unskilled),' indicating the jumper lacked the trained proficiency required for a proper wingsuit exit at this demanding site. The jumper was unable to transition from exit to flight, a skill that requires extensive wingsuit BASE experience and practiced technique at progressively more demanding exits.

L0_description: The jumper attempted a wingsuit BASE jump with only 23 total BASE jumps (only 10 prior to this trip), grossly insufficient for wingsuit BASE which requires a minimum of 100 BASE jumps. The jumper flew head-high and slow (likely stalled), extracted the pilot chute too late, and impacted the ground at an object with insufficient altitude for wingsuit flight. Multiple human errors in progression, object selection, and execution are clearly identified.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature wingsuit BASE attempt
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted wingsuit BASE jumping with only 22 prior BASE jumps (10 slider-down before the trip, plus 12 in the valley), far below the accepted minimum of 100 regular BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE. With only 10 slider-down jumps and no reported slider-up non-wingsuit cliff experience, the jumper lacked the foundational skills for wingsuit BASE. This decision to attempt wingsuit BASE at this experience level was a clear and specific departure from accepted progression norms.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unsuitable object selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper selected Yellow Ocean (350m rock drop, 425m to landing area) for a wingsuit flight. This exit lacks sufficient altitude for wingsuit flight, particularly for a novice wingsuit BASE jumper who would have a large start arc and poor speed management. The jumper assumed it was suitable for beginners without obtaining local site information or registering with the SBA, ignoring advice from experienced jumpers in the valley.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Head-high slow flight to stall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew the wingsuit in a very head-high, slow configuration likely at or near stall, as observed by witnesses. This is a recognized fatal error pattern in wingsuit BASE: flying too slowly bleeds energy, increases sink rate, enlarges the burble, and prevents effective deployment. The resulting loss of speed and glide led to insufficient altitude for canopy deployment, with the canopy reaching line stretch simultaneously with ground impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 22 total BASE jumps (10 slider-down prior to the trip plus 12 in the valley), with no reported slider-up non-wingsuit cliff experience. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 regular BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE. This profound experience deficit directly enabled both the decision to attempt wingsuit BASE and the selection of an unsuitable object.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's lack of wingsuit BASE experience and training directly produced the head-high slow flight pattern. Without the progression through sub-terminal tracking, slider-up cliff jumps, and extensive wingsuit skydiving specific to BASE preparation, the jumper lacked the skill to manage angle of attack and airspeed, resulting in near-stall flight — a recognized fatal error pattern that proper wingsuit BASE progression is designed to prevent.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper, a very experienced skydiver (AFF instructor, tandem master, thousands of skydives), ignored advice from fellow travelers and experienced jumpers in the valley, did not register for a landing card or obtain local site information from the SBA, and proceeded to attempt wingsuit BASE on approximately his 23rd BASE jump. This pattern of disregarding multiple sources of guidance and accepted progression norms indicates the jumper unreasonably overestimated their capability based on skydiving experience, which does not substitute for proper BASE training and progression.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to obtain site information
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper did not register for a landing card with the SBA and had no information from the SBA about exit profiles. Obtaining local site information is a standard preparatory action at Lauterbrunnen, and this failure directly contributed to the selection of Yellow Ocean as a wingsuit exit without understanding that its altitude profile was insufficient for wingsuit flight, particularly at the jumper's experience level.

L0_label: Loss of control during proximity flying
L0_description: The jumper lost speed and altitude after slipping sideways and going up on a wingtip during a competitive proximity wingsuit flight through gates. This represents a human error in flight management: the jumper flew a line that required precise maneuvering near terrain, lost airspeed during a turn (a recognized fatal failure mode in wingsuit BASE), and impacted terrain in freefall without deploying. Flying at speeds and angles that left insufficient margin to recover from the disruption constitutes a decision and skill-based error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of control during turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During a competitive wingsuit proximity flight, the jumper lost control after the first gate while setting up for the second gate. He slipped sideways, went up on his wingtip turning right, and lost speed and altitude, impacting trees in terminal freefall. The execution of the turn was the direct mechanism — the jumper failed to maintain stable flight and adequate airspeed through the maneuver.

L0_label: Unsuitable exit site for new wingsuit
L0_description: The jumper flew a new, larger wingsuit (R-Bird) for the first time from Via Ferrata, which is explicitly described as 'Not a Beginners Wing suit Exit,' despite having planned to test the suit at a more forgiving site (Brento). He exited unstable and never controlled his flight, impacting terrain subterminal. Peers noted his exits in his previous suit were already too head-high with insufficient speed. The decision to fly an unfamiliar larger suit from an unsuitable exit, combined with the unstable exit itself, constitutes clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate site for new wingsuit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a new, larger wingsuit (R-Bird) for the first time from Via Ferrata, which is explicitly described as 'Not a Beginners Wing suit Exit.' This was a clear departure from his own stated plan to test the R-Bird at Brento (a recognized beginner wingsuit exit). The decision to fly an unfamiliar, larger suit from a non-beginner exit was the producing mechanism: the site's demands exceeded what could be managed on a first flight in the new suit, and the resulting uncontrolled flight led to subterminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to uncontrolled flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited unstable and never recovered controlled flight before impacting talus. Other jumpers had previously observed that his exits in his smaller suit (Prodigy) were consistently too head-high and that he was not achieving adequate airspeed on exit. This execution deficiency — an exit technique that failed to establish controlled wingsuit flight — was the direct mechanism producing the fatal terrain impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Other jumpers in the valley had observed that most of his exits in his Prodigy were too head-high and that he was not flying as quickly as he should be on exit. This recurring exit technique problem — falling straight down rather than achieving adequate airspeed — was a known deficiency that was never corrected before he attempted a more demanding suit and exit.

L0_description: The jumper missed his first pull attempt and was unable to locate the pilot chute in time, resulting in impact at line stretch. The inability to locate and extract the pilot chute on the first attempt from a slider-down cliff object constitutes a human error in deployment execution, as the narrow altitude margins required a successful first pull attempt.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Missed pilot chute grab
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to deploy his pilot chute but missed on his first attempt to locate and extract it from the BOC. Although he found it on a subsequent attempt, the delay meant the canopy reached line stretch at or after ground impact. This was an execution error in a time-critical deployment sequence from a slider-down cliff jump.

L0_description: The jumper died from impact during wingsuit proximity flight. The pilot chute was out of the BOC but the container remained closed, indicating a deployment attempt that failed to extract the canopy. However, the narrative does not provide sufficient information to determine why the deployment failed or why the jumper was in a position requiring an emergency pull. While proximity flying is noted and the jumper was progressively flying lower, the specific mechanism — whether pilot error (misjudging terrain, flying too slowly, PC/bridle entanglement from poor deployment technique), equipment malfunction (pin lock), or another cause — cannot be determined from the available information.

The jumper deployed his pilot chute far too late, resulting in canopy extraction but no inflation before ground impact. The narrative and witness account indicate he tracked for too long and deployed right before impact, with speculation of lost altitude awareness or object fixation. This represents a clear human error in deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper tracked for too long and deployed his pilot chute right before impact, resulting in lines and canopy out but no inflation. The witness account and video review indicate he likely lost altitude awareness or became fixated on an object (a tree), failing to monitor altitude and initiate deployment at a survivable height. The jump plan itself (a 2-way with a roll maneuver from an antenna) was not inherently the producing mechanism; rather, the failure was in execution—specifically, the failure to deploy at the correct altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Video review and witness analysis concluded the jumper likely became fixated on an object (a tree near his impact point) or lost altitude awareness during the tracking phase. This attention management failure directly contributed to his failure to initiate deployment at a survivable altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper performed a roll onto his back and then back to belly during the multi-way exit sequence. This maneuver, combined with tracking at a steeper angle below his partner, likely disrupted his spatial orientation and altitude perception, contributing to his failure to recognize the need to deploy.

L0_description: The jumper lost altitude awareness during wingsuit flight and reached back to deploy far too late, impacting before the pilot chute was thrown. The narrative explicitly identifies altitude awareness loss and late extraction as factors, constituting human error in deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to deploy her pilot chute at a survivable altitude. The narrative states she reached back to pull around 15 seconds into the flight but was 'much too low and impacted before she threw her pilot chute.' This indicates a failure to monitor altitude during wingsuit flight, resulting in initiating the deployment sequence too late to complete it before ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor wingsuit flight performance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper 'did not build up much forward speed and was not out flying the talus,' indicating inadequate wingsuit flight technique. The poor flight performance — likely flying too slowly at too high an angle of attack — reduced her effective glide and contributed to her being too close to terrain when she finally attempted deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states this was Amber's first wingsuit BASE jump. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and at least 100 regular BASE jumps before a first wingsuit BASE jump. Her poor flight performance — failing to build forward speed and not outflying the talus — and her loss of altitude awareness are consistent with the fundamental inexperience of a first wingsuit BASE attempt. A first wingsuit BASE jump represents insufficient experience for the demands of wingsuit terrain flying, altitude management, and deployment timing in a wingsuit.

L2_preconditions.label: Altitude Awareness Failure
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'she must have lost altitude awareness,' and the possible factors list includes 'altitude awareness.' The jumper failed to monitor her altitude during wingsuit flight, reaching back to pull only when much too low. This loss of situational awareness regarding altitude directly enabled the late deployment. Task saturation from managing a first wingsuit BASE flight likely contributed to channelized attention away from altitude monitoring.

L0_description: The narrative provides only a URL link and no substantive details about the circumstances of the fatality. The cause of death is impact in terminal freefall (no canopy deployment), but there is no information about what led to this outcome — no details on experience, conditions, equipment state, or sequence of events. There is insufficient information to determine whether human error or other causes were involved.

The jumper attempted a double gainer from a slider-down cliff object. He completed the first rotation but stalled the second, losing altitude awareness. By the time he was belly-to-earth and initiated deployment, there was insufficient altitude remaining. The canopy was extracted but lines were still stowed at impact, confirming a low pull. The decision to attempt the aerial despite visible fear and hesitation, combined with failure to maintain altitude awareness and deploy in time, constitutes clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment after failed aerial
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a double gainer but stalled the second rotation. By the time he recovered to belly-to-earth orientation, he had consumed too much altitude and deployed too late. The canopy was extracted but lines were still stowed at impact, indicating insufficient altitude remained for the deployment sequence to complete. The fatal mechanism was the failure to maintain altitude awareness and deploy in time during the failed aerial maneuver.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper planned the double gainer as a tribute to his deceased friend Daniel Moore, who had done his first double from the same exit point. This emotional motivation to complete the aerial contributed to his proceeding despite visible fear and hesitation (shaking, going back and forth, screaming expletives), overriding the rational signals to abort.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Aerial
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's attention was focused on completing the double gainer rotation rather than monitoring altitude. When the second rotation stalled, his 'Oh shit!' exclamation upon returning to belly-to-earth indicates he only recognized the altitude deficit at that moment, consistent with channelized attention on the aerial task at the expense of altitude awareness.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The tumbling motion of the double gainer, particularly when the second rotation stalled, likely caused vestibular disorientation that impaired the jumper's ability to perceive altitude and orientation. His verbal reaction upon regaining belly-to-earth position indicates he did not recognize his altitude situation until visual reference was restored, by which point it was too late.

L0_label: Failed to turn away from cliff
L0_description: The narrative explicitly states the canopy opened on-heading (facing Lauterbrunnen, parallel to the cliff) and the jumper did not turn the canopy away from the cliff. Flying parallel to a cliff without initiating a turn to gain separation is a clear failure to execute object avoidance, resulting in impact with the talus slope.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to initiate canopy turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a straight, on-heading opening facing Lauterbrunnen (parallel to the cliff), the jumper failed to turn the canopy away from the cliff face. The narrative explicitly states 'she didn't turn the canopy' despite having a functioning, on-heading parachute. This failure to execute a necessary heading correction or object-avoidance maneuver resulted in continued flight parallel to the cliff and fatal impact with the talus.

L0_description: The jumper made two sequential errors during proximity wingsuit flight: turning approximately 100° instead of the planned 70°, which left him low, and then attempting a sharp right turn to still clear the ridge rather than deploying or aborting. The companion's account explicitly identifies these as the two decisions that consumed all available margin, resulting in terrain impact in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued toward ridge after altitude loss
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the initial over-rotation (~100° instead of 70°) left the jumper already low, he made a conscious decision to turn sharp right and attempt to fly over the ridge rather than deploying or flying to open airspace. This second decision committed him to a flight path with insufficient altitude to clear the terrain, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. The companion's account explicitly frames this as a sequential decision: he had margin to survive the first error but the decision to still attempt the ridge crossing was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L0_description: The jumper suffered a 180° off-heading opening with line twists and insufficient separation from the cliff, resulting in fatal object strike under canopy. With only 30 BASE jumps and 1 season of experience, jumping a slider-up cliff object indicates the jumper was at an early stage of progression where off-heading management and object separation skills may not have been adequately developed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to achieve adequate separation and correct off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper opened with a 180-degree off-heading and line twists, with insufficient separation from the cliff. The possible factors indicate the jumper failed to track adequately for object separation and/or failed to correct the off-heading in time, resulting in fatal object strike under canopy. With 30 BASE jumps and 1 season of experience at a slider-up cliff, the execution of tracking and deployment produced inadequate separation, and the off-heading with line twists prevented effective correction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 30 BASE jumps and 1 season of experience while attempting a slider-up cliff jump requiring sub-terminal tracking, off-heading correction skills, and line twist management. Per accepted progression norms, slider-up cliffs are among the most complex BASE objects, requiring well-developed tracking and object avoidance skills typically built through extensive practice at simpler objects. With 30 total BASE jumps, the jumper's experience level was clearly insufficient for the demands of this task, directly contributing to the inadequate separation and inability to correct the 180-degree off-heading with line twists.

L0_label: Aerial at night with equipment interference
L0_description: The jumper performed an aerial (gainer) from an antenna at night with inadequate lighting, resulting in either late pilot chute extraction or bridle/equipment entanglement leading to a total malfunction. Multiple human errors are identifiable: performing an aerial at night (greatly increasing disorientation and altitude awareness loss), jumping with a stash bag whose elastic strap could interfere with deployment, and choosing an awkward exit position that contributed to poor rotation. The domain reference explicitly identifies loss of altitude awareness and bridle entanglements as known risks of aerials, compounded here by darkness.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late or failed deployment during aerial
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a gainer from an awkward exit position that likely caused slow over-rotation or stall. The pilot chute was extracted but the pins were not pulled, indicating either a late pull (insufficient altitude remaining) or bridle entanglement with the stash bag's elastic strap. The execution of the aerial and subsequent deployment attempt failed to produce a functioning parachute before impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was performed at night at a very dark site with inadequate ground lighting and minimal moonlight. The narrative states that aerials can be disorienting even on a sunny day, and that in the low light conditions he may have lost orientation or altitude awareness during the rotation. This visual impoverishment during an aerial maneuver would have degraded his ability to judge when to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Stash bag configuration hazard
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper placed a stash bag on his back beneath the rig with an elastic adjusting strap that had approximately 6 inches of free slack when empty. The elastic line was found pulled taut, indicating it likely interfered with pilot chute extraction. This equipment configuration created a snag hazard that contributed to the deployment failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions at night
L2_preconditions.description: The site was very dark, ground lighting had not been recently replaced, and the moon was low or already set. These conditions directly impaired the jumper's ability to maintain spatial orientation and altitude awareness during the aerial maneuver, contributing to the late or failed deployment.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening on a slider-down cliff jump, corrected heading with rear risers, but upon releasing the riser the canopy surged forward into an underhung portion of the cliff. The forward surge upon riser release indicates the deep brake setting may not have been properly tuned — per the domain reference, a correctly tuned DBS should have the canopy flying as close to stall as possible, minimizing forward surge. This points to a human error pathway in equipment configuration or deployment/correction technique.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate riser release technique
