L1_unsafe_acts.description: After correcting a 180° off-heading with rear riser input, the jumper released the riser in a manner that caused the canopy to dive forward and surge him into an underhung portion of the cliff. The abrupt release of rear riser input after a full heading correction generated a forward surge that drove the jumper into the rock face. This is a technique/execution error in the transition from heading correction back to normal flight, rather than a decision error.

L0_description: The jumper exited unstable from a cliff and impacted the wall seconds after exiting, with insufficient forward separation. The unstable exit and failure to achieve adequate object separation represent human error in exit execution, directly causing the fatal wall strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited unstable from a cliff and failed to achieve adequate forward separation, impacting the wall only a few seconds after exit. The unstable exit was a performance/execution error that directly produced the fatal outcome by preventing the jumper from tracking away from the object.

L0_description: The jumper continued wingsuit flight despite progressively losing altitude and being low relative to terrain. He had 'plenty of time to open safely' but delayed deployment until approximately 20m above ground, resulting in a fatally low pull. This represents a human error in the decision to continue flying rather than deploying the canopy when the deteriorating situation was apparent.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment decision
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued flying his wingsuit despite progressively losing altitude and being clearly too low to clear the terrain ahead. Video footage confirmed he was already low after exit and was 'slowly and regularly losing altitude' throughout the flight. He had 'plenty of time to open safely' but chose to continue flying until approximately 20m from the ground, at which point deployment was too late. This was a decision error — the jumper recognized or should have recognized the deteriorating situation over an extended period but chose not to deploy, making the delayed deployment the producing mechanism of the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the jumper's behavior as 'almost inexplicable' — he had plenty of time and altitude to deploy safely but continued flying as altitude progressively deteriorated. The companion speculates about possible 'brain lock' and wanting to 'enjoy the ride as long as he could.' This pattern is consistent with channelized attention on continuing the flight/completing the line to the exclusion of monitoring altitude margin and making the deployment decision. The sustained failure to act over an extended period despite obvious cues suggests fixation rather than a simple misjudgment.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'it was a while since his last jump' despite 10 BASE seasons of experience. The companion speculates he may not have been 'jumping regularly enough to realize he was in a bad situation right after the exit.' Lack of currency in wingsuit BASE flying could have degraded his ability to accurately assess his altitude margin and glide performance in real time, contributing to the delayed deployment decision.

L0_label: Jumped in excessive wind at night
L0_description: The jumper chose to jump in conditions that exceeded site safety limits - winds were 'beyond the measures they have in place for this site' and it was near nighttime (9pm). The narrative describes an unstable track, poor body position at deployment, line twists, and resulting cliff strike, but the decision to jump in conditions explicitly violating site rules constitutes clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in excessive wind beyond site limits
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly states that winds were 'beyond the measures they have in place for this site' - meaning the jumper chose to jump when wind conditions exceeded the established site safety limits. This decision to jump in conditions that violated site rules was the producing mechanism: excessive wind at a cliff object degrades tracking performance, contributes to unstable body position at deployment, and causes off-heading openings with insufficient separation, directly producing the fatal object strike under canopy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable track and poor deployment body position
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper had a 'poor unstable track' followed by pulling 'having a bad body position,' resulting in line twists and a 180 off-heading. Even given the wind conditions, the execution failures in tracking and deployment body position were the direct mechanism producing insufficient object separation and the off-heading opening that led to cliff strike. The jumper was also 'slow to react' to the off-heading/line twists, failing to execute timely heading correction.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper was rushing to jump because it was getting dark ('don't be in a rush to jump because it is getting dark'). The time pressure of fading daylight at 9pm drove the decision to jump in winds that exceeded site limits rather than waiting for better conditions or walking down.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong winds degraded tracking and deployment
L2_preconditions.description: Winds beyond site safety limits directly degraded the jumper's ability to maintain a stable track and achieve proper body position at deployment. Excessive wind at a cliff object mechanically impairs tracking performance and body stability, contributing to the poor unstable track and bad body position at pull time described in the narrative.

L0_label: Loss of speed/glide during proximity flight
L0_description: The jumper was flying a technical wingsuit proximity line and experienced a loss of speed-glide, resulting in insufficient altitude/separation to clear terrain (the Zebrata/climbing wall). The panic pull just before reaching the terrain feature indicates the jumper recognized the situation too late. Flying a technical proximity line that resulted in inadequate clearance from terrain constitutes a human error pathway, as the jumper placed himself in a situation requiring performance that was not achieved.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Abraham was flying a technical wingsuit line near terrain and lost speed and glide performance, resulting in insufficient clearance to pass the Zebrata/climbing wall. The narrative indicates he experienced a loss of speed-glide during proximity flying, consistent with flying too slowly or at too high an angle of attack, which degraded his available lift and glide ratio to the point where terrain clearance was impossible.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late emergency deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Abraham attempted an emergency deployment just before reaching the terrain obstacle but extracted the pilot chute too late for the canopy to inflate before impact. The panic pull at the last moment indicates he recognized the situation but was unable to execute a timely deployment, resulting in impact in terminal freefall with no functioning canopy.

L0_description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff during the exit/track and extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in impact with a ledge 250-300m below the exit point during terminal freefall. Multiple witnesses and the narrative identify poor projection/tracking and delayed PC extraction as the contributing factors, both of which are human performance errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient track/separation from cliff
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a running exit from a 450m cliff in slick configuration but failed to achieve adequate forward separation through tracking. The jumper impacted a ledge 250-300m below the exit point, described as hitting 'in the middle of the ledge,' indicating insufficient horizontal distance gained during freefall. Witnesses described the track/projection as 'too poor' and 'weak.'

L0_label: Wingsuit control loss during proximity flight
L0_description: The jumper was flying a proximity line with a newer wingsuit whose flight characteristics at highly aggressive angles differed from his previous suit, and he lacked sufficient experience with the new suit at these angles. During the flight, he exposed too much top surface to relative wind, then made an abrupt AoA correction that caused a high-speed stall and fatal tumble. This sequence reflects identifiable human errors in suit familiarity, line selection, and in-flight technique.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: While flying Line 2 at Brevent, Shaun exposed too much of the top surface of his wing to the relative wind during a transition from a hard dive, creating a downward force. His abrupt correction to angle of attack resulted in a high-speed stall and loss of stability, leading to an unrecoverable tumble to impact. The execution errors were in managing the dive-to-flight transition — specifically the over-exposure of the wing's top surface and the abrupt AoA correction — rather than in the decision to fly the line itself.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience on new suit at aggressive angles
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that while Shaun was hyper-current and experienced in wingsuit BASE, he did not have as much experience flying his particular new suit at the highly aggressive angles required for the Brevent lines. The new suit had different flight characteristics from his previous suit, specifically in how a pilot would fly very steep lines. This insufficient familiarity with the suit's behavior at extreme angles directly contributed to the technique errors in managing the dive-to-flight transition — the over-exposure of the wing's top surface and the abrupt AoA correction that produced the fatal stall.

L0_description: The jumper exited from a wet, muddy exit point that was still draining water, at a site described as lacking suitable handholds for wingsuit flyers. The jumper slipped on exit, struck positive cliff terrain immediately, tumbled, and never recovered. The decision to jump from a known-wet exit point in rain conditions, at a site unsuitable for wingsuit exits, constitutes human error in judgment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit / slip
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper slipped on the wet, muddy exit point during a wingsuit launch, immediately striking positive cliff terrain. The slip resulted in an unrecoverable tumble and subterminal impact near the exit. The execution failure (loss of footing) was the direct mechanism that produced the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Wet/muddy exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the exit point was still draining water and conditions were very wet and muddy after many rainy days. This degraded the exit surface traction, directly causing the jumper to slip during the wingsuit launch.

L0_description: The narrative confirms a PC in tow / PC-bridle entanglement as the cause of death, but provides no information about what caused the entanglement. The possible factors list mentions 'gear failure' alongside 'pilot chute entanglement,' but the narrative does not clarify whether this was due to a deployment technique error (weak toss, holding PC too long), equipment malfunction independent of human error, or some other cause. Without information on the jumper's experience, equipment condition, or deployment execution, there is insufficient basis to determine whether human error or a non-human cause produced the fatal PC in tow condition.

The jumper exited from a technical exit requiring clearing a ledge and achieving efficient flight. Despite a strong push, his trajectory did not match his push, resulting in a head-low, left-bank body position almost immediately after exit. His corrective action (dropping leg wing) stalled the suit, causing a flat spin and fatal impact with the ledge and talus below. The exit body position failure at a known technical exit constitutes a human error in execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit / failed start
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Despite a strong push, Alex's trajectory did not match his intended flight path and he was almost immediately in a 'head low, left bank' body position. This unstable exit failed to achieve the efficient start required to clear the ledge below the exit point. His attempted correction (dropping the leg wing aggressively) stalled the suit and induced a flat spin, resulting in ledge impact at subterminal altitude.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying a line described as demanding good control that 'does not allow loss of flight,' and impacted terrain after losing speed/glide. Choosing to fly a line with zero margin for error and subsequently failing to maintain adequate flight performance constitutes human error in both decision-making and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide in flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit proximity line that 'demands good control and does not allow loss of flight.' He experienced a loss of speed-glide during the flight, resulting in insufficient performance to clear terrain (crête de la Chalavreu), leading to impact in terminal freefall. The chosen line required sustained adequate flight performance, and the jumper failed to maintain the necessary speed and glide to complete it.

L0_description: The jumper was alone and there were no witnesses. The narrative explicitly states that the circumstances at the exit are 'only speculation' and the proposed scenario of an exit problem leading to off-heading/object strike is described as 'total speculation.' While possible factors are listed (anabatic airflow, unstable exit), these are speculative rather than evidence-based. There is insufficient information to definitively identify a human error pathway or to establish a non-human cause.

The narrative reports that the jumper appeared to stall his wingsuit approximately 600 meters before the entry to a crack during proximity flying and was unable to recover, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. Flying too slowly near terrain leading to a stall is a well-documented human error pattern in wingsuit BASE, where slow flight near stall depletes available lift and makes recovery impossible. The domain reference explicitly identifies slow-speed flight near terrain as a recognized fatal error mode.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wingsuit stall without recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Witnesses reported the jumper stalled his wingsuit approximately 600 meters before the entry to a crack feature and was unable to recover, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. This indicates a performance failure in airspeed/AoA management during proximity flight — the jumper lost speed and available lift, entered a stall condition, and failed to recover to controlled flight. The domain reference identifies flying too slowly and stalling as a known execution failure mode in wingsuit BASE, where speed loss is easy and recovery requires altitude that may not be available near terrain.

L0_description: The jumper continued flying a proximity line toward a ledge despite losing altitude/glide margin, and by the time he recognized he would not clear it, attempts to fly away and deploy were too late. The narrative explicitly identifies the failure to abort at a reference point as the lesson learned, indicating a decision error in continuing the line beyond the point of safe recovery.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to abort proximity line at decision point
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued flying a proximity line toward a ledge despite losing speed/glide, failing to abort at the point where safe clearance was still possible. The narrative explicitly states that the jumper could have aborted and flown away from the ledge at the right time to gain altitude, but he continued to fly and when he realized he would not make it over, it was too late. The lesson cited by the eyewitness is having a reference point where you abort your line or pull. This was a decision to continue a committed proximity line past the abort point, not an execution error — the jumper flew the suit as intended but chose to press on rather than abort when adequate margin still existed.

L0_description: The jumper attempted to fly a left line too early, misjudging the start arc which was longer than anticipated. This resulted in loss of speed/glide during proximity flying and high-speed terrain impact. The decision to commit to a terrain-proximity line that exceeded the jumper's available performance margin constitutes a human error in flight planning and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed terrain flight execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to fly a left line too early, misjudging the start arc which was longer than anticipated. This execution error during proximity wingsuit flight resulted in insufficient altitude/separation from terrain, leading to high-speed impact. The jumper intended to fly the line but failed in the cognitive execution of timing the transition, misreading the start arc performance relative to the terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper misjudged the start arc, which was longer than he thought, indicating he misperceived his position and trajectory relative to the terrain during the critical transition to the left line. This misperception of spatial position directly contributed to the failed terrain flight execution.

L0_description: The jumper failed to execute a sufficiently strong exit push from an exit point that commanded a hard push, as demonstrated by rock-throw timing and the first jumper's technique. This weak push resulted in insufficient separation from the cliff, causing the jumper to strike a ledge approximately 100 meters below the exit at around 4.5 seconds. The decision to jump was influenced by watching another jumper succeed rather than independently assessing his own ability to execute the required exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit push
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly states that the exit commanded a strong push, the first jumper pushed hard and cleared the terrain, but Donald did not push hard enough. At 90kg he did not generate sufficient speed to clear the first ledge at approximately 100 meters / 4.5 seconds from the exit. The intended action was to clear the terrain, but the physical execution of the exit was insufficient, resulting in impact with the ledge.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Donald's decision to jump was influenced by watching the first jumper successfully clear the exit, which gave him confidence despite not feeling fully comfortable. The narrative states he 'probably decided to jump because the first guy did and made it' and that he 'got the confidence to jump by watching the first guy get off.' This borrowed confidence from observing another jumper's performance served as a misplaced motivational driver, contributing to his proceeding with an exit he did not execute with sufficient force.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: Donald was listening to music at the exit point, which prevented him from hearing repeated attempts by his companion on the observation deck to communicate with him. This external distraction eliminated the opportunity for critical information exchange about exit technique requirements before he jumped, directly contributing to his inadequate exit execution.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying in a tracking suit along a known line and failed to deploy with sufficient altitude. The narrative explicitly states he extracted the pilot chute too late and there was not sufficient altitude for deployment, indicating a human error in deployment timing and/or flight path management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a known tracking suit line near terrain and failed to deploy the pilot chute with sufficient altitude remaining. The narrative states he realized he was low and attempted to pitch, but there was not sufficient altitude for deployment, indicating a failure in altitude awareness and deployment timing during execution of the flight rather than a flawed plan selection.

L0_description: The narrative is absent ('None'), and the structured fields provide only minimal details: wingsuit BASE from an antenna with snow conditions and a possible factor of 'extracted pilot chute too late' leading to a low pull. Without a narrative or supporting detail, there is insufficient information to deduce a clear human error pathway or to confirm a non-human cause. The 'possible factors' field alone is not a confirmed finding.

The jumper rigged his static line system using degraded break cord, potentially compromised by tearing rather than cutting, with attachment points far toward the end of the bridle increasing dynamic forces, and without a pilot chute as backup. The primary static line knot slipped free and the secondary break cord broke prematurely, resulting in the canopy not reaching full line stretch. These rigging decisions and equipment choices constitute human errors in setup and configuration.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate static line rigging method
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Channing chose to rig his static line using improvised methods that departed from accepted practice in multiple ways: he used a spectra line and rock as an anchor, employed two pieces of break-cord tied together rather than a single proper length, attached the static line connections far toward the end of the bridle (increasing dynamic loading), used old/degraded break-cord that had been torn apart rather than cut, and elected not to use a pilot chute as backup. These were conscious rigging decisions that collectively created a system prone to failure. The primary knot slipped free and the secondary break-cord broke prematurely at the intermediary knot, resulting in the canopy not reaching full line stretch and a closed-container impact from 140 feet.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: No pilot chute backup
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Channing deliberately chose to static line without a pilot chute for backup. Accepted practice calls for the PC to never be stowed during a static line jump, as it serves as a last line of defense if the static line system fails. Had a pilot chute been available, it could have extracted the canopy after the static line rigging failed. This decision removed the only remaining means of canopy deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Channing used old and degraded break-cord that had been torn apart rather than cut, compromising its integrity before use. He rigged an improvised static line system with two short pieces of break-cord tied together and attached far toward the end of the bridle, increasing dynamic loading. These specific preparation failures created the conditions that caused both the primary knot to slip and the secondary cord to break prematurely.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a closed-container impact caused by a container lock (pin lock malfunction). Multiple human error factors are identifiable: the jumper constructed his own bridle with a floating pin design, the closing loop was found large enough to admit the pin eye (indicating improper loop sizing or rigging), packing was rushed, and no gear check was performed prior to exit. The domain reference identifies floating pin bridles as having known fatal malfunction tendencies, homemade equipment modification as dangerous, and gear check omission as a failure to catch existing problems.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Rigging/packing execution error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Bryan constructed his own bridle with a floating bottom pin configuration and packed/rigged the system such that the bottom pin locked in the closing loop, resulting in a total malfunction (closed container impact). The intact closing loop was found large enough to admit the pin eye, and various malfunction modes were discovered on similarly configured containers. The pin lock prevented canopy extraction despite normal PC inflation and full bridle extension, directly producing the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: No pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Bryan did not receive a gear check from any other jumper prior to exit. A gear check is an accepted practice that could have detected the rigging condition (e.g., closing loop large enough to admit the pin eye, or improper pin/loop interaction) that caused the pin lock malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste
L2_preconditions.description: Bryan was slightly rushed to finish his pack job because the other jumpers had finished packing and were returning to the bridge, and he wanted to ride in their van back to the top. This time pressure contributed to the conditions under which the rigging error occurred and the gear check was omitted.

L0_description: The jumper extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in impact at line stretch. The narrative and possible factors explicitly identify altitude awareness failure and late PC extraction as contributing to the fatal low pull.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in impact at line stretch. The possible factors explicitly cite 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late' and 'Altitude awareness,' indicating a failure in execution timing rather than a deliberate decision to pull at that altitude. The jumper had sufficient experience (250 BASE jumps, 700 skydives) and appropriate equipment for a slider-up tracking suit jump, suggesting the intended course of action was sound but execution of the deployment was fatally delayed.

L0_description: The jumper performed a burning parachute cutaway stunt and separated from the burning parachute too low, resulting in insufficient altitude for his normal BASE canopy to inflate before impact. The late separation represents a human error in timing/execution of the deployment sequence during a self-planned high-risk maneuver.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late separation and deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jim separated from the burning parachute too low, and although he deployed his normal BASE canopy normally, there was insufficient altitude for it to inflate before impact. The narrative states he 'extracted pilot chute too late' — the execution of the separation and deployment sequence was delayed beyond the point where survival was possible, indicating a performance/timing error during execution of the planned sequence.

L0_description: The narrative identifies an unstable (head-low) exit and late pilot chute extraction as contributing factors. The jumper deployed too late to achieve a functioning canopy before impact, resulting in death from a low pull. The witness did not hear a canopy opening, and the jumper was found wrapped in his canopy 60 meters from the cliff base, consistent with deployment at insufficient altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / low pull
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The cause of death is listed as 'low pull' and possible factors include 'extracted pilot chute too late.' The jumper exited with a slightly head-low attitude (unstable exit) and failed to deploy the parachute at a survivable altitude. He was found wrapped in his canopy 60 metres from the base of the cliff, indicating the canopy was extracted but far too late for adequate inflation and deceleration. The execution failure was in not deploying the PC at the correct time despite presumably intending to do so.

L2_preconditions.label: Cloud cover obscured visual references
L2_preconditions.description: Low-level clouds were present at exit altitude, and the hole in the clouds closed immediately after exit. Loss of visual ground references due to cloud immersion would have directly impaired the jumper's ability to assess altitude and judge correct deployment timing, contributing to the late deployment.

L0_description: Both jumpers died from impact during wingsuit proximity flight through a terrain feature ('the notch') that required near-perfect execution with minimal margin. The decision to fly a line where clearing the ridge by only ~200ft in the best case constitutes a human error in flight planning and execution. Graham made an unexplained hard turn into the right side of the notch and impacted; Dean likely impacted a tree on the downhill side, suggesting insufficient clearance through the notch.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose high-commitment proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Both jumpers chose to fly through 'the notch,' a line described as requiring near-perfect execution with approximately 200ft clearance over a ridge in the best case scenario, where the narrator states 'very few people in the world are capable of' the required exit and flight. The line offered minimal margin — even a perfect flight cleared the ridge by only ~200ft — and the narrator, a close peer, had explicitly told both jumpers the line 'wasn't worth it' because it offered no meaningful gain. This was a deliberate selection of a flight path with essentially zero margin for any deviation from perfection, constituting a decision that was identifiably and substantially inadequate given the known constraints.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Graham's sudden turn toward notch
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Graham made a 'sudden hard right towards the notch for unknown reasons,' passing in front of Dean and impacting the right side of the notch. The narrative explicitly states it is unclear whether this was an intentional course correction, a response to perceived inability to clear the upper ridge, or caused by an unknown weather condition. There is insufficient information to determine whether this was a decision error or an execution/control error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Dean's impact cause unclear
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Dean made it through the lower portion of the notch but 'likely impacted a tree' on the downhill side. It is unclear whether Dean was burbled by Graham's pass in front of him, was already too low, or experienced some other factor. The narrative states it is 'impossible to know' the specific mechanism, making it insufficient to classify as decision or performance error beyond the initial line selection.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator describes the notch line as 'the new standard of being an alpha male in the valley' and compares the jumpers to 'moths to a flame,' indicating that social status and competitive motivation within the local wingsuit community drove the decision to fly this line despite its minimal margin and lack of meaningful flight benefit.

L0_description: Both jumpers chose to fly a wingsuit line through 'the notch,' a terrain feature requiring near-perfect exit and glide with approximately 200ft of clearance over a ridge. The narrative describes this as an extremely challenging flight that 'very few people in the world are capable of,' and a fellow jumper had warned them the line 'wasn't worth it.' Graham made an unexplained hard turn toward the notch and impacted the right side; Dean made it through the lower portion but likely impacted a tree, indicating insufficient altitude/glide. The decision to fly this high-risk proximity line with minimal margin constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose high-commitment proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Both jumpers chose to fly through 'the notch,' a line described as requiring near-perfect execution with approximately 200ft clearance over a ridge in the best case scenario. The narrator, a close associate, had explicitly told both jumpers the line 'wasn't worth it' and that it offered no meaningful benefit to the flight. Despite this, both committed to a line where the margin between survival and impact was extremely thin, and where even minor deviations in exit quality, glide performance, or unexpected interactions (such as burble from another jumper) left no room for recovery. The decision to fly this specific line was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome — both died from terminal impact into terrain features within the notch.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Graham's sudden turn toward notch
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Graham made a sudden hard right turn toward the notch for unknown reasons after initially setting up on the left side. The narrative explicitly states it is unclear whether this was intentional repositioning, a reaction to perceived inability to clear the upper ridge, or caused by an unknown weather condition. This maneuver placed him on a collision course with terrain and may have contributed to Dean's fatal outcome through burble or by forcing Dean into a compromised position, but insufficient information exists to classify the specific error type.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator describes the notch line as 'the new standard of being an alpha male in the valley' and compares the jumpers to 'moths to a flame.' This indicates a social/competitive motivation — maintaining status among elite wingsuit pilots in Yosemite — that drove both jumpers to commit to a line that an experienced peer had explicitly told them was not worth the risk.

L0_description: The narrative reports a wingsuit proximity flight ending in terrain impact with no parachute deployment, but critical details are missing: the jumper's experience level, the specific flight line, what caused the loss of flight performance, and the camera (which would have contained evidence) was lost on impact. While 'Loss of speed-glide' and 'Proximity flying' are listed as possible factors, there is insufficient information to determine whether this resulted from a human error (such as flying too slowly, poor line choice, or inadequate experience) versus an unforeseeable event, as the evidentiary basis for any specific causal mechanism is absent.

L0_label: Loss of speed and failed deployment
L0_description: The jumper flew a wingsuit he had not used in over a year, flew too slowly (loss of speed-glide), went off route, and was unable to successfully deploy his parachute until it was too late, impacting at line stretch. The decision to fly an uncurrent wingsuit and the resulting slow flight, off-route deviation, and failed pull attempts constitute identifiable human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to deploy his pilot chute but failed on the first two pull attempts, only succeeding on the third attempt. By that point there was insufficient altitude remaining, and he impacted at line stretch. The repeated failed pulls represent a performance/execution error in the physical act of deploying the parachute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flying non-current wingsuit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly his old X2 wingsuit after not flying it for one year, despite being current on a different suit (Aura). This decision to fly a non-current suit on a terrain flight resulted in degraded performance — he was slow and went off-route, placing him in a position where he lacked adequate altitude and separation when he needed to deploy. The decision to use non-current equipment for a flight requiring precise performance was clearly and specifically inappropriate.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had not flown the X2 wingsuit for one year, despite being current on a different suit (Aura). This lack of currency on the specific suit directly contributed to his degraded flight performance — flying slowly and going off-route — which is the consequence of the decision to fly non-current equipment.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had not flown the X2 wingsuit for one year. Different wingsuits have different BOC access characteristics and arm-wing configurations affecting the reach and deployment motion. His lack of currency on this specific suit directly contributed to his failure to execute the PC deployment on the first two attempts.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper went slightly off-route, then realized he wouldn't be able to continue on his line. By the time he recognized the need to deploy, he was in a position requiring immediate action, and the delayed recognition of his off-route position contributed to the late deployment attempts.

L0_description: The jumper was on only his second wingsuit BASE flight, far below the accepted minimum of 100 regular BASE jumps and 100 wingsuit skydives before attempting wingsuit BASE. He flew too slowly/head-high after exit, never achieved adequate performance from his suit, maintained minimal separation from terrain throughout the flight, and was unable to deploy his pilot chute (found still in BOC after impact). This represents a clear progression violation and inadequate skill for the task, constituting human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate wingsuit flight execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exit, the jumper did not begin effectively flying his wingsuit until approximately 100ft over the trees at the end of the talus, and the suit was not performing well throughout the approximately 30-second flight. He maintained minimal separation from terrain, consistent with flying too slowly or at an inefficient angle of attack, resulting in loss of speed and glide that left him unable to gain adequate separation from terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The pilot chute was found still inside the BOC after impact, indicating the jumper never successfully deployed his parachute. With insufficient terrain separation due to poor wingsuit performance, the jumper was unable to reach his BOC or chose not to attempt deployment, resulting in closed-container terminal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was only the jumper's second wingsuit BASE flight (the first being from the same exit). Wingsuit BASE requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps as prerequisites, and proficient wingsuit terrain flying demands extensive progressive experience. His second-ever wingsuit BASE jump placed him at a critically early stage of progression where the skills needed for effective wingsuit flight execution and managing terrain proximity had not been developed.

L0_description: The narrative provides only a link to an external article and states 'more details to come after investigation.' The possible factors listed (crosswinds on exit, not enough forward separation on exit) suggest potential human error pathways (decision to jump in crosswind conditions, poor exit technique), but the narrative lacks sufficient detail to confirm whether the crosswinds were foreseeable and the jumper chose to jump anyway (decision error), whether the exit technique was flawed (skill-based error), or whether conditions were unforeseeable (other cause). The cause of death—subterminal impact near exit—indicates a catastrophic failure in the exit/flight phase but without details about the jumper's experience, the specific conditions, or the sequence of events, a well-supported classification cannot be made.

The narrative provides only minimal details: a wingsuit BASE fatality from terminal freefall impact with possible factors listed as loss of speed-glide and proximity flying. However, there is no detailed accident narrative, no experience data, no weather information, and the report explicitly states 'more details to come after investigation.' There is insufficient information to determine whether human error or other causes led to the fatal outcome.

The jumper impacted terrain during a wingsuit proximity flight with nothing deployed, approximately 2/3 through the flight path. The narrative identifies 'Loss of speed-glide' and proximity flying as possible factors, and notes the jumper did not have many skydives or BASE jumps in this suit. Per the domain reference, insufficient experience in a specific wingsuit, flying proximity lines without adequate suit mastery, and loss of speed/available lift near terrain constitute human error pathways in wingsuit BASE flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of terrain impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was found approximately 2/3 through the normal wingsuit flight path with nothing deployed, having impacted terrain in terminal freefall. He was last seen flying in fine form around the first corner. There is no video evidence and the narrative explicitly states there is no basis for speculation about what occurred between the last sighting and impact. The cause of the fatal error — whether a decision regarding line choice, loss of speed/glide, spatial misjudgment, or other execution failure — cannot be determined from available information.

L0_description: The narrative describes a pilot chute entanglement resulting in PC in tow, which is a known malfunction mode in wingsuit BASE. The domain reference identifies PC/bridle entanglement as caused by improperly deployed PC (weak toss, holding PC too long) or heavy PC design, and emphasizes that the PC must be deployed into clean air with clear intent. The deployment failure during extraction from the BOC pouch indicates a human error in the deployment process.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to cleanly extract and deploy the pilot chute from the BOC pouch during a wingsuit flight, resulting in a PC/bridle entanglement that rendered the PC unable to extract the canopy. The domain reference identifies that PC/bridle entanglement is caused by improperly deployed PCs (weak toss, holding PC too long allowing bridle to flow downstream) and that the PC must be deployed into clean air with clear, unwavering intent. The jumper's three subsequent attempts to extract the canopy by hand were unsuccessful before ground impact.

L0_label: Aerials with wet gear and bridle entanglement
L0_description: The jumper performed 3 backflips (aerials) before deploying, which is a known risk factor for bridle entanglement with limbs. The bridle entangled with his foot during deployment, resulting in a horseshoe malfunction and fatal impact. Additionally, the jumper had previously landed in the river and was jumping wet gear, which the domain reference explicitly states should never be done ('Never jump wet equipment'). Performing aerials with wet equipment that could delay PC inflation represents identifiable human errors contributing to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle entanglement during aerobatic deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed 3 backflips before deploying at approximately 4-5 seconds. During or after the aerobatic maneuver, the bridle entangled with his foot. Aerobatic deployments are recognized as creating high risk of bridle entanglement due to non-belly-to-earth body positions, and the domain reference explicitly identifies deploying head-down and experiencing bridle entanglement with a foot as both a total error and total malfunction. The entanglement was the direct mechanism that prevented canopy inflation, producing the fatal horseshoe configuration and closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Jumping wet equipment
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that prior to this jump, Ian had landed in the river and gotten his gear wet. The domain reference explicitly states 'Never jump wet equipment.' The wet PC and canopy likely delayed PC inflation, contributing to the over-rotation and bridle entanglement during the aerobatic deployment. The extra weight of wet equipment coupled with a relatively small 36" PC for a ~4-second delay further degraded the deployment sequence.

L0_description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit from a new exit with a 'considerably short' rock drop and impacted the terrain seconds after exiting. Selecting an exit with insufficient vertical relief for wingsuit flight represents a human error in site/exit evaluation. The outcome was a subterminal impact near the exit point, indicating the object did not provide adequate altitude for the wingsuit start arc.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit from a new exit point behind the normal exit on Gavea, where the rock drop was considerably short. Selecting an exit with insufficient vertical relief for wingsuit flight is a clearly and specifically wrong decision — a wingsuit requires adequate altitude to transition from exit to flying speed, and a 'considerably short' rock drop does not provide this. The jumper impacted the terrain seconds after exiting, indicating the chosen exit point was fundamentally inadequate for wingsuit flight.

L0_description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall while flying a wingsuit proximity line ('the Barn Line') he had not flown before. The narrative identifies 'Loss of speed-glide' and proximity flying as possible factors, indicating the jumper failed to maintain adequate speed/glide margin while flying close to terrain on an unfamiliar line. Flying an unfamiliar proximity line without confirmed in-depth research of its requirements, combined with the loss of speed-glide leading to terrain impact, constitutes a human error pathway.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Magnus was flying a proximity line ('the Barn Line') in a wingsuit and impacted terrain in terminal freefall. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall with 'loss of speed-glide' listed as a possible factor, indicating he failed to maintain adequate airspeed and glide performance during the flight to clear terrain. He was an experienced jumper who had chosen this line deliberately; the fatal outcome resulted from insufficient execution of the flight rather than from the decision to fly the line itself.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost / Unfamiliar Line
L2_preconditions.description: Magnus had never flown the Barn Line before and planned to follow another jumper who had. When the other jumper exited and Magnus did not follow, he lost the benefit of a guide on an unfamiliar line. Flying an unknown proximity line solo, without the visual reference of a lead jumper, directly degraded his ability to manage speed, glide, and terrain clearance along the route.

L0_description: The narrative states only that the jumper 'impacted ledge trying to out track,' resulting in terminal freefall impact. This provides no information about why the jumper was unable to clear the ledge — whether it was a decision error (choosing a line requiring mandatory perfection), a skill-based error (poor tracking technique), an environmental factor (unexpected wind conditions), or something else. The jumper had substantial experience (600 BASE jumps, 6950 skydives), and proximity flying near terrain is an inherent aspect of terminal cliff jumping with tracking suits. Without details on the specific line chosen, terrain geometry, conditions encountered during flight, or what went wrong with the track, there is insufficient information to distinguish between human error and other causes.

The jumper was proximity flying a wingsuit along terrain and impacted through a small tree/bush during a technical part of the line, dying from terminal freefall impact. Proximity terrain flying involves deliberate flight near terrain where loss of speed/glide or misjudgment of the flight path constitutes a human error in flight management. The possible factors of 'loss of speed-glide' during proximity flying indicate a failure to maintain adequate margin from terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during terrain flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a proximity line along terrain when he lost adequate speed and glide performance, resulting in impact with terrain (through a small tree/bush) during wingsuit flight. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating he struck terrain before deploying his parachute. The narrative indicates loss of speed-glide as a possible factor, and the impact occurred on a technical part of the line, suggesting an execution failure in maintaining adequate airspeed and flight path rather than a fundamentally wrong decision about the line itself, which he had previously flown successfully.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying near terrain, was observed to be lower and slower than the lead jumper, and impacted the mountainside while attempting to clear a ledge. This indicates a loss of speed/glide during proximity flight resulting in insufficient margin to clear terrain, consistent with human error in flight management and/or decision-making regarding the chosen flight line.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate speed/glide management near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was observed flying lower and slower than the lead jumper during proximity flight along terrain. When encountering a ledge, the jumper lacked sufficient speed and glide performance to clear it, resulting in full-flight impact with the mountainside. Witnesses confirmed the jumper was 'not flying as well/fast' as the first jumper, indicating a failure in execution of speed and angle-of-attack management rather than a flawed plan, as the lead jumper successfully cleared the same terrain.

L0_description: The jumper had a bad push/exit from a very technical exit point requiring advanced wingsuit BASE skills, resulting in insufficient forward separation and impact with a ledge seconds after exit. The narrative explicitly identifies the bad push and insufficient separation as factors, and notes the exit was not appropriate for beginner or intermediate wingsuit BASE jumpers, indicating a human error pathway in either exit execution or object selection.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a bad push from the exit point and failed to achieve sufficient forward separation, resulting in impact with a ledge only seconds after exit. The exit technique was insufficient for this technical exit which required precise execution to clear terrain features near the exit point.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states this was a 'Very Technical exit with a few second rock drop not for a beginner or intermediate wingsuit basejumper,' directly indicating the exit required advanced wingsuit BASE skill. The jumper's failure to execute an adequate push from this technical exit is consistent with insufficient experience for the specific demands of this object. Accepted progression norms require extensive wingsuit BASE experience before attempting highly technical exits with minimal terrain clearance margins.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper jumped in 'extremely high winds' which 'played a huge factor in this fatality.' The domain reference establishes that few objects are good in winds >10 mph and no objects are good >20 mph, and that weather conditions must be evaluated before jumping. Choosing to exit in extreme winds is a decision within the jumper's control, constituting human error. The jumper flipped after exit, could not recover, and impacted near the exit point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in extreme winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper jumped in 'extremely high winds' that 'played a huge factor in this fatality.' The domain reference establishes that few objects are good in winds >10 mph and no objects are good >20 mph. The decision to exit in extremely high winds from a cliff in a wingsuit was clearly and specifically inappropriate — strong winds at a cliff exit are a recognized contraindication for jumping, and the conditions directly produced the unstable exit and inability to recover. This was not an execution failure; the jumper could not have been expected to fly successfully in these conditions regardless of skill.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed recovery from unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exiting, the jumper flipped and could not recover in time, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit. The jumper failed to regain a flyable body position from the unstable attitude induced by the wind conditions. However, this execution failure was substantially a product of the decision to jump in extreme winds.

L2_preconditions.label: Extreme wind at exit
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper jumped in 'extremely high winds' that directly caused the unstable exit (flipped after exit). The strong wind at the cliff exit physically disrupted the jumper's body position and prevented recovery, directly degrading execution of the exit and flight.

L0_description: The jumper flew a new wingsuit with only 2 skydives on it, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 wingsuit skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE jumping. The jumper experienced a failed start from a technical exit and impacted subterminal with nothing deployed, indicating the unfamiliar suit contributed to the inability to recover from the exit and deploy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate wingsuit familiarity for BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used a wingsuit he had only skydived twice before attempting a wingsuit BASE jump from a technical exit. Accepted practice requires 40-60 skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE use. This decision was clearly and specifically wrong — jumping a near-unfamiliar wingsuit from a technical cliff exit meant the jumper lacked the suit-specific knowledge needed to manage the start arc, and this inadequacy directly produced the failed start and subterminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed wingsuit start / unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate flight after exit, resulting in a 'fail to a short start' with subterminal impact and nothing deployed. The execution failure — whether an unstable exit, incorrect angle of attack, or inability to transition to flight in the unfamiliar suit — was the direct mechanism producing the fatal ground impact with no canopy deployed.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit familiarity
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only skydived the wingsuit twice before attempting a wingsuit BASE jump. Accepted practice requires 40-60 skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE use. This gross insufficiency of suit-specific experience directly degraded the jumper's ability to execute a successful start arc and transition to flight from a technical exit.

L0_description: The jumper was wingsuit proximity flying a technically demanding line and impacted terrain with only an emergency pull achieved (one pin popped). While proximity flying a line described as having a 'technical glide ratio' and being 'one of the most technical jumps to fly' suggests possible decision or execution errors, the narrative provides no information about the jumper's experience level, what specifically went wrong during the flight (whether a flight error, unexpected conditions, equipment issue, or other factor caused the terrain impact), or why the emergency pull came too late. Without knowing what caused the jumper to be in a position requiring an emergency pull, neither human error nor other cause can be adequately supported.

The jumper reportedly slipped on exit and was unable to establish stable wingsuit flight. He was observed falling straight down with only his pilot chute out, impacting the talus near the wall beneath the exit point. The unstable exit and failure to recover stable flight before attempting deployment constitute identifiable human error in the chain leading to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and failed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reportedly slipped on exit and was unable to establish stable wingsuit flight. He was observed falling straight down with only his pilot chute out, impacting on the talus near the wall beneath the exit point. The unstable exit and failure to recover into stable flight was the execution error that produced the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Exit surface condition
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'possibly slipped on exit,' indicating the exit surface condition (wet, icy, or loose rock) degraded his footing and directly caused the unstable exit that he was unable to recover from.

L0_description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit proximity line, reached to make a challenging entrance point on the terrain, stalled the wingsuit, and lost flight, resulting in fatal impact. Multiple human error factors are identifiable: flying a line with insufficient familiarity (only flown a few times, uncertain of waypoints, changed appearance due to snow), flying in katabatic conditions that made the already challenging line more difficult (after 3:30pm in shadow), being distracted by a phone conversation via bluetooth during flight, and possible motivational pressure from wanting to impress a companion jumper. The stall and loss of flight during proximity flying represents a decision/skill error in managing speed and angle of attack near terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Stall during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Philippe Jean flew his wingsuit toward a challenging entrance point on line 7, reached to make the point, stalled the wingsuit, and lost flight, resulting in terminal impact with the grassy slope. The recovered camera video confirms he stalled while attempting to access the entrance, indicating a technique/execution failure in airspeed and angle of attack management during the critical phase of flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Katabatic airflow degraded flight
L2_preconditions.description: After 3:30pm in late December, line 7 was in shadow with radiational cooling producing katabatic (downslope sinking) airflow. This atmospheric condition actively degraded the wingsuit's available lift and effective glide performance during the critical approach to the entrance point, making the already challenging flight path significantly more difficult and directly contributing to the stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction from phone call
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe Jean was on a phone call via a Bluetooth communicator during the wingsuit flight. The narrative explicitly identifies talking while flying as challenging and potentially distracting. This external stimulus diverted attention during a flight phase requiring precise airspeed and position management, contributing to the execution failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded landmark recognition
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe Jean had only flown line 7 a few times and had not familiarized himself with how waypoints and landmarks appeared under snow cover. He had explicitly stated after a previous flight that he was unsure of his position before making the entrance. Snow-altered visual references impaired his ability to accurately judge speed, position, and the appropriate moment to commit or abort, contributing to the stall during the critical approach.

L2_preconditions.label: Motivation to impress companion
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe Jean held the other jumper in high regard and had expressed excitement about showing him line 7. He exited immediately after the other jumper, passed him, and went straight for the challenging line rather than flying conservatively. This desire to demonstrate the line to an admired companion is a clearly identifiable misplaced motivation that contributed to committing to the aggressive flight path rather than exercising caution.

L2_preconditions.label: No abort plan established
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that Philippe Jean and his mentor/companion did not discuss how or when to abort if things did not go as planned on line 7. The absence of a pre-briefed abort plan for a challenging proximity line meant there was no predetermined decision point at which to abandon the approach, contributing to his commitment to the entrance point despite insufficient energy.

L0_description: The narrative states only that the jumper was following two others in a 3-way wingsuit jump and impacted a ledge during flight. There is no information about experience level, specific flight decisions, equipment state, environmental surprises, or what caused the impact. The cause of death (terminal freefall impact with terrain during proximity flying) could result from multiple pathways—misjudged line, insufficient speed/margin, environmental factors, or following error—but the narrative provides no evidence to distinguish among them. Neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be clearly supported.

The jumpers chose to jump in poor conditions including steady tailwind, violent winter swell at near high tide, and a smaller than usual landing zone. The narrative explicitly states conditions were poor during prejump and setup, and the jumper lacked the canopy penetration to overcome underestimated winds, resulting in landing in ocean wash and drowning. Jumping in these clearly observable adverse conditions constitutes a decision error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate landing area selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumpers chose a very small landing area closer to the ocean to avoid crossing a stream, in conditions of steady tailwind and violent winter swell at near high tide, which reduced the already small LZ further. This decision to use an undersized landing area in poor conditions with inadequate margin from the ocean was the producing mechanism: the tailwind reduced canopy penetration, and the jumper landed in the wave wash, leading to drowning. The narrative explicitly states conditions were poor, the LZ was smaller than usual, and the object is rated as highly technical.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 BASE season and was attempting a jump at an object described as 'very much a blue to black diamond' — a highly technical site requiring advanced site assessment skills. This insufficient experience level directly contributed to the inadequate evaluation of the landing area, wind conditions, and ocean hazard that constituted the L1 decision error.

L0_label: Failed PC extraction due to technique
L0_description: The jumper collapsed his wingsuit wings before initiating the pull, causing excess fabric to cover the BOC/PC handle. This prevented him from locating and extracting the pilot chute, resulting in a total malfunction (closed container impact). The detailed post-accident analysis by his peers confirmed that collapsing wings before the pull created a fabric obstruction over the BOC, constituting a deployment technique error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper collapsed his leg wing before initiating the pull, causing excess fabric to cover the BOC/PC handle. He was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute despite several seconds of attempting, resulting in a total malfunction (closed container impact). The intended action was to deploy the parachute, but the execution technique—collapsing wings before reaching the PC handle—prevented successful extraction.

L0_label: Wingsuit terrain impact in poor weather
L0_description: The jumper exited in bad weather with low-level fog and medium winds, which reduced visibility and likely contributed to clipping a ledge after exit due to insufficient forward separation. Jumping in conditions with low visibility and clouds obscuring the flight path represents a decision error, as the conditions were observable before the jump and a competent BASE jumper could have assessed them as unsafe for wingsuit terrain flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in inadequate weather
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited in bad weather with low-level fog and medium winds, which the police report states could have increased the chance of impact from low visibility. Despite being highly experienced with 1400 WS BASE jumps and intimate familiarity with this exit point, the decision to jump in conditions with low-level clouds and fog was clearly inappropriate as it degraded or eliminated the visual references essential for wingsuit terrain flying. The result was terminal freefall impact, consistent with inability to see and avoid terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions
L2_preconditions.description: Low-level fog and clouds degraded the jumper's ability to see terrain and maintain spatial orientation during wingsuit flight. The police report explicitly states low-level fog could have increased the chance of impact from low visibility. These conditions directly impaired the visual references essential for wingsuit terrain flying, contributing to the terminal freefall impact.

L0_description: The jumper exited a guyed antenna at night in strong winds (31 knots at jump altitude, 20 knots at midpoint) with a slider-up configuration on a short delay, which the domain reference identifies as yielding very poor heading performance. Forecasted winds aloft were well beyond the accepted safe range for BASE jumping (few objects good above 10 mph, none above 20 mph), and the decision to jump in these conditions constitutes a human error. The cause of death was object strike under canopy, consistent with wind-driven off-heading into guy wires.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping antenna in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump a guyed antenna at night with winds of 31 knots at 1600 feet and 20 knots at the midpoint. The domain reference states few objects are good in winds over 10 mph and no objects are good over 20 mph. Guyed antennas restrict desirable heading range to approximately 120 degrees, and wind evaluation is mandatory because wind conditions frequently rule out safe jump plans. Jumping a guyed antenna in 31-knot winds (approximately 36 mph) at exit altitude is a clearly and specifically inappropriate decision that produced the fatal object strike under canopy, as crosswinds degraded heading performance and canopy control near guy wires.

L0_description: The narrative reports line twists leading to object strike under canopy, but provides no information about the jumper's experience level, specific actions, equipment configuration details, or the cause of the line twists. Line twists can result from multiple causes (packing, body position at deployment, wingsuit asymmetry, equipment issues) and the narrative lacks sufficient detail to determine whether human error or another cause was responsible. The report is based on secondhand observation and explicitly requests additional information.

The jumper deployed his pilot chute too late, at an altitude insufficient for the canopy to fully inflate before ground impact. The narrative explicitly identifies the late/low pull as the cause of death, with the canopy inflating only moments before impact. Contributing factors suggest the jumper may have prioritized tracking distance over altitude awareness, resulting in deployment below a survivable altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed his pilot chute too late, at an altitude insufficient for the canopy to fully inflate before ground impact. The narrative states he was 'going faster and lower than the rest of the three way' and 'started to pull over the road or very close to it' when he was already critically low. The canopy inflated right before impact, confirming the deployment was initiated far too late. This represents a failure in altitude awareness and timely execution of deployment rather than a flawed decision about the jump plan itself — the jumper intended to deploy but failed to do so at a survivable altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Tracking Performance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'had felt improvements on his tracking' and may have 'pushed it too far,' and that 'the road is a landmark on that jump and it's common for people to want to track past it.' This suggests channelized attention on tracking performance and the goal of passing the road landmark, to the exclusion of monitoring altitude — directly contributing to the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions from goggles
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'had complained about his ski-goggles he jumped with not being optimal to see through.' Impaired visual acuity from suboptimal goggles could have degraded his ability to accurately assess altitude and terrain proximity during the tracking phase, directly contributing to the late deployment.

L0_label: Rigging/Equipment Error Leading to Total Malfunction
L0_description: The jumper experienced a pilot chute in tow malfunction resulting in closed-container impact. Investigators found a hole in the pilot chute netting and a broken zipper on the harness, with the bridle appearing caught on something. The jumper packed her own equipment, and the combination of equipment deficiencies (damaged PC, broken harness zipper) and the resulting bridle entanglement/snag indicates human error in equipment inspection, maintenance, or rigging prior to the jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of total malfunction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a total malfunction with only the pilot chute deploying (PC in tow). Investigators found a hole in the PC netting and a broken zipper on the right side of the harness, with a witness stating the bridle appeared caught on something. However, the narrative does not establish clearly whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., choosing to jump with damaged equipment) or a performance error (e.g., packing or rigging error that caused the bridle to snag). The relationship between the hole in the PC, the broken zipper, and the bridle entanglement is not sufficiently explained to distinguish between decision and execution failure.

L0_description: The jumper used a two-move dynamic exit technique where he advanced his right foot and then pushed, likely stepping on his leg wing and losing grip with his right foot. This caused an immediate unstable exit with a 45-degree bank within one second, leading to loss of control and subterminal impact approximately 200 meters below the exit point. The exit technique was inappropriate for a large wingsuit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used a two-move dynamic exit with his right foot advanced, and during the push he likely stepped on his leg wing and lost grip with his right foot, causing him to slip. This resulted in an immediate 45-degree bank to the left within less than a second, escalating to a 90-degree bank with the jumper struggling not to turn on his back. Despite recovering from the near-inversion and completing a roughly 270-degree turn, he was unable to establish a viable flight path and impacted terrain approximately 200 meters below the exit point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in gusty wind conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper initially waited approximately two hours because conditions were too windy, then declined to jump with his partner specifically because winds had become gusty. After waiting only 15 more minutes he decided to exit solo in conditions that had not meaningfully improved — winds were still visible on the grass at the exit and described as moderate to gusty. Jumping a large wingsuit (Vampire Race) in gusty crosswind conditions from this object was a clearly inappropriate decision that directly contributed to the fatal outcome by degrading exit stability and eliminating any possibility of recovery from the resulting unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Gusty crosswind at exit
L2_preconditions.description: Winds were gusty and visible on the grass at the exit point. These gusty crosswind conditions directly degraded the jumper's exit stability, contributing to the slip and immediate loss of control during the two-move dynamic exit in a large wingsuit.

L0_description: The narrative states only that the jumper entered low-level clouds and impacted terrain mid-flight. While proximity flying in clouds suggests possible human error (flying near terrain without visibility), the narrative provides almost no detail about the jumper's experience, specific flight plan, decision-making process, or the exact mechanism of the impact. The report itself solicits additional information. There is insufficient detail to confidently distinguish between a decision error (choosing to fly in clouds), an environmental event, or other causes.

The jumper was proximity flying a wingsuit near terrain and deviated from his planned flight path, flying into a gulley where he lost speed/glide and impacted terrain at high speed. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, with gear evidence showing only a low/emergency deployment attempt. The deviation from the planned direct flight path into a gulley during proximity flying, combined with the listed possible factor of loss of speed-glide, indicates human error in flight path management and energy management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during terrain flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was performing a wingsuit proximity flight through a gulley near the rifugio. Evidence indicates he lost speed and glide performance during the flight, as he impacted terrain at high speed in terminal freefall with only an emergency-level low deployment attempt (slider still at top of lines). His flight path deviated from the original plan—turning south and entering a gulley east of the rifugio where he disappeared from view—suggesting he was unable to maintain adequate energy and separation from terrain. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, consistent with a wingsuit pilot who lost available lift and could not recover or deploy in time.

L0_description: The jumper discovered a dislodged bottom pin at the exit point and re-closed the container without removing the bridle and pilot chute from their stow, routing the bridle inside the closing loop. This misrigging created a pin lock malfunction: the pilot chute deployed and extracted fully but could not pull the pin, resulting in a total malfunction and high-speed impact. This is a clear human error in rigging/packing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle misrouting during field repack
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After discovering a dislodged bottom pin at the exit point, the jumper re-closed the container without removing the bridle and PC from their stow. This resulted in the bridle being routed inside the closing loop, creating a pin lock malfunction. The PC deployed and extracted fully but could not pull the pins, resulting in a total malfunction and high-speed impact. This was an execution error in a task the jumper knew how to perform — the correct procedure (removing the bridle/PC before re-closing) was skipped during the field adjustment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate field repack procedure
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper re-closed the container at the exit point without removing the bridle and PC from their stow — a critical step in the repacking sequence. This failure to follow the complete procedure when re-securing the dislodged pin created the bridle misrouting that caused the pin lock malfunction. A systematic gear check after the field adjustment would have detected the improper bridle routing before exit.

L0_description: The jumper flew his wingsuit at very low forward speed from exit, consistent with flying at too high an angle of attack near stall. His speed decreased further as he attempted to clear power lines, leaving him with insufficient altitude to complete canopy deployment. The extended narrative reveals the jumper was operating well beyond his wingsuit BASE experience level, had demonstrated a stalled flight on the previous day's jump, rejected mentoring and progression advice, and proceeded to jump again in the same manner, constituting clear human error in both the decision to jump and the flight execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Slow/stalled wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Gary exited and immediately flew with very little forward speed, consistent with a stalled or near-stalled wingsuit configuration. His forward speed continued to decrease as he attempted to fly over the power lines, indicating he was flying at too high an angle of attack and failing to achieve efficient wingsuit flight. This poor execution consumed all available altitude, leaving him with insufficient altitude to complete deployment after clearing the power lines.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped beyond demonstrated ability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative establishes that Gary's previous jump from the same object the day before resulted in a stalled flight requiring an emergency pitch and an unplanned highway landing. Despite receiving extensive coaching about his deficiencies (exit technique, angle of attack, flight in non-thermic conditions), he chose to jump the same object again the next morning under similar shaded/non-thermic conditions. The prior day's near-miss clearly demonstrated that this object exceeded his wingsuit capability, making the decision to repeat the jump identifiably and specifically wrong given the information available to him.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: Gary's wingsuit flight on both days exhibited stalled/near-stalled flight from exit, indicating he lacked the fundamental skill to achieve efficient wingsuit flight. The witness noted that basic wingsuit BASE concepts (exit technique, angle of attack, flying in non-thermic conditions) were 'brand new to him,' and his request to join a technical multi-way formation further demonstrated a gap between his actual capability and the demands of the task. His inability to produce adequate forward speed on two consecutive attempts indicates insufficient wingsuit proficiency for the object.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Gary repeatedly described the Chief as 'basically like Brento' despite the witness knowing this was incorrect, dismissed corrective information from another source, and planned to immediately travel to France for Brevant after returning. After a near-miss requiring an emergency pitch and highway landing, he packed slider-up with his wingsuit again and even asked to join a technical multi-way formation.
