This pattern of dismissing risk information and overestimating his own capability directly contributed to his decision to repeat a jump that had clearly exceeded his ability the previous day.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of currency from injury break
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Gary had taken a break from jumping to recover from an injury prior to this trip. While the exact duration is not specified, the witness noted it as a relevant factor. Returning from an injury-related break degrades currency and physical readiness, which would have contributed to his poor wingsuit execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site evaluation
L2_preconditions.description: Gary characterized the Chief as similar to Brento and rejected corrective information about the actual jump profile, indicating he did not adequately evaluate the site's demands before committing to the jump. Proper site evaluation—understanding the flight profile, power line obstacles, and non-thermic morning conditions—is a required preparatory action that would have informed a more appropriate decision.

L0_description: The jumper flew into the lead jumper's burble approximately 4 seconds after exit, causing loss of speed and lift. Despite prior discussion about not following closely, JVH pushed on the same axis as the first jumper rather than maintaining adequate separation. The resulting loss of power at low altitude near terrain left insufficient margin for recovery, leading to subterminal impact. The exit angle selection and flight path constituted human errors in multi-way wingsuit terrain flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit angle execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: JVH exited on the right position but pushed to the same axis as the first jumper rather than maintaining a different angle. The pre-jump discussion established that the right-side jumper should push parallel or at a slightly different angle, but JVH flew into the first jumper's flight path, entering his burble approximately 4 seconds into the jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted fly-out instead of deploying
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After hitting the burble and experiencing clear loss of power at approximately 4 seconds, JVH chose to attempt to fly it out by turning 90 degrees right at a nose-up pitch rather than deploying his parachute while he still had altitude. He continued in degraded flight for another 3-4 seconds in a nose-up, energy-depleting configuration until impact at 10 seconds. The nose-up turn bled remaining speed and available lift, and the decision to continue flying rather than deploy produced the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Incomplete exit angle briefing
L2_preconditions.description: Although exit angles for camera flying were discussed on the cable car, when the plan changed at the top (the lead jumper would fly his normal line without camera-flyer procedures, and JVH chose the right side), there was no further discussion of exit angles at the exit point. This incomplete re-briefing after the plan change left the specific exit angle for JVH's position unresolved, directly enabling his push onto the same axis as the first jumper.

L2_preconditions.label: Burble from lead jumper
L2_preconditions.description: After entering the first jumper's flight path due to the L1 exit angle error, JVH encountered the lead jumper's wake turbulence (burble) approximately 4 seconds into the jump. This caused visible shaking, clear loss of power, and a leftward swerve, mechanically degrading his wingsuit's aerodynamic performance and placing him in the energy-depleted state from which he attempted to fly out rather than deploy.

L0_description: The jumper flew his wingsuit progressively shallower trying to stay above terrain rather than turning toward safer ground, resulting in loss of speed and glide (a recognized fatal pattern in wingsuit BASE). He realized too late he was unable to clear the ridges and his emergency deployment did not complete before terrain impact. Multiple human errors are identifiable: insufficient experience on the specific wingsuit (only 2 skydives on the V3 before BASE jumping it), choosing a terrain-proximate turning line despite unsteady performance on prior jumps, and flying at progressively higher AoA near stall rather than maintaining speed or executing an escape route.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After initiating the left turn over the ridgeline, Jimmy progressively pulled the suit to a shallower angle of attack in an attempt to stay above terrain rather than turning out toward safer ground. This slow-flight technique bled airspeed and available lift, causing severe lateral instability and an unrecoverable sink rate. The execution error was in flying too slowly near terrain — a recognized fatal pattern — rather than maintaining speed or converting to a dive to regain energy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew unfamiliar line with minimal suit experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jimmy chose to fly a turning line over ridges requiring terrain management, despite having only 2 skydives and approximately 13 BASE jumps on the V3 (a suit he was unfamiliar with, purchased as a gap-filler), and despite his previous Brento jumps being described as 'not very good, rather unsteady.' The decision to fly a committing terrain line — rather than going straight out as he had done successfully — was clearly inappropriate given his demonstrated lack of proficiency on this suit. He had a safe straight-out option and an alternative landing area available.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience for terrain flying
L2_preconditions.description: Jimmy had approximately 100 wingsuit skydives (almost all on a different suit, the P3) and only 2 skydives on the V3 before taking it to BASE. His previous Brento jumps were described as 'not very good, rather unsteady.' This level of experience and demonstrated proficiency on the V3 was clearly insufficient for choosing a committing terrain line requiring turns over ridges, per accepted wingsuit BASE progression norms.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from recent success
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that two steady straight-out jumps the previous day 'gave him confidence' to attempt the more committing turning line over ridges. This confidence was disproportionate to his overall demonstrated ability on the V3, which was characterized by unsteady performance across most of his Brento jumps.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient suit familiarity for terrain management
L2_preconditions.description: Jimmy had only 2 skydives and approximately 13 BASE jumps on the V3, a recently purchased suit that was not his primary wingsuit. His lack of familiarity with the V3's flight characteristics — speed range, stall behavior, and recovery — directly contributed to his inability to manage speed and glide near terrain, as he progressively slowed the suit into lateral instability rather than maintaining speed or converting to a dive.

L0_description: The bridle separated from the canopy on deployment, indicating a rigging error (misrigging) that resulted in a total malfunction. The narrative explicitly identifies incorrect rigging setup as the cause and points to extreme fatigue from a night shift, minimal sleep, and ten prior skydives as contributing to the rigging mistake.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Rigging error bridle-canopy separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The bridle separated from the canopy on deployment, indicating the bridle was not properly attached to the canopy during rigging/inspection. This is a memory or technique failure in executing a known critical rigging step — the jumper intended to have a properly configured system but failed in the physical execution of ensuring the bridle-canopy connection. The narrative states 'very likely a rigging error' and that the bridle separated from the canopy on opening, resulting in a total malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had worked a night shift as an air traffic controller, slept only a few hours, then completed nine tandem jumps and one fun jump before inspecting and rigging her BASE gear. The narrative concludes that 'all information points toward extreme fatigue played a large role in this rigging mistake.' This level of sleep deprivation and physical exertion directly degraded the cognitive capacity needed to execute the critical rigging check.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper opened both rigs to inspect them before jumping. Despite this inspection in a lit area, the disconnected bridle-canopy attachment was not detected. The gear check failed to catch the existing rigging deficiency — a bridle not attached to the canopy — which is a fundamental verification step in BASE equipment preparation.

L0_description: The jumper attempted a wingsuit BASE jump from a known difficult exit requiring a downhill run with a ledge at 300 feet that previous jumpers barely cleared. The jumper failed to clear the ledge, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit. The decision to jump this highly technical exit—described as 'super sketchy' by the narrative, previously vowed never to be repeated by one prior jumper, and jumped solo in post-rain conditions—constitutes a human error pathway.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose object requiring mandatory perfection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump an exit known to be extremely difficult, featuring a downhill exit requiring many steps with a ledge at 300' that previous jumpers had barely cleared by inches — one of whom vowed never to jump it again. The decision to attempt this object, which demanded mandatory perfection with essentially zero margin for error at the ledge, was the producing mechanism: the jumper did not clear the ledge and impacted near the exit. The object's known characteristics made it a clear case where any less-than-perfect execution would be fatal, and the decision to jump it was identifiably and specifically inadequate.

L0_description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with nothing deployed. The narrative indicates he was flying a borrowed tracking suit he was having trouble getting to fly correctly, suggesting loss of altitude awareness or inability to deploy due to suit-related difficulties. Jumping with equipment he was known to have trouble controlling constitutes identifiable human error in decision-making and/or execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed deployment cause unknown
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with nothing out, indicating a failure to deploy the parachute. The narrative states he was having trouble flying a borrowed tracking suit, but provides no specific detail about what prevented deployment — whether it was a decision to delay too long, inability to reach the BOC due to suit problems, loss of altitude awareness, or some other factor. There is insufficient information to distinguish between a decision error and a performance error.

L0_description: The jumper was flying a committing wingsuit proximity line and impacted terrain at the ridge crossing point. The narrative identifies multiple human error factors: flying a committing line in late afternoon when thermal lift had diminished compared to earlier jumps, operating at the highest margins of ability, and insufficient speed/glide to clear the ridge. The jumper had been progressively getting lower on previous passes and the conditions (reduced afternoon lift) made the line unviable, yet the jumper committed to the same flight path.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committing line in degraded conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly the most committing entrance to line 3 — where the ridge is flat and bail-out requires a sharp 90-110 degree turn — during late afternoon when thermal lift had diminished compared to his earlier jumps. He had observed himself 10-15 meters above the ridge on previous jumps but those were made with more lift available. The decision to fly this committing line in conditions with less lift than previously experienced was the producing mechanism: he lacked the margin to clear the ridge and the line geometry prevented escape once he recognized the problem.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient speed management
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper sank out and impacted the ridge, indicating he did not maintain sufficient airspeed to achieve the glide needed to clear the terrain. The narrative identifies loss of speed-glide as a possible factor, and the lessons emphasize that speed was lost while trying to maintain glide. Inadequate speed management — failing to maintain the energy reserves needed to convert to glide at the critical ridge crossing — was the execution-level failure that directly produced the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Reduced thermal lift
L2_preconditions.description: The late afternoon timing meant diminished anabatic/thermal lift compared to the jumper's earlier flights that day. The narrative explicitly states there was less lift than on previous jumps, when he had cleared the same ridge point by 10-15 meters. This reduced environmental lift directly degraded the jumper's effective glide performance, contributing to his inability to maintain sufficient altitude to clear the ridge.

L0_label: Insufficient terrain clearance during tracking flight
L0_description: The jumper impacted terrain (the top of a notch/ridge) during a tracking flight without deploying a parachute, indicating she committed to a low flight pattern and failed to clear mandatory terrain features. The site required out-tracking specific notches, and the evidence (steep trajectory, no deployment attempt, impact at the notch) indicates a loss of speed/glide resulting in failure to clear terrain — a flight path management error. Conditions were reported as calm and ideal, ruling out unforeseeable environmental factors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation / tracking performance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited cleanly and established a steep track, but failed to achieve sufficient glide to clear a known terrain feature (the first notch) that trackers must out-fly on this line. She impacted the top of the notch in terminal freefall without deploying her parachute, indicating she either did not recognize the terrain closure in time or was unable to generate the glide performance needed. The narrative describes a 'low flight pattern' and an attempt to 'out track the first notch' that was unsuccessful, pointing to inadequate tracking execution or misjudgment of glide performance relative to terrain.

L0_description: The jumper impacted subterminal near the exit point, indicating failure to achieve a flyable configuration after exiting. The narrative reports snow on the exit point causing a likely slip, combined with a homemade tracking suit as a possible contributing factor. Jumping from a snow-covered exit point represents a human error in evaluating conditions, and the resulting unstable exit led directly to the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit / slip
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reportedly slipped on snow at the exit point, resulting in an unstable exit from which he was unable to recover. The cause of death was subterminal impact near the exit, indicating he never achieved a stable body position or adequate separation to deploy his parachute. The execution failure (slipping) produced the fatal outcome directly.

L2_preconditions.label: Snow on exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states there was snow on the exit point, and the jumper's family believes he slipped on it. Snow on the exit surface directly degraded footing and caused the slip that produced the unstable exit.

L0_description: The jumper exited unstable with legs going over his back, resulting in impact with a known ledge shortly after exit. The narrative explicitly identifies an unstable exit as the producing mechanism. Additionally, strong winds were present, and the jumper chose the higher exit with a known dangerous ledge rather than the lower, more forgiving exit, indicating human error in both execution and decision-making.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and his legs went over his back, causing an unstable exit that prevented him from establishing flight. This execution failure directly resulted in him failing to clear the ledge below the exit point, leading to subterminal impact within seconds of exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong winds degraded exit stability
L2_preconditions.description: The report states strong winds were present at the time of the jump. At a high-altitude exit point (Titlis), strong winds can directly destabilize a wingsuit exit by disrupting the jumper's body position during launch, contributing to the legs going over the back and the resulting unstable exit.

L0_description: The jumper elected to fly a left line over a ridge and misjudged his position, lacking the height or glide to clear it. He failed to make an emergency pull and impacted in full track. This represents a decision error in line selection and a failure to deploy the parachute when clearance was not achievable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose line with insufficient clearance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper elected to fly a left line that took him over a ridge he did not have the height or glide to clear. This was a conscious line selection that was clearly and specifically inadequate for the terrain, as the chosen trajectory placed him on a collision course with a ridge without sufficient altitude or glide performance to pass it. The decision to fly that line was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy emergency parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper 'failed to make an emergency pull' after misjudging his position relative to the ridge. Once the line choice proved unviable, deploying the parachute was the remaining survival action available, and the failure to execute this pull resulted in impact in terminal freefall with no canopy deployed.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'misjudged his position' relative to the ridge. This indicates he misperceived his spatial relationship to the terrain during flight, which directly led to the selection of a line he could not clear.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to make an emergency pull despite being on a collision course with terrain. The narrative's statement that he 'failed to make an emergency pull' after misjudging his position suggests he was fixated on the tracking line or terrain avoidance to the exclusion of recognizing the need to deploy, or recognized it too late to act.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying a 'very positive' cliff described as lacking any margin for suboptimal parameters. He clipped a ledge after exiting and impacted terrain in mid-track without deploying a parachute. The decision to fly a line with zero margin for error constitutes a human error pathway, as the object's characteristics made any deviation from perfect performance fatal.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose jump requiring zero margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a 'very positive' proximity tracking line at Chapeau de Napoléon that the narrative explicitly describes as 'lacking any sort of margin for human or environmental suboptimal parameters.' This was a decision to commit to a line where any deviation from perfect performance or conditions would be fatal, constituting a course of action that was clearly and specifically inadequate given the zero-margin nature of the terrain. He clipped a ledge during the proximity flight and impacted in terminal freefall.

L0_label: Aggressive maneuver caused loss of control
L0_description: The jumper attempted a radical 90-degree right turn toward the base flyer to close an altitude gap, misjudged closing speed, and the resulting collision avoidance maneuver (closing right arm wing and bending right leg) put the wingsuit into an unrecoverable diving right turn into the wall. The narrative explicitly identifies that he chose a non-standard technique to lose altitude and executed the maneuver too aggressively, constituting a human error in flight execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Aggressive maneuver execution error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Uli attempted a radical 90-degree right turn toward the base flyer to close the altitude gap, but misjudged his closing speed. The maneuver was executed too aggressively, and when he braced for the resulting near-collision by closing his right arm wing and bending his right leg, this distortion put the suit into a hard diving right turn toward the wall from which he could not recover. The narrative explicitly states he 'executed the maneuver too aggressively' and 'appeared to misjudge his closing speed,' indicating a technique/execution failure rather than a fundamentally wrong decision.

L0_description: The narrative describes an exit that was 'too head low and not far away,' resulting in the jumper clipping a ledge with his feet approximately 2 seconds after exit. This unstable exit threw the jumper head-down, from which he never recovered, impacting the terrain with nothing deployed approximately 7-8 seconds later. The poor exit technique constitutes a human error that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with object strike
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed an exit that was 'too head low and not far away,' resulting in insufficient separation from the cliff. Approximately 2 seconds after exit, he clipped a ledge with his feet, which threw him into a head-down position from which he never recovered, impacting the terrain approximately 7-8 seconds later with no canopy deployed. The exit technique failure was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Slick exit surface conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'the mud was still a bit slick' at the exit area. Slick footing from mud directly degraded the jumper's ability to execute a clean, powerful exit, contributing to the 'too head low and not far away' exit that resulted in clipping the ledge.

L0_description: The narrative provides only that the jumper impacted terrain in full wingsuit flight during proximity flying. No details are provided about experience level, equipment configuration, specific flight decisions, environmental conditions beyond 'light and variable' winds, or the mechanism that led to terrain impact. There is insufficient information to determine whether human error or an unforeseeable cause produced the fatal outcome.

The jumper was performing an aerobatic maneuver (barrel roll or corkscrew roll) during a wingsuit proximity flight and impacted trees during the maneuver. Performing aerobatic maneuvers near terrain represents a deliberate action that directly produced the fatal outcome, constituting human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error during aerobatic maneuver
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was performing an aerobatic maneuver (barrel roll or corkscrew roll) during wingsuit proximity flight and impacted trees during the maneuver. The jumper intended to execute the maneuver and continue flying but failed in execution, resulting in terrain impact at terminal freefall speed. The fatal outcome was produced by insufficient technique or spatial awareness during the maneuver rather than by the decision to attempt it, as the maneuver could theoretically be survived with correct execution.

L0_description: The jumper exited a 250-meter wall with a known ledge just below the exit that required a running exit or very strong push to clear. The jumper clipped the wall with his feet shortly after exiting, indicating an insufficient exit push or technique. This unstable exit led to inadequate object separation, a 180-degree off-heading opening, and fatal wall impact during deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The 250-meter wall had a ledge just below the exit requiring a running exit or very strong push to clear. The jumper clipped the wall with his feet shortly after exiting, indicating insufficient push/run to achieve the separation needed to clear the ledge. This inadequate exit execution directly produced the object strike that caused the fatal injuries.

L0_description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall during a wingsuit proximity flight. The possible factors listed—loss of speed-glide and proximity flying—indicate the jumper flew too close to terrain without sufficient margin. Per the domain reference, flying near terrain with insufficient retained energy (speed) to convert to glide is a recognized human error pattern in wingsuit BASE, where slow flight or inadequate margin leads to inability to clear terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity flight beyond margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall during a wingsuit proximity flight, with loss of speed-glide identified as a possible factor. Flying a proximity line that left insufficient margin to recover from speed/glide degradation represents a decision that was clearly inappropriate — the chosen flight path did not allow survival when available lift diminished. The decision to fly close enough to terrain that a loss of speed-glide resulted in unrecoverable impact is the producing mechanism.

L0_description: The jumper exited 45 degrees head high, resulting in an upright/knee-first fall rather than forward flight. This poor exit prevented adequate forward separation and created a steep recovery arc that was insufficient to clear the ledge 50 meters below the exit. Multiple human error factors are identifiable: the jumper decided to jump despite physical exhaustion from a prolonged hike, marginal conditions with tailwind/downdraft, and executed a critically flawed exit from an object requiring mandatory obstacle clearance.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited 45 degrees head high, resulting in an upright/knee-first falling position rather than achieving forward flight. This poor exit technique prevented adequate forward separation and created a steep recovery arc that was insufficient to clear the ledge 150 meters below the exit point, resulting in chest impact with the ledge.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper struggled on the approach, turning a 3-hour hike into a 5-hour trip. He stated he would 'rather jump than hike down' due to being tired. This physical overexertion from the prolonged approach likely degraded his physical capacity to execute a demanding wingsuit exit requiring precise body positioning and a strong forward push.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's stated motivation for jumping was to avoid hiking down ('I'd rather jump than hike down') rather than being driven by readiness and confidence in conditions. This misplaced motivation to avoid the descent contributed to proceeding with the jump despite physical fatigue, directly setting up the conditions for the poorly executed exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Tailwind-induced downdraft at exit
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes a tailwind over the mountain creating a slight downdraft at the exit. For a wingsuit exit requiring forward separation to clear a ledge 150 meters below, a downdraft would actively degrade the jumper's ability to achieve forward flight and increase the altitude consumed during the start arc, directly contributing to the insufficient separation from the already-compromised exit.

L0_description: The jumper flew at maximum glide (too high AoA) without maintaining adequate airspeed, progressively losing speed until the wingsuit stalled. The witness explicitly states the jumper did not realize he was losing speed and lacked sufficient experience with the suit to recognize the deteriorating energy state. This is a clearly identifiable human error in airspeed/AoA management during wingsuit flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to maintain adequate airspeed
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to monitor and maintain adequate airspeed during wingsuit flight. After achieving a good start, he transitioned to maximum glide at approximately 100m above terrain without recognizing progressive speed loss. By the time the suit began to stall, he lacked sufficient altitude and positioning to recover. The eyewitness explicitly states 'He lost his speed slowly and didn't realise he was losing speed' and 'He had not enough experience with this suit to realise he was losing speed,' indicating an attention/monitoring failure during execution rather than a deliberate decision to fly slowly.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Glide
L2_preconditions.description: The eyewitness states the jumper 'lost his speed slowly and didn't realise he was losing speed.' He was flying at maximum glide approximately 100m above terrain, indicating his attention was directed toward maintaining glide ratio and terrain clearance rather than monitoring airspeed — a critical parameter for wingsuit flight. This channelized attention on glide performance to the exclusion of speed awareness directly enabled the progressive speed loss that led to the stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With 65 wingsuit BASE jumps and only 3 BASE seasons, the jumper was flying an Aura 2 — an intermediate-to-advanced wingsuit — that the eyewitness explicitly describes him as a 'beginner with this suit.' The eyewitness directly attributes the fatal outcome to insufficient experience: 'He had not enough experience with this suit to realise he was losing speed.' Recognizing progressive speed decay and managing the speed-glide tradeoff near terrain are skills that develop with extensive wingsuit experience, and this jumper lacked the experience to detect the deteriorating energy state before it became unrecoverable.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading with line twists resulting in object strike. However, the narrative provides no information about the specific cause of the off-heading — it could stem from packing error, body position at deployment, PC issues, wind conditions, or other factors. The reported 'medium winds' are noted but without specifics on direction, speed, or how they related to the exit orientation, this alone does not establish a clear human error pathway. With 450 BASE jumps, the jumper was experienced, and the narrative lacks sufficient detail to distinguish between human error and other causes of the off-heading opening.

The jumper was proximity flying in a wingsuit and attempted to fly over a terrain feature (the original Melchstul exit ledge). He impacted the terrain in terminal freefall just after crossing the ledge, indicating insufficient speed, glide, or margin to clear the terrain. The decision to fly a line requiring clearance of a terrain feature with inadequate margin constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose unviable proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a flight line that required flying over the original Melchstul exit ledge at extremely close proximity. Impact occurred just after crossing the ledge, indicating the chosen line required a level of terrain clearance performance that left zero margin. This was a deliberate line selection that was clearly and specifically inadequate — the jumper committed to flying over/past terrain with insufficient altitude and energy reserves to clear it, resulting in impact in terminal freefall with no opportunity to deploy.

L0_description: The jumper impacted with nothing deployed ('nothing out') and was unable to locate the pilot chute. There were no witnesses, and the narrative explicitly states 'we may never know' the cause. While 'unable to locate pilot chute' is listed as a possible factor, the reason for this failure is unknown — it could be human error (inability to reach BOC, loss of altitude awareness, incapacitation) or other causes, but there is insufficient information to determine which.

The jumper exited in winds of 5-10 m/s (11-22 mph), which the domain reference identifies as conditions where few objects are good (>10 mph) and none are good (>20 mph). The witness observed the jumper tumbling from exit to impact with no parachute deployed, indicating an unstable exit leading to a subterminal impact. The decision to exit in strong winds constitutes a human error, as these conditions were observable and forecastable before the jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in excessive wind
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited in winds of 5-10 m/s (11-22 mph), which is at or beyond the upper limit for safe BASE jumping and particularly hazardous for wingsuit exits where stable flight initiation is critical. The domain reference states few objects are good above 10 mph and none above 20 mph. The strong winds were a known condition at the time of the decision to jump, and the decision to exit in these conditions produced the unstable/tumbling flight from which the jumper never recovered, resulting in subterminal impact with no parachute deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Wind-induced exit instability
L2_preconditions.description: Winds of 5-10 m/s at the exit point directly degraded the jumper's ability to achieve stable wingsuit flight after exit. The witness observed the jumper tumbling immediately from exit until impact, indicating the wind mechanically disrupted body position and prevented flight initiation or recovery.

L0_description: The jumper had a bad exit from an unforgiving exit point and impacted terrain subterminal near the exit a few seconds later. The narrative explicitly identifies a bad exit resulting in insufficient separation from the object, indicating human error in exit execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a bad exit from an unforgiving exit point and impacted terrain a few seconds later. The narrative and possible factors indicate an unstable exit that failed to achieve adequate separation from the object, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit. The execution of the exit was the producing mechanism — a properly executed exit from this object could have been survivable.

L0_description: The narrative states only that the jumper impacted with nothing out while attempting to open a new wingsuit exit near terrain. No details are provided about the exit, body position, equipment state, deployment attempt, or flight path that would allow identification of a specific human error pathway. While proximity flying and a new exit are noted, the cause of the total malfunction (no deployment) cannot be determined from the available information.

The jumper experienced a steep flight path immediately after exit, flipped, and was unable to recover before impact. Whether the steep flight was caused by slipping or another factor is explicitly stated as unknown. While the outcome involved an unstable exit, the root cause cannot be determined from the available information, and the jumper's extensive experience (2400 BASE jumps, 1500 WS BASE) does not support a clear human error pathway without knowing what caused the initial instability.

The jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC during wingsuit deployment. Rather than returning to stable flight and reattempting, he continued searching while losing speed, altitude, and stability, ultimately impacting the ground without deploying his canopy. This was only his second jump with this wingsuit, indicating insufficient familiarity with the suit's BOC access characteristics.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper initiated the correct pull maneuver but was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC. Rather than returning to stable flight to reattempt, he continued searching while losing speed, altitude, and stability, eventually becoming unstable and rolling until ground impact. This was a technique/execution error in the deployment sequence - the correct action was intended but physical execution failed, resulting in inability to deploy the parachute.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was only the jumper's second jump ever with the C-Race wingsuit and second jump from Brento. The C-Race is a high-performance wingsuit. Accepted progression norms require 40-60 skydives with a specific suit before BASE use, and extensive practice pulls in all body positions. With only one prior jump in this suit, the jumper had minimal familiarity with the BOC reach mechanics and recovery procedures specific to this wingsuit, directly contributing to the failed PC extraction and the inability to return to stable flight for a reattempt.

L0_description: The jumper experienced an off-heading opening (60-70° left) that continued turning toward the cliff, possibly due to a brake fire or tension knot, resulting in object strike. The witness account is uncertain about the malfunction type ('Hard to say'), and there is insufficient information about packing, equipment condition, body position at deployment, or other factors to determine whether a specific human error or equipment failure independent of human error caused the off-heading and continued turn. The narrative does not provide enough detail to distinguish between human error and other causes.

The jumper executed a noticeably head-low exit from a wingsuit cliff jump, resulting in a corkscrew (uncontrolled rotation) that led to wall impact approximately 4 seconds after exit. A head-low exit is a recognized wingsuit exit error that prevents proper flight initiation and constitutes a human error in exit technique.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed a noticeably head-low exit from Pilastro. This unstable exit in crosswind conditions resulted in a corkscrew (uncontrolled rotation) from which the jumper was unable to recover, and the wingsuit flew him into the wall approximately 4 seconds after exit. The exit technique failure was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswind degraded exit stability
L2_preconditions.description: A 7 mph crosswind from the jumper's right to left was present at the exit point. This crosswind actively contributed to the unstable, head-low exit by introducing an asymmetric aerodynamic force during the launch, degrading the jumper's ability to achieve a stable exit attitude and contributing to the subsequent corkscrew.

L0_description: The jumper received a 180-degree off-heading opening on a slider-down cliff jump and failed to execute heading correction maneuvers. Evidence shows toggles were never unstowed, indicating no corrective action was taken. The canopy flew back into the cliff resulting in multiple object strikes and fatal injuries. The failure to perform off-heading correction procedures constitutes identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed off-heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper received a 180-degree off-heading opening on a slider-down cliff jump. Witness accounts and gear inspection confirm that toggles were never unstowed, indicating the jumper failed to execute an effective heading correction using rear risers or brakes. The canopy flew back into the cliff, striking it multiple times before the jumper came to rest on the talus, dying from impact injuries.

L0_description: The jumper flew a full-glide proximity line above a ridge that required sustained high glide ratio, without retaining sufficient speed and altitude reserves. His brother's account explicitly states he did not leave enough safety margin, lost speed at the end of the ridge, and encountered descending air during a turn that he could not recover from. Flying a line that demands full glide performance with no margin for error or environmental variation constitutes a decision error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient speed margin for terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a full-glide proximity line above a ridge that required at least 3.5 glide ratio, without maintaining adequate speed reserves. His brother noted he 'did not leave enough safety margin' and that 'it's never a good idea to proximity fly without some speed in reserve.' The decision to commit to this line without retaining sufficient energy to manage the flattening ridge profile and potential atmospheric changes (descending cooler air) was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed speed/energy management during flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: At the end of the ridge where terrain flattened and required higher glide performance, the jumper had insufficient speed and height. When he turned toward the landing zone, he encountered descending air and lost flight characteristics rapidly. His inability to maintain adequate airspeed through the critical section — arriving at the end of the ridge without enough energy to either clear terrain or execute the turn — represents a failure in execution of speed and energy management during the flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Descending air degraded flight
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes that when the jumper turned toward the landing zone, he 'may have caught some cooler descending air and he lost his flight characteristics very quick.' This atmospheric disturbance — katabatic or sinking air on the cooler side of the ridge — actively degraded his wingsuit's aerodynamic performance at the critical moment, directly contributing to the loss of speed and glide that constituted the L1 skill-based failure.

L0_description: The jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket during wingsuit flight, resulting in impact without canopy deployment. The narrative explicitly describes the jumper struggling to pull the PC, trying different moves, and de-arching instead of arching - indicating a failure in the deployment sequence. The pilot chute was found barely out of the pocket post-impact with the bridle still folded, confirming the PC was never successfully deployed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and deploy pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket during his wingsuit deployment attempt. Witnesses observed him struggling with multiple different moves to find the PC, including de-arching instead of arching, and ultimately turning onto his back before impact. The pilot chute was found just out of the pocket with the bridle still in folds post-impact, confirming it was never successfully deployed during flight.

L0_description: The jumper struck a ridge during wingsuit proximity flying with no attempt to deploy a parachute. The narrative indicates he was flying a challenging left-sector line rather than the easy direct path, possibly pushing to fly further over terrain on his second flight of the day, suggesting a judgment error in line selection or terrain clearance assessment. Contributing factors include limited experience with the specific wingsuit (10-12 jumps) and a selfie stick camera mount that may have impaired vision and depth perception.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose proximity line exceeding margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a 'rather left flight path line' — one of the more challenging lines — that required flying over a ridge with trees. Given the available direct/easy line to the landing area, the jumper's choice to fly a proximity line over terrain he needed to clear, on his second jump of the day from this exit, with relatively few jumps on this wingsuit (10-12), constitutes a decision that was clearly and specifically inadequate. The decision to fly this line placed him in a no-pull zone over a ridge he failed to clear, resulting in terminal impact with no deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Possible visual impairment from selfie stick
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was wearing a selfie stick camera mount on his helmet, which the narrative notes may have disturbed his vision and depth perception. If this impaired his ability to accurately judge terrain proximity and clearance margins, it would have degraded the situational awareness underlying his decision to commit to the challenging left-sector line over the ridge.

L2_preconditions.label: Limited experience on wingsuit model
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 10-12 jumps on the Vampire Alpine wingsuit. Limited familiarity with a specific suit's performance envelope — its start arc, glide characteristics, and speed range — degrades the jumper's ability to accurately assess whether a given proximity line is within achievable margins, directly contributing to the decision to fly a line that exceeded the suit's performance or the jumper's ability to extract that performance.

L0_description: The jumper flew a slow, low flight path over the plateau, passing the ridge lower than expected. Despite multiple opportunities to perform an emergency pull, he continued flying in an attempt to pass the plateau and impacted terrain without deploying his parachute. This represents a decision error in continuing proximity flight without adequate speed/glide margin and failing to execute an available emergency deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued flight without deploying
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David flew a slow, low flight path over the plateau and was observed to have had multiple opportunities to perform an emergency low pull. Rather than deploying his parachute when it became clear he lacked adequate altitude and speed to complete the planned line, he decided to try to pass the plateau and impacted approximately 2m before its end without having attempted to pull. The decision to continue flying through a no-pull zone rather than deploying when opportunities existed was the producing mechanism of the fatal impact.

L0_description: The narrative states the jumper reached his hand through the stash bag compression cord and pulled the PC out through it, causing a PC entanglement that resulted in a total malfunction (PC in tow). This indicates a deployment execution error where the jumper's hand path during PC extraction created the fatal entanglement.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: PC deployment through compression cord
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reached his hand through the stash bag compression cord and pulled the pilot chute out through it, causing the PC to become entangled with the compression cord. This execution error during the deployment sequence resulted in a PC in tow / PC-bridle entanglement and a total malfunction. The jumper intended to deploy but failed to execute the BOC reach correctly, routing the PC through an obstruction rather than cleanly extracting it.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 25 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. The BOC extraction skill—cleanly reaching past container features to extract the PC without routing it through obstructions—is a fundamental motor skill that develops through repetition. With only 25 jumps, the jumper had limited opportunity to develop the reliable muscle memory needed for consistent, error-free BOC deployment, particularly while wearing a tracking suit that may alter reach dynamics.

L0_description: The jumper deployed at 3-4 seconds while unstable at the vertical section of a cliff configured for a 9-second slider-up delay. This premature, unstable deployment of a slider-up packjob at sub-optimal airspeed produced a violent off-heading opening (~140°) with canopy surge toward the cliff, resulting in fatal object strike. The deployment timing, body position at deployment, and mismatch between packing configuration and actual delay all represent identifiable human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature deployment while unstable
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed at approximately 3-4 seconds into a jump configured for an 8-9 second delay (slider-up), while in an unstable body position after failing to establish a controlled track. This premature, unstable deployment at the vertical section of the cliff produced a violent ~140° off-heading opening with canopy surge, resulting in fatal object strike. The slider-up configuration at sub-terminal airspeed degraded heading performance, and the unstable body position further compromised the opening, but the core failure was the execution: deploying at the wrong time, in the wrong body position, at the wrong altitude relative to the cliff profile.

L0_description: The jumper had a bad exit, possibly slipping, resulting in a head-down orientation with insufficient forward separation from the cliff. He impacted a ledge approximately 5 seconds after exit with no time to recover or deploy, indicating the exit execution was the direct human error producing the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a bad exit, possibly slipping on the wet/damp exit point, resulting in a head-down attitude from which he could not recover. He impacted a ledge approximately 5 seconds after exit with no canopy deployment, indicating the unstable exit directly produced the fatal conditions by leaving him head-down without sufficient time or separation to recover and deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Wet/damp exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'perhaps he slipped (there was previous rainy days),' indicating the exit surface may have been wet or damp from recent rain. This surface condition would have directly degraded the jumper's footing during the exit, contributing to the unstable/head-down departure.

L0_description: The jumper was missing for weeks, jumped alone, no video was recovered, and the narrative explicitly states details cannot be confirmed. The possible factor 'loss of speed-glide' and the cause of death 'impact in terminal freefall' suggest a wingsuit flight failure, but the specific mechanism (human error vs. environmental cause) cannot be determined from the available evidence. Police recovery details are described as speculation.

The jumper committed to a proximity line with a no-pull zone and no escape route despite flying at lower-than-normal speed and a high glide ratio near the limits of the wingsuit's performance. The narrative shows the jumper was already low and slow before the burble encounter, and the decision to continue the line rather than peeling out to the right before the no-out section constitutes a decision error. Flying in close formation through terrain with mandatory performance requirements, combined with failure to execute the pre-planned abort when conditions warranted it, represents identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committed to no-out line without adequate margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued into a known no-pull zone with no out to the right despite having lower groundspeed and a higher-than-normal glide ratio (indicating near-maximum AoA with minimal energy reserves). The pre-jump plan explicitly called for peeling out to the right before the no-out section if the terrain was not flyable, yet the jumper committed to the line rather than executing the planned escape. This decision to continue into terrain with no escape route while already flying at marginal energy states was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to maintain adequate airspeed
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew at a high glide ratio with insufficient airspeed, consistent with flying too slowly near stall. After encountering the burble from Jumper 2, the already marginal speed was further reduced, leading to a wingsuit stall with no altitude or energy to recover. The domain reference identifies flying at high AoA near stall as a recognized fatal error pattern in wingsuit BASE, and the jumper's groundspeed was already lower than on previous successful flights of the same line.

L2_preconditions.label: Burble from lead jumper
L2_preconditions.description: Jumper 2's wingsuit burble directly disrupted Ben's flight, causing instability and a dramatic decrease in groundspeed. The narrative explicitly states Ben flew through the burble, became unstable, and lost groundspeed from which he could not recover. This environmental disturbance actively degraded his ability to maintain adequate airspeed at a critical moment when he was already flying at high GR with limited energy reserves.

L0_description: The narrative provides only that the jumper was found underneath the exit after a subterminal impact near the exit point, with 'unstable exit' listed as a possible factor. There is no information about the jumper's experience level, the specific exit dynamics, equipment condition, or what precisely occurred. Without details on skydive/BASE experience, the exit itself, or equipment state, neither a specific human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be reliably established from the available evidence.

The jumper lost speed during proximity flying near terrain, which is consistent with flying at too high an angle of attack (too slowly), a recognized human error in wingsuit BASE. Loss of speed near terrain eliminated available lift and margin for terrain clearance, resulting in impact. The domain reference identifies slow flight near terrain as a known fatal error pattern in wingsuit BASE.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Airspeed management failure during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During wingsuit proximity flight, the jumper lost speed mid-flight, which caused altitude loss and placed her close to terrain. The domain reference establishes that speed loss in a wingsuit rapidly depletes available lift (a 20% speed decrease results in 36% AL loss), and that flying too slowly is a recognized execution failure pattern where the pilot allows AoA to increase, bleeds energy, and enters a degraded flight state. The jumper was unable to recover sufficient energy to clear the terrain, resulting in impact in terminal freefall.

L0_description: The jumper exited head-down from a wingsuit BASE jump with only 85m to a large ledge and was unable to recover to flying configuration before impacting the ledge. The head-down exit represents a departure from accepted practice for wingsuit exits, and the decision to jump this object with such minimal margin and downwind conditions compounds the error. The narrative clearly identifies human error in both exit execution and jump planning.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with failed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited head-down from a wingsuit exit at 6000m with only 85m to a ledge below. He was unable to recover from the head-low exit attitude quickly enough to establish forward flight, resulting in impact with the ledge 85m below the exit point. The exit execution was the producing mechanism — a stable, properly executed wingsuit exit could have allowed forward flight away from the terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Downwind degraded exit recovery
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states there was downwind coming from the right, and that the jumper 'couldn't manage to recover quickly because of the downwind.' The wind actively degraded his ability to recover from the head-low exit attitude by pushing him toward terrain and reducing effective forward separation, directly contributing to the failed recovery from the unstable exit.

L0_description: The jumper exposed his hands to cold conditions without adequate protection (no gloves for 12 minutes, then only thin gloves) before a wingsuit BASE jump. This resulted in loss of sensation and dexterity in his fingers, rendering him unable to locate and extract the pilot chute during a 5-6 second pulling attempt, leading to a no-pull impact. The decision to jump with inadequately protected and cold-impaired hands constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unable to locate/extract pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Kristian attempted to pull for 5-6 seconds but was unable to locate or extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket. The narrative indicates he had lost feeling in his fingers and hands due to cold exposure, resulting in inability to physically execute the pull despite repeated attempts. He impacted without deploying his parachute.

L2_preconditions.label: Cold-induced loss of hand dexterity
L2_preconditions.description: Kristian was without gloves for at least 12 minutes before the jump and wore only thin gloves for the final 5 minutes. He was observed trying to blow his hands warm before exit. The narrative states it is 'truly believed that the cold made him lose the feelings in his fingers and hands,' directly impairing his ability to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket.

L0_description: GoPro footage clearly shows the jumper made a series of attempts to locate and deploy the pilot chute but was unable to do so, resulting in a total malfunction and ground impact. Failure to successfully deploy the PC from BOC during wingsuit flight constitutes a human error in execution of a critical skill.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and deploy PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: GoPro footage clearly shows a series of attempts to find and deploy the pilot chute, all of which failed. The jumper intended to deploy but was unable to locate the BOC handle, resulting in no parachute deployment and closed-container impact. This is an execution failure in a fundamental skill — extracting the pilot chute from the BOC pouch during wingsuit flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had 3 BASE seasons and was flying a Vampire Sukhoi, a high-performance wingsuit. The narrative describes flight 'a bit on the slow side,' consistent with insufficient experience to fly the suit efficiently. Reliable BOC location during full-flight wingsuit deployment is a skill requiring extensive wingsuit-specific training (hundreds of practice pulls in all body positions per accepted norms). The repeated failed attempts to locate the PC handle suggest the jumper had not developed the muscle memory and technique required for consistent BOC extraction in this suit, indicating insufficient training/experience for the specific wingsuit BASE task attempted.

L0_description: The jumper impacted subterminal near the exit without deploying the parachute while attempting a committing wingsuit line in strong winds. The combination of strong winds, an unstable exit, and flying a committing line that left no margin for error points to human error in the decision to jump and/or execution of the exit. The jumper's relatively low experience (110 BASE jumps, 3 seasons) for a committing wingsuit terrain line further supports a human error pathway.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unpulled wingsuit impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted subterminal near the exit without deploying the parachute. The narrative states a possible unstable exit and strong winds, but the jumper was found the next day with no witnesses, so it cannot be determined whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., choosing to jump in strong winds on a very committing line) or a performance error (e.g., unstable exit leading to loss of control and inability to deploy). Insufficient information exists to discriminate between decision and execution failure.

L0_description: The jumper used non-locking carabiners on climbing slings stowed in harness saddlebags. Prior to exit, the right carabiner had partially worked free of its stowage. On exit, the bridle laid over the carabiner gate, and static line tension forced the gate open, clipping the bridle and choking off canopy extraction, resulting in a total malfunction. The failure to properly secure or remove non-essential equipment (non-locking carabiners on climbing slings) that could interfere with the deployment system constitutes a human error in equipment rigging and preparation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate stowage of climbing sling
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper stowed his climbing sling and non-locking carabiner by tucking them into the saddlebag opening on his leg strap. During climbing and sitting on the railing, the right-side carabiner worked halfway out of its stowage. This inadequate stowage/failure to verify secure stowage before exit allowed the bridle to lay over the carabiner gate, which then clipped the bridle when tension was applied, choking off the canopy and producing a total malfunction resulting in impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to verify gear stowage before exit
L2_preconditions.description: The video shows Eddie handling and checking the bridle routing prior to exit, but he failed to verify that the right-side carabiner remained securely stowed after climbing over the railing and sitting on the top rail. A pre-exit gear check that included verifying the slings and carabiners were secure would have detected the partially dislodged carabiner before it could interact with the bridle.

L0_label: Aerial attempt led to low pull
L0_description: The jumper attempted a gainer from a slider-up antenna with only 50 slider-down jumps, no meaningful slider-up experience, and a history of poor/unstable gainers. The failed aerial resulted in loss of altitude awareness and stability, leading to PC deployment too late for canopy inflation before impact. Multiple human errors are evident: performing aerials from a slider-up object far beyond his progression level, and deploying too late after the failed maneuver.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Aerial from inadequate object/experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to perform a gainer from a slider-up antenna with only 2-3 prior slider-up jumps and no established competence in aerials (described as performing 'poor gainers' that were 'rather unstable and uncontrolled'). Attempting an aerial from his first slider-up object with minimal slider-up experience and demonstrated inability to execute gainers competently was a clearly inappropriate decision. The domain reference states aerials exponentially increase danger and require careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed gainer execution and late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper could not set the rotation, hovered, rolled over his side, then attempted to stabilize and turn away from the antenna. He deployed his PC too late, impacting without a canopy. The failed aerial execution consumed altitude and the delayed deployment was the direct mechanism producing the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only one BASE season, approximately 50 slider-down jumps, and only 2-3 slider-up jumps from this antenna (his first slider-up object). Accepted progression norms require extensive experience with aerials from forgiving objects before attempting them from technical objects, and slider-up jumping requires significant progression beyond slider-down. His experience level was clearly insufficient for the task of performing a gainer from a slider-up antenna.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'performed poor gainers, some of them were rather unstable and uncontrolled,' indicating a sustained pattern of inadequate aerial execution that he was aware of through repeated personal experience. Despite this known recurring deficiency, he continued to attempt gainers and escalated to performing one from a more demanding object (slider-up antenna) without correcting the underlying skill deficit.

L0_description: The jumper was unable to locate the pilot chute at pull time despite a high pull attempt, resulting in impact without canopy deployment. Failure to extract the PC from the BOC is a skill-based active failure. The noted possible health issues may have contributed to the inability to locate the handle, but the proximate cause was the failed deployment action.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to deploy at a reasonably high altitude but was unable to locate the pilot chute in the BOC pocket. Video evidence shows the jumper made a pull attempt but could not find the PC, then flipped over and impacted the ground. This is an execution failure during a correctly intended action — the jumper knew to pull and attempted to do so but failed in the physical execution of locating and extracting the pilot chute.

L2_preconditions.label: Health issues
L2_preconditions.description: The report lists 'Health issues' as a possible factor. At age 50 with documented health issues, a physical or physiological condition may have degraded the jumper's ability to execute the PC extraction, contributing to the inability to locate the pilot chute in the BOC pocket.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 150 skydives, which is below the generally accepted minimum of 200-250 before beginning BASE. With 143 BASE jumps on an insufficient skydiving foundation, core skills including reliable BOC extraction under stress may not have been adequately developed, contributing to the failed pull attempt.

L0_description: The jumper flew a wingsuit proximity line too slowly and without adequate energy, fixating on repeating his morning line at a lower altitude. Video evidence confirms slower-than-normal flight, inability to clear trees he was targeting, and failure to execute an available escape to the right. This constitutes human error in airspeed management and situational awareness during proximity terrain flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Slow flight into terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Oscar flew the same proximity line as his morning jump but lower and slower, without adequate speed/energy. The video shows slower-than-normal flight for at least the last 10 seconds, during which he appeared fixated on buzzing the trees on the ridge shoulder. In the final second he lost his flight, was unable to clear the trees, and it was too late to turn right to the available escape route. This constitutes a failure to maintain adequate airspeed and available lift — a fundamental execution requirement for proximity wingsuit flying that he understood but failed to perform on this flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The video evidence and mentor's analysis indicate Oscar was fixated on repeating his morning proximity line and buzzing the trees for at least the last 10 seconds of flight. His mentor states he was 'so focussed on flying the line that he didn't pay attention to how this flight was going differently.' This channelized attention on the intended line prevented him from recognizing his degraded speed/energy state and executing the available escape to the right until it was too late.

L0_description: The jumper clipped the underhung section of the cliff with his legs immediately upon exit, indicating insufficient forward separation during the exit. This poor exit caused a violent spin from which he could not recover before impact. The exit technique failure is a human error that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper clipped the underhung section of the cliff with his legs immediately upon exiting, indicating an insufficient exit push or improper body position that failed to clear the terrain. This contact sent him into a violent spin from which he was unable to recover before impact. The exit execution failure was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L0_label: Late deployment during first terminal track
L0_description: The jumper failed to deploy the pilot chute at a safe altitude during her first terminal tracking jump from a slider-up cliff. The narrative explicitly states she extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in an emergency low pull with only approximately 2 seconds under canopy before impact. The jumper did not achieve adequate tracking performance to clear terrain features, and failed to recognize the need to deploy earlier given her proximity to the ledge.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to deploy her pilot chute at a survivable altitude. Despite being described as a habitual 'high puller,' she extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in only approximately 2 seconds under canopy before impact. The narrative indicates she likely lost altitude awareness, possibly due to visual fixation on the landing area rather than monitoring her proximity to terrain ('my guess is that her visuals were at the landing and that she did not see the ledge coming'). This was an attention/monitoring failure during execution rather than a decision error — the jump plan itself (terminal tracking, slider-up) was appropriate for the object, but the execution of altitude monitoring and timely deployment was insufficient.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The witness estimated that the jumper's visuals were fixed on the landing area and she did not see the ledge coming. This channelized attention on the landing zone to the exclusion of terrain proximity awareness directly contributed to the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first terminal tracking jump from a slider-up cliff, with only 70 BASE jumps and 2 BASE seasons. Terminal slider-up cliff jumping with a tracking suit requires sub-terminal tracking proficiency and altitude awareness skills developed through progressive cliff experience. The jumper did not effectively establish her track or maintain adequate terrain awareness, consistent with insufficient BASE experience for this specific task.

L0_description: The jumper deviated from his initial flight path, made late hard turns to re-align with a terrain gate, lost speed and altitude, reached a critical angle of attack, stalled the wingsuit, and impacted terrain. The eyewitness account clearly describes a sequence of decision and skill errors—flying at an oblique angle, making late aggressive turns that bled speed, and continuing toward the gate at progressively higher AoA until the suit stalled—constituting human error that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late aggressive maneuvering toward terrain gate
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After initially flying at an oblique angle away from the tree gate, Alex made a late decision to bank hard right and then left to realign on the gate. This late course correction at approximately 8 seconds into the flight caused significant speed and altitude loss, placing him in a critically high AoA from which he could not recover. The decision to aggressively maneuver back toward the terrain feature rather than continuing the safer detour around it to the left was the producing mechanism for the fatal stall and impact.

L0_description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff wall during sub-terminal tracking, turned toward the wall during the track, and deployed the pilot chute at 10 seconds while facing the wall with minimal separation. With only 19 BASE jumps, 1 BASE season, and limited tracking suit experience in sub-terminal conditions, the jumper was at a slider-up cliff requiring mandatory sub-terminal tracking skills that had not been sufficiently developed. The late deployment with no forward separation resulted in wall impact at line stretch.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed sub-terminal tracking execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and swept into a tracking position but immediately began turning to the right, achieving almost no forward separation from the wall. This is a technique/execution failure in sub-terminal tracking — the jumper intended to track away from the cliff but failed to maintain heading and generate adequate horizontal distance. The narrative notes he 'had a tendency to turn once he was up to speed,' indicating a recurring execution deficiency rather than a deliberate decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment with insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: At 10 seconds the jumper pitched his pilot chute while facing the wall with no meaningful object separation. Having failed to achieve forward distance, deploying at that point without adequate separation was a failure to recognize and respond to the critical situation — he needed to deploy earlier while still facing away from the wall or at a point where separation existed. The deployment timing and orientation constituted an attention/awareness failure during execution, resulting in impact at approximately line stretch.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 19 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season, and was attempting slider-up terminal cliff jumps requiring mandatory sub-terminal tracking in a tracking suit he had only recently acquired. Accepted progression norms indicate that sub-terminal tracking from terminal walls requires substantially more experience, and the narrative explicitly states 'there is nothing that can simulate subterminal tracking other than subterminal tracking' and recommends balloon jumps prior to terminal walls. His experience level was clearly insufficient for the task attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Turning Tendency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'did have a tendency to turn once he was up to speed,' indicating a recurring sub-terminal tracking deficiency observed across previous jumps. This known performance issue was not corrected before continuing to jump terminal cliff objects where tracking separation is mandatory for survival.

L0_description: The jumper performed a weak push from an exit point with a known positive incline requiring approximately 2m of clearance. Combined with a head-high posture, the jumper failed to achieve sufficient forward separation, resulting in foot strike on the ledge 6-8m below the exit, followed by fatal impact. The narrative explicitly identifies a weak push and head-high posture as the producing factors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Weak exit with poor body position
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Rob performed a weak push from the exit point combined with a head-high posture. This execution failure resulted in insufficient forward separation to clear a significant positive incline approximately 6-8m below the exit, causing his left foot to strike the cliff and initiating a fatal tumble. The exit technique — not the decision to jump — was the producing mechanism; with a stronger push and proper body position, the ledge could have been cleared.

L2_preconditions.label: Adverse physiological state
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Rob's 'judgement may have been somewhat impaired by a bad stomach and altitude.' A gastrointestinal illness combined with altitude effects could have degraded his physical execution capacity, contributing to the weak push and poor body position on exit.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states this was 'a case of jumping from the wrong exit point.' The jumper exited from a position that did not provide sufficient clearance from a large ledge 30 meters below, resulting in subterminal impact with the ledge after two seconds. The co-jumper, positioned roughly a meter in front and slightly to the left, barely cleared the same ledge, confirming that the exit point selection was the critical error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wrong exit point selected
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited from a position that did not provide adequate clearance over a large ledge 30 meters below. The narrative explicitly states this was 'a case of jumping from the wrong exit point.' The other jumper, positioned roughly a meter to the left, barely cleared the ledge, while Bjørn Emil's exit position on the right side — where the ledge gradually protruded further — made clearance impossible. The decision to exit from that specific point, given the terrain geometry, was the producing mechanism of the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states this was 'a case of jumping from the wrong exit point.' The jumper exited from a position he apparently believed provided adequate clearance, but the ledge gradually protruded further on the right side where he stood. This indicates he misjudged or was unaware of the terrain geometry at his specific exit position relative to the ledge below — he was effectively at a different position relative to the terrain than he believed.

L0_description: The jumper performed a multi-way aerial (totem pole front flip) from a cliff, broke off facing the cliff, had to execute a 180° turn, and deployed too late for the canopy to fully inflate before impact. The narrative explicitly identifies 'extracted pilot chute too late' and 'loss of altitude awareness' as factors, with the aerials consuming altitude and the post-breakoff correction further delaying deployment. This constitutes human error in deployment timing and altitude management during an advanced maneuver.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of altitude awareness
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After performing a multi-person aerial exit (totem pole front flip), the jumper broke off facing the cliff, rotated 180 degrees to face away, and deployed very low. The canopy extracted but did not fully inflate before impact. The narrative indicates the jumper lost altitude awareness while focusing on turning away from the cliff, resulting in a fatally late deployment. The aerials were intentional and within the jumper's chosen plan; the fatal mechanism was the failure to monitor altitude and deploy in time.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper appeared to focus on turning himself 180 degrees away from the cliff rather than monitoring altitude. This channelized attention on the heading correction task came at the expense of altitude awareness, directly contributing to the fatally late deployment.

L0_description: The jumper performed a gainer (aerial maneuver) and became entangled with the bridle upon deployment. The domain reference explicitly identifies bridle entanglements as a known risk of aerials, as equipment is designed for belly-to-earth deployment and deploying from non-standard body positions risks bridle wrapping around limbs or torso. The decision to perform an aerial and the resulting entanglement constitute human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle entanglement during aerial deployment
