L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited too head-down, which is a recognized wingsuit exit technique error. This caused him to lose altitude during recovery, left him pointed toward higher terrain on the left, and forced a corrective turn to the right that consumed additional altitude. The cascading altitude loss from the poor exit ultimately left him too low to clear terrain or deploy, resulting in high-speed ground impact.

L0_description: The jumper attempted a BASE jump from a 14-story building using a skydiving canopy (Sabre 2 150 sqft, slider up) rather than BASE-specific equipment, with only 1 prior BASE jump, fewer than 200 skydives, after drinking alcohol, into an unsuitable landing area. Multiple human errors are clearly present: use of inappropriate equipment (skydiving gear for a BASE jump), grossly insufficient experience, impaired judgment from alcohol consumption, and inadequate assessment of the object and landing area.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped with skydiving gear from building
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump a 14-story building using a skydiving canopy (Sabre 2 150 sqft) in slider-up configuration. This is a fundamentally inappropriate equipment choice for a building jump: skydiving canopies are not designed for BASE applications, slider-up configuration from a building provides insufficient altitude for the slow opening sequence, and the canopy's flight characteristics (high aspect ratio, high forward speed, no deep brake setting) are unsuitable for the confined landing environment. The decision to use skydive gear for a BASE jump was the producing mechanism for the fatality, as the equipment could not function adequately for this application regardless of execution quality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted BASE jump without training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had only 147 skydives (below the accepted 200+ minimum prerequisite) and had completed only 1 prior BASE jump. He had no BASE-specific equipment, no apparent FJC or mentorship, and attempted a building jump — a highly technical object requiring experienced jumpers with refined canopy skills and understanding of urban wind effects. This represents a clear departure from accepted progression norms and a decision that was identifiably inadequate.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper had been drinking at a bar earlier that evening before deciding to jump. Alcohol impairment degrades judgment and decision-making capacity, directly contributing to the decisions to attempt a BASE jump with skydiving gear and without adequate training or experience.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had 147 skydives, below the accepted 200+ minimum prerequisite for beginning BASE jumping. This insufficient foundation contributed to his inability to recognize that skydiving equipment is fundamentally unsuitable for BASE applications.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 prior BASE jump and no evidence of completing a first jump course or having competent mentorship. The narrative explicitly states he lacked the knowledge to understand the critical differences between skydiving and BASE gear and the hazards of the specific situation. Without BASE-specific training, he could not recognize that a skydiving canopy in slider-up configuration from a 14-story building was non-survivable.

L0_description: The narrative describes a jumper with only 5 BASE jumps and 60 skydives (well below the accepted minimum of 200) who either failed to verify proper bridle routing before exit (bridle passing in front of arm) or improperly deployed the pilot chute over rather than under the arm, causing the bridle to entangle around the arm. Both hypotheses describe human errors in rigging verification or deployment technique that led to premature break cord rupture, poor body position, and a fatal PC in tow or entanglement.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle routing/deployment error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper either failed to perform a final check of the static line routing (allowing the bridle to pass in front of his right arm) or incorrectly threw the pilot chute over rather than under his arm, resulting in the bridle becoming entangled around his arm. This execution error caused premature break cord rupture, body position disruption, and a compromised deployment that the jumper could not recover from at the available altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 BASE jumps, 60 skydives, and 1 BASE season. With 60 skydives he was far below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, and with only 5 BASE jumps he had minimal experience with static line deployment procedures. This insufficient experience base directly contributed to the execution error in bridle routing or pilot chute handling during the static line deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform final gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that under the first hypothesis, the jumper did not perform a final check of the static line routing before jumping, which would have caught the bridle passing in front of his right arm. Accepted practice for static line jumps explicitly requires that every jumper have a knowledgeable person verify they are clear of rigging before jumping by tracing the bridle by hand from shrivel flap to anchor. This omitted check would have detected and prevented the routing error.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'in the rush before the jump' as a contributing factor to the jumper not performing a final check of the static line routing. This haste directly contributed to the execution error by causing the jumper to skip the verification step that would have caught the bridle misrouting.

L0_description: The jumpers encountered strong southwest winds (~50km/h) at the original exit point, moved to a lee-side exit believing the wind shadow made conditions acceptable, but were struck by severe turbulence and rotor wind upon leaving the sheltered area. The strong winds were known before the jump, experienced local jumpers had declined to jump due to conditions, and the nearby paragliding ramp was closed for high winds. The decision to jump in these conditions constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in known strong winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumpers knew there were strong southwest winds (~50km/h) at the primary exit point and relocated to a secondary exit that was in the lee of a mountain, mistaking the wind shadow for safe conditions. The decision to jump was clearly and specifically wrong: the paraglider/hang glider ramp nearby was closed due to strong winds, all experienced local jumpers they contacted had declined to jump due to unsuitable weather, and the strong winds were a known macro condition that would be encountered once leaving the wind shadow. This decision directly produced the fatal outcome when the jumper exited the lee-side shelter and encountered severe turbulence and rotor winds that dragged him into buildings and a highway.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 50 skydives and 40 BASE jumps in 1 BASE season. With skydiving experience far below the accepted minimum of 200+ jumps before beginning BASE, and limited BASE experience, the jumper lacked the foundational knowledge and judgment to correctly evaluate the lee-side wind shadow as dangerous. This insufficient experience directly contributed to the flawed decision to jump in strong wind conditions that all experienced local jumpers recognized as unsuitable.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumpers had 'only a few days to be in that area,' indicating time pressure from a limited trip window contributed to the decision to jump despite strong winds. This external pressure drove them to relocate to a secondary exit and proceed rather than wait for suitable conditions, even after experienced local jumpers had all declined to jump that day.

L0_description: The jumper exited from a point with considerable talus to clear into tight terrain, where the first jumper barely cleared the obstacle. The deceased jumper failed to clear the talus and impacted subterminal near the exit with no space for parachute deployment. This indicates errors in exit point selection and/or exit execution at an object that demanded mandatory perfection, constituting identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose exit requiring mandatory clearance beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to exit from a point with considerable talus to clear into tight terrain, after observing the first jumper barely clear the same obstacle. The decision to jump from this exit point was clearly and specifically inappropriate given the observable evidence that clearance was marginal even for the preceding jumper, and the terrain offered no margin for a below-average start. This was the producing mechanism: the exit point selection committed the jumper to a flight path that required near-perfect performance with no tolerance for error, resulting in subterminal impact on the talus.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying a wingsuit along a line requiring him to clear a ridge. He reached a reference point with less speed than needed, flew a suboptimal path (going slightly left past an outcrop rather than straight to the ridge), and by the time he recognized he was low and cornered, he lacked sufficient energy to clear the ridge. This represents decision and execution errors in line selection, speed management, and commitment to a no-pull zone without adequate margin.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate speed management during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reached reference points on level but with less speed than required. After the left corner, he deviated slightly left past an outcrop rather than flying straight to the ridge, then made a slight right turn to line up. By that point he was low and cornered with insufficient energy to clear the ridge. The flare attempt failed because the suit was underpowered at that speed. This represents a failure in execution of the chosen flight line — inadequate speed/energy management and suboptimal trajectory through the technical section — rather than a fundamentally wrong decision, given he had practiced this line on multiple prior jumps.

L0_description: The jumper had only 10 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season, yet was jumping a cliff (Earth object) slider-down with BOC deployment. With such minimal experience, the jumper lacked the skill set for instant off-heading correction at a cliff. The 180-degree off-heading resulted in object strike, and the jumper's extremely low experience level for this object type represents a clear human error in progression decisions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed off-heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the canopy opened fully, it turned left and back toward the cliff, resulting in object strike. The jumper failed to execute a timely heading correction (via rear risers or brakes) to prevent flying back into the cliff. With only 10 BASE jumps, the jumper likely lacked the practiced muscle memory for instant off-heading corrections that could have prevented the object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 10 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. Instant off-heading correction via rear risers or brakes is a skill that requires extensive practice at forgiving objects (spans) to develop the muscle memory needed for reflexive response. With 10 jumps, the jumper had insufficient experience to have developed reliable off-heading correction skills, directly contributing to the failure to arrest the 180° turn before object strike.

L0_label: Wingsuit exit failure and low pull
L0_description: The jumper had known problems with wingsuit starts and was warned this site was not appropriate for addressing them. With only 3 wingsuit BASE jumps, the jumper attempted a wingsuit BASE jump from a site beyond his skill level, resulting in a poor exit, insufficient altitude/separation, and a fatally late deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped site beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had only 3 wingsuit BASE jumps and was known to have problems with his starts (wingsuit exits). He was specifically warned that this site was not appropriate for fixing or learning proper wingsuit exits, yet he chose to jump it anyway. With 3 WS BASE jumps and acknowledged start problems, the decision to jump a site that did not tolerate poor starts was a clearly inappropriate decision given the information available to him.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor wingsuit exit and delayed deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper most likely had a poor exit, lost altitude trying to get the wingsuit flying, and by the time he decided to deploy it was too late. The canopy was found out but had insufficient altitude to inflate, resulting in death from low pull. The execution failure in the exit and the delayed deployment decision during the emergency were the direct producing mechanisms of the fatality.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 3 wingsuit BASE jumps and was known to have recurring problems with wingsuit starts. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE, and extensive practice before attempting sites that do not tolerate poor exits. With 3 WS BASE jumps and acknowledged start deficiencies, his experience was clearly insufficient for the site attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 3 wingsuit BASE jumps and known problems with starts, the jumper lacked the skill and experience to recover from a poor wingsuit exit and transition to effective flight or timely deployment. The execution failure in the exit directly reflects insufficient wingsuit BASE experience.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Start Problems
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was aware of his problems with wingsuit starts and had been warned by others, yet he did not correct this deficiency before attempting a site that required competent exits. This sustained failure to address a known recurring performance issue directly enabled the poor exit that produced the fatal outcome.

L0_description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with nothing out, indicating a total malfunction or failure to deploy. However, the narrative provides no specific evidence explaining why deployment did not occur. Possible factors listed (altitude awareness, katabatic airflow) are speculative and not substantiated by explicit evidence in the narrative. Low clouds below exit could have affected altitude awareness, but the mechanism connecting conditions to the failure to deploy is not established. There is insufficient information to clearly identify a human error pathway or to positively attribute the outcome to a non-human cause.

The jumper initiated his opening sequence too late after proximity flying, resulting in three mispulls with insufficient altitude remaining. The narrative explicitly states he lost his life because he initiated his opening sequence too late, and identifies his habit of pulling low and proximity flying as contributing factors. Friends had warned him about his low-pull habit prior to the accident.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment initiation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Bryce initiated his opening sequence too late after proximity flying, leaving insufficient altitude to recover from execution problems. The narrative explicitly states he would be alive if he had disconnected from terrain and begun his pull sequence three seconds earlier. He had been warned by close friends about his 'bad habit of pulling low' and had already experienced a consequence (water landing after low pull from Altissimo), yet continued the practice. The decision to delay deployment initiation until an altitude with zero margin for error was the producing mechanism — the subsequent mispulls, which would have been survivable at adequate altitude, became fatal only because of this decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed pilot chute extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Bryce failed to extract the pilot chute on his first and second attempts, and his third attempt produced only a weak pull. The deployment sequence took approximately 6 seconds across three attempts (00:48 to 00:54), with the PC finally out at 00:54 and impact at 00:55. He was not wearing gloves or extra layers, so the mispulls represent a technique/execution failure in BOC extraction during wingsuit flight rather than a gear-access issue. While the low pull altitude was the decision that made these mispulls fatal, the mispulls themselves were an independent execution failure that directly contributed to the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Bryce had done 70-80 wingsuit BASE jumps in one month, was 'very confident and feeling great in his suit,' and 'got quickly bored in Lauterbrunnen and started to fly proximity.' Despite warnings from close friends about his 'bad habit of pulling low' and a prior consequence (water landing at Altissimo), he continued the practice. This pattern of escalating confidence overriding external feedback and personal experience directly enabled his decision to delay deployment initiation.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: On the morning of the accident, Bryce told his partner Gabe that he 'felt tired.' The investigation summary explicitly identifies that long BASE trips can be exhausting and lists physical/mental tiredness as a contributing factor. This fatigue may have degraded his judgment regarding deployment timing, contributing to his decision to initiate the pull sequence too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct low-pull habit
L2_preconditions.description: Bryce had developed a recognized pattern of pulling low. He had already experienced a consequence (water landing after a low pull from Altissimo weeks earlier), and two close friends explicitly warned him about this habit the day before the fatal jump. Despite this awareness and feedback, he failed to correct the behavior, directly enabling the repeated decision to delay deployment initiation.

L0_description: The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, and the jumper was found on terrain under the exit point, suggesting a problem at or shortly after exit. However, the camera and GPS were lost in the crash, weather was not reported as a factor, and the theory about an immediate turn to the right combined with a poor exit is explicitly described as conjecture. There is insufficient evidence to confirm a specific human error pathway or to establish a non-human cause.

The jumper exited a slider-up cliff object but exhibited no freefall control and no tracking during a 10-second delay, resulting in a 100-110 degree side rotation and insufficient separation from the wall. The canopy opened very close to the wall with a significant off-heading from exit direction, leading to object strike within 1.5 seconds. With only 144 skydives (below the generally accepted minimum of 200), 16 BASE jumps, and 2 BASE seasons, the jumper lacked the experience and skill for a terminal slider-up cliff requiring mandatory tracking, constituting clear human error in both execution and the decision to attempt this jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to track for object separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper took a 10-second delay from a slider-up cliff but performed no tracking whatsoever, and experienced 100-110 degrees of uncontrolled side rotation during freefall. Sub-terminal tracking is mandatory for slider-up cliffs to gain separation from the object. The complete absence of tracking and freefall control resulted in opening very close to the wall with insufficient separation, directly producing the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: No corrective action under canopy
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After opening 120-140 degrees off-heading from her exit push direction and very close to the wall, the jumper took no visible corrective action on the canopy during the 1.5 seconds before impact. While time was extremely limited, the absence of any heading correction attempt (rear risers or brakes) contributed to the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 16 BASE jumps, 2 BASE seasons, and 144 skydives (below the accepted minimum of 200+ before beginning BASE). A slider-up cliff requiring a 10-second delay with mandatory tracking represents an advanced BASE task. The complete absence of freefall control and tracking — fundamental skills for slider-up cliff jumping — indicates experience and skill clearly insufficient for this task, directly contributing to the failure to track and maintain body control.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 144 skydives, the jumper had not reached the generally accepted minimum of 200-250 skydives before beginning BASE jumping. Skydiving develops the core freefall body control and canopy piloting skills that are prerequisites for BASE. The inability to control body position or initiate tracking during a 10-second freefall reflects this foundational skill deficit.

L0_description: The jumper performed a very head-high exit with a fully exposed leg wing, resulting in a scorpion that drifted the jumper backwards toward the wall. This exit technique error is a clearly identified human error that directly led to insufficient object separation and fatal impact with a ledge at 5 seconds.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a very head-high exit with a fully exposed leg wing, resulting in a scorpion that drifted the jumper backwards toward the wall. This execution error in exit technique directly produced insufficient object separation, causing the jumper to clip a ledge at 5 seconds. The exit technique failure was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact.

L0_description: The narrative states the jumper was found with chute out and slider still up, indicating a low or mis-timed pull. The possible factors field identifies 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late.' This points to a human error in deployment timing, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to fully open.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was found with the parachute out and slider still up, indicating the pilot chute was extracted too late to allow sufficient altitude for the canopy to fully deploy. The cause of death was low pull, and the possible factors note 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late,' indicating a failure in execution timing rather than a decision to delay intentionally beyond plan.

L0_description: The jumper impacted terrain subterminal near the exit point while flying a technical proximity line from a short-start cliff in a one-piece tracking suit. The narrative identifies possible factors including unstable exit, loss of speed/glide, late pilot chute extraction, and altitude awareness issues. The conditions included strong thermals with turbulent sink and lift, and the narrative explicitly states the death was preventable, with lessons about short starts in tracking suits and not trusting lift conditions. These factors point to human error in the decision to fly a technical proximity line under those conditions and/or execution errors during the flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed exit/start execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted terrain between the first and second ledge at approximately 270m below the 2170m exit point, dying from subterminal impact near the exit. Given that he was flying a one-piece tracking suit (not a wingsuit) from a site requiring a technical start, the fatal outcome was produced by a failure in execution during the exit and initial flight phase — whether through a weak/offset push, failure to achieve a flying position quickly enough, or inability to build adequate speed and separation from terrain. The jumper had previously jumped this exit successfully in the same suit, indicating the decision to jump the line was within his demonstrated capability, but execution on this occasion was fatally insufficient.

L2_preconditions.label: Turbulent thermal conditions
L2_preconditions.description: Another BASE jumper present that day reported conditions were extremely turbulent and noisy to fly in with a lot of sink and lift closer to the ground. Strong anabatic conditions were present at exit. These atmospheric disturbances (turbulence, sink, and lift) would have directly degraded the jumper's ability to build speed, maintain a stable flight path, and achieve adequate terrain separation during the critical start phase in a one-piece tracking suit, which has minimal margin compared to a wingsuit.

L0_description: The jumper was solo with no witnesses and no video evidence available at the time of reporting. The narrative is explicitly based on guesses and assumptions from another jumper's interpretation of FlySight data. While the analyst speculates about thermal loss causing insufficient separation from terrain, there is insufficient confirmed evidence to definitively classify this as human error (such as a clear decision to fly too close to terrain or misjudge conditions) or as an unforeseeable environmental event (002OC). The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, but the precise mechanism and contributing factors remain speculative.

The narrative states 'Unknown at this stage' and provides no details about the jumper's actions, experience, equipment condition, or environmental factors. The cause of death (subterminal impact near exit) indicates something went critically wrong very early in the jump, but without further information, neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be supported.

L0_label: Multiple Human Errors in Rigging and Execution
L0_description: Multiple human errors are clearly identified: a non-BASE-jumper was used to give the PCA and dropped the PC too early, resulting in inadequate deployment; the jumper used a 42-inch PC despite being advised to use a 48-inch (undersized PC for the application); and the rigging setup was identified as incorrect. These errors led to a total or partial malfunction resulting in object strike and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unqualified PCA assistant
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper arranged for a non-BASE-jumper to perform the PCA. The PCA assistant dropped the PC early, which at this cliff is described as 'catastrophic.' Entrusting the PCA—a life-critical deployment method where the assistant's timing and technique directly determine canopy extraction—to an unqualified person was a decision that produced the fatal malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Undersized pilot chute selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was advised by friends to use a 48-inch PC but chose to use a 42-inch PC instead. For a PCA deployment at a slider-down cliff with minimal altitude, the smaller PC provided less drag force, compounding the early-release error and contributing to the PC-in-tow / total malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 BASE season and 100 BASE jumps, and the narrative indicates 'No or little training' as a possible factor. Arranging a PCA from an unqualified non-jumper and disregarding advice on PC sizing reflect a knowledge gap about PCA requirements and equipment selection that accepted BASE training and mentorship would have addressed.

L2_preconditions.label: Unqualified PCA assistant not briefed/replaced
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to ensure a qualified person performed the PCA. Accepted practice requires a knowledgeable person to hold the bridle (not just the PC), maintain position, and release at the correct moment. Using a non-BASE-jumper without apparent training or briefing as the PCA assistant was a preparatory failure that directly enabled the early release and resulting malfunction.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states uncertainty about whether the jumper fell before setting up his static line (slip/fall) or whether his static line failed. No anchor point or break cord was found at the scene or on the ground, and the investigators acknowledge they will likely never know what happened. Without evidence to distinguish between a human error pathway (e.g., climbing without being secured, improper rigging) and a potential equipment/anchor failure, neither 001HE nor 002OC can be adequately supported.

The jumper attempted a wingsuit BASE jump from an exit point that transitions directly into terrain flight with very little to no margin for error. The jumper exhibited insufficient forward speed during the start arc and was unable to outfly the terrain, impacting the talus approximately six seconds after exit. The narrative identifies multiple human error factors: the jump selection itself had minimal margin for error, the jumper lacked terrain flying experience, forward speed was lower than expected, and the suit selection may not have been optimal for the demanding start arc.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Selected object beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper selected an exit point (Super Gorgeous) that by the witness's own account had 'very little to no margin for error' and transitioned 'directly to terrain flight immediately after exit,' where even the experienced witness could not 'outfly the terrain with any additional altitude beyond what was needed to fly at a minimum.' The jumper was not a terrain flyer, yet chose an object that required immediate terrain flying from the start arc. This was a decision that was clearly and specifically inappropriate given the jumper's skill set — the object demanded a specialized capability (terrain flying) that the jumper did not possess, and the zero-margin nature of the jump meant any shortfall in start arc performance would be fatal.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit and flight performance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The witness observed that the jumper's forward speed was 'not quite as high as I would expect during the first four to five seconds,' he was slightly right shoulder low from the start, and was crabbing right to left rather than achieving clean forward flight. This suboptimal exit and flight execution — insufficient speed and imperfect body configuration during the critical start arc — meant the jumper failed to generate the glide performance needed to clear the terrain, resulting in impact approximately six seconds after exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient terrain flying experience
L2_preconditions.description: The witness explicitly states 'I have not known Jimmy to be a terrain flyer,' yet the selected exit point required immediate terrain flying from the start arc with zero margin for error. This indicates the jumper lacked the specific wingsuit terrain flying experience and skill set required for this object, which directly contributed to the decision to attempt a jump that demanded capabilities beyond his experience level.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Despite being told explicitly by the witness that the start arc transitions directly to terrain flight, that even the experienced witness could not outfly the terrain beyond the minimum needed, and despite not being a terrain flyer himself, the jumper proceeded with the jump. The narrative indicates he believed he was 'current and ready' for his first wingsuit BASE jump in Moab, suggesting he unreasonably overestimated his ability to manage a zero-margin terrain flight that exceeded his demonstrated skill set.

L0_description: The jumper chose to jump slider-down from an exit that is predominantly wingsuited (slider-up/terminal), indicating significant vertical relief. Jumping slider-down from a terminal cliff with a slightly positive wall means the canopy opens with forward speed toward the wall and minimal separation. The 180-degree off-heading resulted in immediate object strike, a foreseeable consequence of this configuration choice at this object. Additionally, performing a gainer adds complexity and risk of off-heading on what was already a marginal equipment configuration for the site.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Off-heading opening from gainer
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a gainer exit from a slider-up cliff and experienced a 180-degree off-heading opening to the left, resulting in immediate object strike against a slightly positive wall. The exit was described as looking 'solid and symmetrical,' suggesting the intended technique was correct but the execution of the deployment sequence (body position at deployment, PC deployment, or heading management during opening) was insufficient, producing the fatal 180-degree off-heading with inadequate separation from the wall.

L0_description: Thomas performed a one-step exit that was slightly unstable, brought his arms back too soon causing an over-rotation/scorpion, then corrected to a flat tracking position that lacked forward movement. This sequence resulted in insufficient separation to clear the ledge, leading to a ledge strike, violent rotation, disorientation, and fatal wall impact with no deployment. The exit technique errors (arm timing, body position correction to flat rather than maintaining head-down) constitute identifiable human errors in the deployment chain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit causing insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Thomas performed a one-step exit that was slightly unstable, brought his arms back too soon with legs slightly bent, causing an over-rotation/scorpion. His correction into a flat tracking position rather than maintaining a steeper angle resulted in clipping the ledge with his lower legs by approximately 20cm, sending him into violent unstable rotation from which he did not recover or deploy his parachute before impacting the wall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After clipping the ledge and being sent into violent rotation, approximately 6 seconds passed before wall impact with nothing deployed. The narrative notes a panic pull might have saved his life, indicating that despite available time, Thomas failed to initiate deployment, likely due to disorientation or shock from the impact with the ledge.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: After clipping the ledge and being sent into violent unstable rotation, the narrative states Thomas was 'probably disoriented or in shock' during the 6 seconds before wall impact. The violent physical impact with the ledge and resulting tumble likely triggered a startle/freeze response or spatial disorientation that prevented him from initiating deployment despite available time.

L2_preconditions.label: Absence of anabatic lift
L2_preconditions.description: The jump occurred at 08:50, before significant solar heating could generate anabatic updraft on the cliff face. A fellow jumper who jumped at 07:50 the same morning reported being closer to the wall than usual. The absence of the lift normally present during later conditions reduced the vertical and horizontal separation margin, directly contributing to Thomas clipping the ledge by only 20cm despite an otherwise corrected track.

L0_label: Wingsuit terrain flight loss of performance
L0_description: The jumper lost speed and glide while flying a wingsuit terrain line ('Cachaca line') and impacted terrain in terminal freefall. Loss of speed and glide during wingsuit terrain flight indicates a failure to maintain adequate airspeed and available lift, which the domain reference identifies as a critical pilot-managed factor. This represents a human error in wingsuit flight management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed and glide
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper lost speed and glide during wingsuit flight along the Cachaca line, resulting in terrain impact in terminal freefall. The narrative explicitly states loss of speed-glide as the factor, indicating a failure in wingsuit piloting execution — maintaining adequate airspeed and angle of attack — rather than a decision error in selecting the line. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, meaning the jumper never deployed a parachute, consistent with flight into a no-pull zone after losing performance.

L0_description: The jumper flew a committed proximity line deeper and lower than usual, lost speed and altitude attempting to clear a ridge, stalled during a turn, and impacted terrain. The narrative explicitly describes deliberate flight path choices that left insufficient margin, constituting human error in flight planning and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committed proximity line with insufficient margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flying line that was deeper and lower than usual past terrain features, leaving no viable escape route. When he realized he was too low to clear the second ridge, his only option was a turn toward lower terrain, but this turn was itself a margin-consuming maneuver in close proximity. The narrative explicitly states he 'rarely jumped the same exit or flew the same line several times' and his lines were 'committed,' indicating a pattern of choosing novel, high-commitment flight paths. The specific line selection—flying deeper than usual, flattening out lower than usual past obstacles, with a ridge oriented 90° ahead—was a decision that eliminated outs and produced the fatal outcome independent of execution quality.

L2_preconditions.label: Novelty-seeking pattern
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'had a passion for beautiful flying lines and was always chasing novelty: he rarely jumped the same exit or flew the same line several times' and that 'his lines were committed, which he was totally aware of.' This enduring behavioral pattern of seeking novel, committed lines directly contributed to the decision to fly a deeper, lower, unfamiliar variation on this jump, reducing his margin and eliminating outs.

L0_description: The jumper held the pilot chute for approximately 6 seconds on a slider-down configuration, far exceeding the planned 1.5-second delay. According to the domain reference, slider-down deployments above 4 seconds are potentially dangerous to jumper and equipment, and a 6-second slider-down delay results in excessive airspeed causing a dangerously hard opening. The hard opening likely rendered him unconscious or dazed, preventing canopy control, resulting in a slow turn into the wall and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Excessive delay slider-down
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper held the pilot chute for approximately 6 seconds on a slider-down deployment from a 1300ft cliff, despite the pre-jump plan calling for a ~1.5 second delay. A 6-second delay slider-down results in deployment at near-terminal airspeed with a slider-down configuration, which is explicitly contraindicated (the PC/delay chart shows slider-down is dangerous above 4 seconds). This produced the hard opening that likely rendered him unconscious or dazed, leaving him unable to correct a slow off-heading turn into the wall.

L0_description: The narrative is absent and structured fields provide minimal detail. The cause of death is listed as 'PC in tow or PC-Bridle entanglement,' which could result from human error (weak PC toss, packing/rigging error) or equipment failure, but there is no information to distinguish between these. A night building jump (22:30) with only 3 BASE seasons is noted, but without further detail, neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be clearly supported.

The jumper experienced a total malfunction (impact with nothing out) after a hand-held deployment from a slider-down cliff jump. The narrative explicitly states the cause is 'only speculation' and lists multiple possible explanations (pin lock, bridle wrap, gear failure, incorrect pin tension from previous wingsuit configuration) without evidence to confirm any single cause. There is insufficient information to determine whether human error or equipment failure independent of human error was responsible.

The narrative describes a total malfunction caused by the bridle being wrapped around the jumper's leg, resulting in PC in tow with no line stretch. The witness explicitly identifies this as a rigging/setup error, noting the bridle was routed between the jumper's legs and not corrected before exit. This is a human error in pre-jump equipment configuration.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle routing error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited with the bridle routed between/around his leg, causing a bridle entanglement that prevented the static line deployment from achieving line stretch. The witness describes nearly making the same mistake himself - routing the bridle between the legs when stepping over the railing - indicating this was an execution error during exit preparation rather than a deliberate decision. The entanglement resulted in PC in tow / no line stretch and closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to verify bridle routing before exit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to perform a final gear check that would have detected the bridle routed around his leg before exiting. The witness describes that he himself nearly made the identical error and caught it only by chance (a bird landing drew his eyes to his crotch area), indicating that a deliberate trace of the bridle from shrivel flap to anchor — which accepted static-line practice requires — was not performed or was inadequate. A proper pre-exit bridle check would have caught and corrected the misrouting.

L0_description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall during a wingsuit proximity flight. Evidence strongly indicates he struck a tree while flying along a ridge during proximity terrain flying, a deliberate flight path choice that placed him within the no-margin zone near terrain. This represents a human error in flight path selection or execution during proximity flying, consistent with the listed possible factors of loss of speed-glide and proximity flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Terrain impact during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted a tree while flying in proximity along the ridge to the left of the exit point, prior to making a planned left turn toward the tree gates. Despite being described as a technically proficient flyer with nearly perfect starts, the jumper failed to maintain adequate separation from terrain/obstacles during the proximity portion of the flight. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating no deployment was initiated, consistent with an unexpected collision with a tree during active wingsuit flight.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening resulting in fatal object strike. Multiple human error factors are identified: insufficient forward separation on exit ('not the greatest push'), insufficient experience (only 50 BASE jumps, 100 skydives — well below accepted minimums), lack of currency ('a winter without jumping'), and poor object selection for skill level ('second slider down stowed from a cliff,' site described as 'not really a cliff for slider down'). The mentor's report explicitly identifies complacency, bad group dynamics, and inadequate progression as contributing factors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient exit push and unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited with 'not the greatest push, maybe a bit to the side' and pulled immediately after exit from a slider-down cliff. The inadequate forward separation and possibly asymmetric exit produced insufficient object clearance, and the canopy opened with a 180° off-heading resulting in object strike. The jumper intended to exit and deploy successfully but failed in execution quality — the exit push and body position were insufficient to generate the separation needed to survive an off-heading opening.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 100 skydives (well below the accepted minimum of 200+) and approximately 50 BASE jumps across 2 seasons. This was described as only his second slider-down stowed deployment from a cliff. The domain reference establishes that slider-down cliff jumping requires refined exit and deployment skills developed through extensive progression. His limited experience directly relates to the insufficient exit push and immediate pull that failed to generate adequate object separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's mentor explicitly identified complacency as a contributing factor, attributing it to the jumper's positive personality traits and confidence from paragliding experience that led to reduced vigilance about execution quality. This complacency contributed to the insufficient exit push and immediate deployment without adequate separation from the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had 'a winter without jumping,' indicating an extended period without BASE activity before this fatal jump. This lack of currency would have degraded the motor skills and timing needed for a quality exit push and proper deployment sequence from a slider-down cliff, directly contributing to the execution errors identified in L1.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration for site
L2_preconditions.description: The mentor's report states the cliff was 'not really a cliff for slider down' — at 500m and slightly overhanging with extensive cliff below, the site characteristics combined with a slider-down configuration (which produces rapid openings with less time for heading correction) created conditions where the insufficient exit push became fatal. A slider-up configuration with delay and tracking would have provided greater object separation.

L0_description: The jumper was wingsuit proximity flying and impacted terrain in terminal freefall, indicating failure to maintain adequate speed, glide, or terrain separation. The narrative describes the flight appearing slower than expected, a line choice far right over terrain that witnesses considered difficult without thermals present in early morning conditions, and the possible factors list includes altitude awareness, loss of speed-glide, and proximity flying. These point to human error in flight path selection and/or airspeed management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate line selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Dylan chose a flight line far to the right over terrain that, according to the witness, would require thermals to be viable but was being flown in early morning calm conditions when no thermals were present. This line selection was the producing mechanism: without adequate thermal lift to support the chosen trajectory, the jumper lost glide performance over terrain and impacted in terminal freefall. The witness explicitly stated this line would be possible later with thermals but would be difficult in the early morning without them.

L0_description: The narrative from a close friend indicates the jumper flew a proximity line too close to terrain limits despite not having developed adequate wingsuit/tracking suit flying skills, with known instability issues (significant rolling and pitching). The friend states the jumper was 'pushing a lot' beyond his skill level and had been warned about this on more than one occasion. This constitutes identifiable human error in decision-making and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity line beyond skill level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flight line that was 'too close to the limit' despite not having developed adequate wingsuit/tracking suit flying skills. A close friend confirmed the jumper experienced significant rolling and pitching instability in the suit, that he 'wasn't very good at flying,' and that he had been warned on multiple occasions that he was pushing beyond his skill level. The decision to fly a proximity line requiring clean, precise flight with a suit he could not reliably control was a clearly inappropriate course of action that produced the fatal terrain impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit proficiency
L2_preconditions.description: A close friend stated the jumper 'wasn't very good at flying' the tracking suit and experienced 'significant rolling and pitching movements,' indicating he had not developed the skill level required for proximity flight. He had been warned on multiple occasions that he was pushing beyond his skill level, confirming his capability was insufficient for the task attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence despite warnings
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was 'pushing a lot in the last period before the accident' despite being told on more than one occasion that he had not yet developed the right skills. This pattern of disregarding repeated warnings from peers indicates an overestimation of his own capability that drove the decision to fly a proximity line beyond his ability.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known instability problem
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was aware of recurring instability issues (significant rolling and pitching) in his tracking suit and had been warned multiple times about pushing beyond his skill level, yet he continued to attempt proximity lines without correcting the underlying performance deficiency.

L0_description: The jumper exited very head-low and rotated onto his back, striking a ledge below the exit point in freefall. He had experienced a very head-low exit from the same object the previous day, which was a clear warning of an exit technique problem at this specific site. Returning to the same exit without correcting the technique constitutes a human error pathway.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable head-low exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited High Ultimate very head-low and transitioned onto his back, completing the first half of a front flip. This unstable exit resulted in an uncontrolled trajectory that caused him to impact a lower ledge subterminal. The jumper had experienced a very head-low exit from the same object the day prior, indicating a recurring execution problem with his exit technique at this specific site.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced a very head-low exit from the same object the day prior and was aware of the problem, yet returned the next day without correcting the underlying technique issue. His comment about having enough separation to do an emergency front flip and still clear the ledge indicates he recognized the head-low tendency but rationalized it rather than addressing it.

L0_description: The accident narrative states 'Unknown at this time' and provides no details about the sequence of events leading to the fatal impact. While possible factors (katabatic airflow, proximity flying) and strong winds are noted, there is insufficient information to determine whether human error or other causes led to the terminal freefall impact. No specific error pathway or non-human cause can be established from the available data.

The jumper was performing wingsuit proximity flying along a terrain line he had flown multiple times. After passing the houses, he turned right into terrain instead of left toward clear airspace, became boxed in below tree level, attempted a maximum flare to clear the trees but was unable to do so, and impacted terrain in terminal freefall with no canopy deployed. The decision to fly a line that left insufficient margin to clear terrain, combined with possible altitude/terrain awareness errors during the flight, constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committed to unsurvivable proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a flight line that took him right into terrain below tree level with no escape route, rather than turning left to the known exit from the terrain. The narrative describes him turning right 'into what looked like flat terrain' after the houses, becoming boxed in below tree level with no way to turn left to fly out. Despite intimate knowledge of the terrain from 15-16 prior flights, the chosen line committed him to a path where even a maximum flare was insufficient to clear the trees, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. The decision to fly that specific line segment—rather than taking the known exit to the left—was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal turbulence degraded flight
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly describes hitting 'a wall of lift' when crossing the ridge onto the sun-exposed south side, flying in 'turbulent air and probably a bit slower than expected,' and the witness observing Chris 'struggling a bit.' These thermal conditions mechanically degraded flight performance—reducing speed and available lift—which directly contributed to the jumper being unable to execute his intended line or maintain adequate margin when he committed to the right turn into terrain.

L0_label: Aerial exit led to object strike
L0_description: The jumper performed an aerial maneuver (tweener) from a cliff, resulting in a 140-degree off-heading opening with line twists, leaving insufficient time or ability to correct heading before striking the wall. Performing aerials from a slider-down cliff object introduces known risks including off-headings and instability at deployment, and the domain reference identifies aerials as exponentially increasing danger. Additionally, the wingloading of ~0.87 lbs/sqft significantly exceeded the accepted mid-range of 0.70 and the upper recommended range of 0.80, which would increase forward speed on opening and reduce time available for heading correction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable aerial exit causing off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a 'tweener' (aerial maneuver) from a slider-down cliff jump and experienced a 140-degree off-heading opening with line twists. The aerial likely contributed to an unstable body position at deployment, producing the off-heading and line twist that sent him into the wall. The execution of the aerial and/or deployment was insufficient, as evidenced by the severe off-heading and line twist, resulting in object strike before any correction could be made.

L0_description: The jumper impacted terrain during wingsuit proximity flight near a glacier. The narrative describes flying a technical line with several sharp turns close to the ground, with the companion noting they flew closer than other times. Multiple consecutive tight turns caused speed loss, and katabatic airflow contributed to sink, but the decision to fly a technical proximity line at that closeness to terrain represents human error in flight path management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew too close to terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew a technical wingsuit line with multiple sharp turns extremely close to the ground over a glacier, in conditions where katabatic airflow was producing sinking air. The companion explicitly states the main reason for the accident was 'flying so close to the ground' and that they flew closer than on previous occasions. The combination of consecutive tight turns (which bleed speed and available lift) at minimal terrain clearance in sinking katabatic conditions left no margin for recovery, resulting in terrain impact at terminal velocity.

L2_preconditions.label: Personality Style
L2_preconditions.description: Friends explicitly described Jonny as having 'a tendency to go all out and fly right at the limit,' indicating an enduring behavioral pattern of pushing margins. This stable pattern directly contributed to the decision to fly closer to terrain than on previous occasions on a technical line with multiple sharp turns.

L2_preconditions.label: Katabatic airflow over glacier
L2_preconditions.description: The companion reported that katabatic wind over the glacier produced sinking air that pushed them toward the ground, particularly noticeable at the end of the glacier where the impact occurred. This atmospheric condition mechanically degraded the jumper's available lift and effective glide, directly contributing to the inability to maintain terrain clearance despite the chosen flight path.

L0_description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit close to terrain and most probably failed to recognize in time that he could not clear a hill, attempted a late escape maneuver, became trapped in a cove, and impacted. The narrative explicitly states he habitually flew close to terrain despite low experience and repeated warnings from peers, and that he made a mistake in his decisions during the flight. The possible factors also indicate he extracted his pilot chute too late, resulting in PC in tow or PC-bridle entanglement, consistent with a late emergency deployment attempt.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient terrain margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew a wingsuit proximity line too close to terrain, leaving insufficient margin to clear a hill. The companion's reconstruction indicates he realized too late he could not pass the terrain feature, attempted an escape left into a bowl, and impacted. Multiple experienced peers had repeatedly warned him about flying close to terrain despite his low experience level (80 BASE jumps, 3 seasons), indicating a pattern of accepting clearly inappropriate risk in proximity flying.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple close friends and the companion explicitly stated that the jumper habitually flew close to terrain despite his low experience level (80 BASE jumps, 3 seasons) and was told on numerous occasions to slow down. This pattern of repeatedly disregarding warnings from experienced peers indicates the jumper systematically overestimated his ability to manage proximity flying relative to his actual skill and experience level.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to heed repeated warnings
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was warned on numerous occasions by multiple close friends and his companion to stop flying close to terrain given his experience level. Despite this sustained feedback over time, he failed to correct the behavior, representing a pattern of inaction in the face of known deficiency that directly enabled the fatal proximity flying decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 80 BASE jumps across 3 seasons and no reported wingsuit BASE jump count, the jumper was flying proximity terrain lines that his peers explicitly identified as beyond his experience level. Accepted progression norms require extensive wingsuit BASE experience before proximity terrain flying, and the narrative confirms multiple experienced jumpers assessed his experience as insufficient for the flying he was attempting.

L0_description: The jumper impacted very close to the exit in terminal freefall, indicating failure to achieve flight. The narrative identifies two probable causes: slipping at the exit or jumping too head-low, and notes the jumper had a known tendency for head-low exits. Either scenario represents a human error in exit execution that prevented the wingsuit from generating adequate lift to fly away from terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable/head-low exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall very close to the exit point, indicating failure to achieve a flyable wingsuit configuration after launch. The narrative reports two plausible mechanisms: slipping at the exit or jumping too head-low. The jumper was known to have head-low exits ('He use to have head low exits'), and either a slip or an excessively head-low launch represents a technique/execution error that prevented the wingsuit from generating adequate lift to fly away from terrain. The cause of death—terminal freefall impact close to the exit—confirms the exit execution failure directly produced the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct recurring head-low exits
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'He use to have head low exits,' indicating a known, recurring performance deficiency. The jumper's failure to correct this pattern over time directly contributed to the fatal exit execution error.

L0_label: Gainer leads to fatal object strike
L0_description: The jumper performed a gainer (aerial exit) from a slider-up cliff despite primarily being a wingsuit jumper with limited aerobatic experience. The gainer led to over-rotation, instability, insufficient forward separation, and a 180-degree off-heading opening facing the wall, resulting in object strike. The decision to perform the gainer and the resulting unstable body position constitute identifiable human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate aerial maneuver
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to perform a gainer (backflip exit) from a slider-up cliff object despite only ever jumping his wingsuit at this type of object, having no established competence in aerials from slick cliff jumps. The domain reference explicitly states aerials exponentially increase danger, require careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object, and that extensive training is mandatory. The gainer caused over-rotation and instability that directly led to poor body position, inadequate object separation, and a 180-degree off-heading opening facing the wall, producing the fatal sequence of object strikes.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient aerial training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's partner explicitly noted he 'only ever jumps his wingsuit' at cliff objects, and she told him performing a gainer was 'a stupid idea' for that reason. The domain reference states aerials require extensive training, careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object. Performing a gainer from a slider-up cliff without established competence in slick aerial exits represents insufficient training for the specific task attempted, directly contributing to the over-rotation and instability.

L2_preconditions.label: Camera mount as snag hazard
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's helmet was equipped with an Insta360 camera on a unicorn extension mount with a protruding 6cm bolt. After the gainer-induced 180-degree off-heading opening, the front left riser swoop loop snagged on this bolt, converting a survivable object strike sequence into a sustained, unrecoverable entanglement against the wall. While the camera mount did not cause the gainer decision, the failure to identify and mitigate this specific snag hazard in pre-jump preparation directly amplified the consequences of the L1 error.

L0_description: The jumper changed to a new, higher exit point on the day of the jump, which was offset from the planned flight axis. The terrain assessment from this new exit was inadequate—a ledge on the flight trajectory was imperceptible from the exit point, and the couloir's details were obscured by shade. The decision to use an insufficiently evaluated exit point, combined with the resulting inability to execute the flight plan, constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Changed exit point without adequate assessment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: On arrival, the jumper and partner chose a new exit point 20 meters higher than the planned one, offset to the right of the flight axis and the deepest part of the couloir. The new exit was in a shaded couloir where landscape details—including a ledge on the flight trajectory—were imperceptible from the exit point. The decision to use this inadequately assessed exit point produced a flight plan that 'did not correspond to reality,' placing the jumper on a collision course with hidden terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Overcorrection during sub-terminal wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Upon recognizing the hidden ledge, the jumper initiated a left input to avoid it. At sub-terminal speed the input had little immediate effect, so he reinforced it. When airspeed increased seconds later, the cumulative input became excessive, causing an uncontrollable deviation to the left and impact with rock. This was an execution error—the correct action was identified but the magnitude of control input was poorly calibrated to the changing airspeed, directly producing the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Shaded couloir obscured terrain details
L2_preconditions.description: The exit point was located in a shaded couloir at that time of day, which the narrative explicitly states 'made it really difficult to see the details of the landscape.' This degraded visual condition directly impaired the jumper's ability to assess the terrain from the new exit point, preventing detection of the ledge on the flight trajectory and contributing to the inadequate assessment underlying the decision error.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial disorientation from visual illusion
L2_preconditions.description: The sub-terminal flight occurred in a shaded couloir where hidden terrain details only became visible once in flight. The jumper's inability to perceive the immediate aerodynamic effect of his control inputs at sub-terminal speed—reinforcing an input that would become excessive as airspeed built—reflects a perceptual mismatch between control input magnitude and changing flight dynamics in visually degraded conditions where terrain references were unreliable.

L0_description: The jumper's foot caught on the railing during exit, causing a head-low over-rotation that produced a 130-degree off-heading opening into the wall. The awkward exit position (upper body sideways, climbing over a rail at a difficult exit point) and the resulting foot snag constitute identifiable human errors in exit execution. Additionally, the hand-mounted camera may have contributed to delayed heading correction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited from an uncomfortable position with his upper body sideways to the left of the jump direction, resulting in a rotation to the right. His foot caught on the rail, causing a head-low over-rotation. This poor exit execution directly produced the unstable body position that led to the 130-degree off-heading opening and subsequent fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to correct off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the 130-degree off-heading opening, the jumper failed to execute a heading correction before striking the wall. The narrative suggests the hand-mounted camera may have cost reaction time, but regardless of cause, the failure to correct heading was the final execution failure in the chain that produced the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Difficult exit surface condition
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the exit is 'very uncomfortable and difficult to stand there if you climb over the rail.' This degraded exit surface condition directly impaired the jumper's ability to execute a clean exit, contributing to the foot snag on the rail and resulting head-low over-rotation.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction from hand camera
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the hand-mounted camera 'may have distracted him or cost him some time in terms of reaction and correcting the off heading.' While stated as a possibility, a hand-mounted camera is a plausible source of channelized attention or distraction that could have delayed the heading correction after the 130-degree off-heading opening.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 25 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. While he had 800 skydives, 25 BASE jumps represents very early progression. The exit required managing an uncomfortable stance and clearing a rail — a technically demanding exit that a jumper with limited BASE experience would be less equipped to execute cleanly. The poor exit technique (sideways stance, foot catch) and failure to correct a 130-degree off-heading on a static line cliff jump are consistent with insufficient BASE experience for this object.

L0_description: The jumper performed a gainer that over-rotated, leading to an additional rotation during which the pilot chute wrapped around his leg and the bridle wrapped around his body, resulting in a total malfunction (PC in tow / bridle entanglement) and closed-container impact. The domain reference identifies aerials as exponentially increasing danger, with bridle entanglement around limbs being a specific known risk of deploying from non-belly-to-earth positions. The over-rotation and subsequent deployment from an unstable/inappropriate body position constitute identifiable human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable aerial execution causing entanglement
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a gainer but over-rotated, then added a second rotation. During this unstable, extended aerobatic sequence the pilot chute deployed or was deployed into the jumper's body, wrapping around his leg in a bow-style entanglement and then further wrapping the bridle around his body. The over-rotation and resulting head-down/unstable body position during PC deployment directly caused the PC/bridle entanglement that produced a total malfunction and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 50 skydives and 1 BASE season with 100 BASE jumps. The domain reference states the accepted minimum is 200-250 skydives before beginning BASE, and that aerials 'exponentially increase danger' requiring 'extensive training,' 'careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object.' With only 50 skydives, the jumper lacked the foundational freefall body control and spatial awareness needed for aerobatic BASE exits, directly contributing to the over-rotation and inability to manage the deployment sequence.

L0_description: The accident narrative is only a URL link with no accessible text content describing the sequence of events. The structured fields list possible factors (katabatic airflow, insufficient forward separation on exit, proximity flying) but these are speculative labels without supporting detail. The cause of death (impact in terminal freefall) indicates the jumper never deployed a canopy, but without a substantive narrative explaining what occurred, there is insufficient information to determine whether human error or other causes led to the fatal outcome.

The narrative states 'Unknown at this time' and provides no details about the circumstances of the fatal impact. The cause of death is impact in terminal freefall, indicating the canopy was never deployed, but there is no information to determine why. Without evidence to identify a human error pathway or to positively establish a non-human cause, the classification is Insufficient Information.

The narrative states the circumstances are 'Unknown at this stage.' The only known facts are that this was the jumper's first cliff jump, he had 2 BASE seasons, and he died from subterminal impact near the exit. While a first cliff jump with only 2 BASE seasons and a subterminal impact near the exit point could suggest multiple human error pathways (e.g., inadequate progression, poor exit, failure to deploy), the narrative provides no specific information about what actually occurred during the jump to support any particular classification over others.

The jumper mis-routed the bridle when re-closing the container after a popped pin, creating a pin lock condition that resulted in a total malfunction (pilot chute in tow). This was a rigging error — the jumper failed to verify correct bridle routing from PC to canopy attachment points after replacing the pin, which is explicitly identified as a critical procedural requirement.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle misrouting during reclose
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a pin popped during gear preparation, the jumper re-closed the container but mis-routed the bridle between the two pins, creating a pin lock condition. This rigging error produced a total malfunction (pilot chute in tow) that could not be cleared in flight, directly causing impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to verify bridle routing after reclose
L2_preconditions.description: After the pin popped and the container was re-closed, accepted practice requires checking the bridle routing all the way from PC to canopy attachment points. The jumper did not perform this verification, which would have detected the mis-routed bridle before the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'we can only assume she didn't know about this malfunction,' indicating the jumper likely lacked knowledge of how re-closing a popped pin can create a bridle misrouting pin lock. This specific knowledge gap about the pin lock failure mode from improper bridle routing during reclose directly contributed to the rigging error.

L0_description: The jumper's canopy opened with a 90-degree off-heading on a cliff that 'doesn't allow for mistakes.' The detailed narrative identifies multiple human error factors: suboptimal exit technique (side-slipping instead of pointing skis to the fall line, losing speed), adopting a belly-to-earth position instead of the agreed tucked-race position (contributing to body rotation), and releasing only one toggle during the off-heading correction attempt, which caused an additional 90-degree turn into the cliff. The decision to jump a small, unforgiving cliff with edges that make off-headings problematic also reflects decision-making errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the canopy opened with a 90-degree off-heading to the right, the jumper released only his left toggle and not the right one. This asymmetric input caused the canopy to turn an additional 90 degrees to the right (now 180 degrees off-heading), directly producing the fatal object strike. The violent body rotation from the opening likely contributed to the execution failure, but the result was an incorrect corrective input that drove the canopy into the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The canopy opened 90° off-heading to the right while the jumper's body had been turning left, and the added inertia of heavy ski boots and skis caused a violent body rotation back to the right. This sudden, forceful physical disruption likely overwhelmed the jumper's ability to execute the correct toggle inputs, resulting in releasing only the left toggle instead of performing a proper heading correction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient ski-BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative indicates ski-BASE was a dream the jumper was fulfilling, suggesting limited or no prior ski-BASE experience. The added mass of ski boots and skis fundamentally altered opening dynamics, producing a violent body rotation that a jumper experienced in standard BASE but not in ski-BASE would not have anticipated or trained to manage. This inexperience with the discipline-specific forces directly contributed to the failed heading correction.

L0_description: The jumper did not jump from the established exit point but from a location 20 meters to the right where no viable exit existed. Evidence indicates he climbed down unstable terrain to scout a new exit point without adequate safety measures and fell, attempting an emergency deployment that did not complete before he struck a large ledge 30 meters below. Additionally, his toggles were set asymmetrically (one deep, one shallow brake), and he had not jumped in 3 years, indicating currency and rigging errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Fall from unstable terrain during exit reconnaissance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was not at the established exit point but approximately 20 meters to the right, on unstable terrain, apparently scouting a new exit. Witnesses and physical evidence indicate he did not intentionally jump but fell while checking this location. The fall from unstable terrain while reconnoitering was the initiating event that produced the fatal outcome, as it left him with insufficient altitude and separation to survive — he impacted a large ledge 30 meters below with the parachute not yet fully deployed.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states this was the jumper's first jump after 3 years of not jumping. This extended period without jumping likely degraded his situational awareness, physical coordination, and judgment regarding terrain hazards while scouting the unstable exit area, contributing to the fall.

L0_description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with the rig still packed and the pilot chute still in the BOC, indicating he was unable to locate or extract the pilot chute after exiting. This failure to execute deployment constitutes a human error in the deployment process. The night conditions likely contributed to the difficulty locating the BOC handle.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with the pilot chute still in the BOC and the rig fully packed on his back. He failed to extract and deploy the pilot chute, which was the direct mechanism of the fatal outcome. The narrative states 'possible factors: unable to locate pilot chute,' indicating an execution failure during the deployment attempt rather than a decision not to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The jump occurred at night (23:45). Darkness would have directly impaired the jumper's ability to visually locate the BOC pilot chute handle, contributing to the failure to extract and deploy the pilot chute.

L0_description: The jumper was observed flying very slowly during a proximity wingsuit flight around a rock spire. He impacted trees in terminal freefall with a closed container and no pull attempt. The domain reference identifies flying too slowly as a critical wingsuit error that bleeds energy, reduces available lift, and kills glide performance. The jumper chose a line requiring good glide performance while flying without adequate speed, and failed to abort to the available escape route to the right, constituting human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flying too slowly near terrain
