L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was observed flying very slowly during a wingsuit proximity flight around a rock spire. Flying at insufficient airspeed depleted his available lift and glide performance, preventing him from clearing the terrain. The narrative explicitly states he appeared to be flying very slowly and notes the known lesson that 'we need to fly with speed,' indicating the fatal outcome resulted from inadequate speed management during execution rather than from the decision to fly the line itself, as he had the option to abort by flying right at any time but did not.

L0_label: Slow flight leading to terrain impact
L0_description: The narrative describes the jumper flying very flat with the wingsuit likely stalling, making repeated overcorrections with almost no forward movement for approximately 7 seconds. This flight pattern - flying too slowly/flat near stall - is a well-documented human error in wingsuit BASE. Additionally, despite sufficient time for an emergency deployment, the jumper did not pull, suggesting a decision error in believing he could outfly the terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Despite approximately 10 seconds of flight time and sufficient time for an emergency pull, the jumper failed to deploy his parachute and impacted terrain subterminal with nothing out. The narrative explicitly states there was sufficient time for an emergency pull. Regardless of the flight difficulties, the omission of deployment was the direct mechanism of death.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit flight with overcorrections
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exit, the jumper made oscillating left-right corrections that were excessive, flying very flat with the suit most likely stalling and producing almost no forward movement. These repeated overcorrections and flat/stalled flight attitude resulted in loss of speed and glide, placing him fatally close to terrain. This represents a technique execution failure in wingsuit flight control.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Flight Recovery
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper appears to have fixated on recovering his wingsuit flight path during the oscillating corrections, channeling attention on regaining forward flight to the exclusion of monitoring altitude and terrain proximity. The narrative states there was sufficient time for an emergency pull but he likely 'felt he could out fly the terrain,' indicating his attention was locked on the flight problem rather than shifting to deployment as a survival action.

L0_label: Low pull after terrain proximity flight
L0_description: The jumper flew in extreme terrain proximity, performed a speed-reducing corkscrew maneuver, achieved only marginal separation from terrain, and initiated deployment at an altitude too low to survive. The narrative explicitly identifies late separation from terrain, dependence on an effective flare for pull altitude, and loss of speed as contributing factors—all reflecting decisions and actions within the jumper's control.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient altitude margin for deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper habitually flew close to terrain and relied on flare performance to achieve adequate pull altitude. On this flight, after losing speed from a corkscrew maneuver, he continued flying low above trees and terrain, initiating his flare at approximately 80m or lower above landing with insufficient speed to gain meaningful altitude. This left him in a no-pull zone where even a single complication during deployment would be fatal, and the decision to fly at that altitude with that energy state was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed pilot chute extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: At the critically low altitude available, the jumper failed to extract and deploy the pilot chute on his first two attempts. The first attempt used only one hand (asymmetric reach) and missed the handle. The second attempt grabbed arm wing fabric instead of the handle due to his tilted body position and depressurizing suit. A clean extraction on the first attempt may have been survivable, but the execution failures consumed the remaining altitude margin.

L2_preconditions.label: Habitual low terrain separation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that the jumper 'often separated late from the terrain' and that 'the altitude that he gained from the flare was often needed to get a good pull altitude.' This is a sustained pattern of flying with minimal margin, relying on flare performance for adequate deployment altitude, representing a normalization of deviance that directly enabled the fatal decision to continue flying low with insufficient energy on this jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Altitude awareness degraded without GPS
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative notes the jumper did not have his Flysight (GPS) on this flight. The Flysight provides audible altitude and speed feedback during wingsuit flight. Its absence removed a tool that could have alerted him to his low altitude and reduced speed state, contributing to his failure to recognize the insufficient margin before initiating the flare at approximately 80m or lower.

L0_description: The jumper exited in a wingsuit and immediately entered an unstable, uncontrolled, spinning freefall, clipping a ledge near the exit point and impacting sub-terminally. The unstable exit and failure to achieve controlled flight represent human error in exit execution, leading directly to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and immediately entered an unstable, uncontrolled, spinning freefall. Witnesses observed the wingsuit spinning from shortly after exit through approximately three-quarters of the cliff height. This unstable exit resulted in clipping a ledge and subterminal impact near the exit point, indicating a fundamental execution failure in the wingsuit exit and subsequent inability to establish controlled flight.

L0_description: The narrative field is empty ('None'), providing no account of what occurred. While structured fields indicate a wingsuit proximity flight resulting in terminal freefall impact with possible factors of loss of speed-glide and insufficient separation, there is no narrative detail to deduce a specific human error pathway or to establish a non-human cause. The 'Possible Factors' field lists speculative contributing elements but does not constitute evidence sufficient to classify a specific error or other cause.

The jumper made an aggressive right turn during proximity wingsuit flight that resulted in greater altitude loss than anticipated, leading to terrain impact. The narrative explicitly identifies a 'bad trajectory analyse' — the jumper misjudged clearance from a pillar, likely due to looking far ahead on the line rather than monitoring the close terrain during the turn. This constitutes a human error in flight path management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure during turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper initiated an aggressive right turn during proximity wingsuit flight and failed to maintain awareness of the nearby terrain (pillar) during the turn. Witnesses and video analysis indicate he looked far ahead along the flight line rather than monitoring the close obstacle, and showed no sign of corrective action (flare or course change) before impact. The execution error was in failing to monitor proximity to terrain during an aggressive maneuver, resulting in more altitude loss than anticipated and impact with the pillar.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses and video analysis indicate the jumper fixated on the far flight line after initiating the aggressive right turn, failing to monitor the close terrain (pillar) during the maneuver. He showed no corrective action before impact, consistent with attention channelized on distant reference points to the exclusion of proximate obstacle awareness.

L0_description: The jumper lost all forward performance in his tracking suit near the end of the gully, began falling vertically, and deployed his pilot chute too late, resulting in object strike during line stretch/canopy inflation. The narrative explicitly identifies an emergency low pull as a factor, and the deployment was made at insufficient altitude to achieve a fully flying canopy before striking trees/cliff. The performance loss and delayed deployment represent identifiable human error in flight management and deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of flight performance and late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper lost all forward speed and began falling vertically after making a turn at the end of the gully, indicating a loss of controlled flight (likely a stall or suit performance collapse). He then appeared to struggle with recovery, making swim strokes before deploying his pilot chute too late, resulting in object strike during line stretch. The jumper had the correct plan and appropriate experience but failed in execution: he either allowed his airspeed to decay to the point of stall during the turn, failed to maintain proper body configuration, or both, and then did not deploy early enough to survive.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying a wingsuit along terrain and flew too low from the beginning of the flight. When encountering a flatter terrain section, he lacked sufficient altitude/separation to clear it and attempted an emergency pull too late, resulting in terminal impact. The decision to continue proximity flight at insufficient altitude, combined with flying a line that left no margin for the conditions encountered (headwind, thermals, shade-induced sink), constitutes human error in flight path management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued proximity flight with insufficient margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit proximity line near terrain in conditions that produced worse-than-expected performance (headwind, thermals, shade-induced sink). Despite sinking noticeably from the 4th second onward and flying too low from the first part of the flight, the jumper continued the proximity line rather than deploying or separating from terrain while altitude remained available. By the time he reached the flatter second section, he was too low to clear the terrain and his emergency pull came too late. The decision to continue flying a proximity line without adequate margin — when the flight was clearly underperforming from early on — was the producing mechanism for the fatal terrain impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Atmospheric conditions degraded flight performance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes headwind gusts, high temperature producing thermals, and shade on the wall creating differential heating — conditions that actively degraded the wingsuit's aerodynamic performance. The witness reports the jumper was 'sinking a bit after the 4th second,' experienced 'not much power from the 4th to 9th second,' and 'a bit turbulent flight due to the stronger wind at mid-flight.' These atmospheric conditions (anabatic flow, headwind, turbulence) mechanically reduced the jumper's effective glide ratio and available lift below what was needed to clear terrain, directly contributing to the insufficient margin that characterized the L1 decision error.

L0_description: The jumper performed two backflips from a backward-facing exit off a ramp. He failed to complete the rotations and achieve adequate separation from the cliff, striking a ledge during terminal freefall. Performing aerials from a cliff exit is an advanced technique that exponentially increases danger, and the failure to pull through the rotations and gain separation constitutes identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed aerial recovery and deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted two backflips off the ramp but failed to pull through the rotations in time, striking a ledge below the exit point. The aerials were intentionally chosen, but the execution failed — he did not complete the rotations, did not achieve adequate separation from the object, and did not deploy his parachute before impacting the ledge. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating a failure in execution of the aerial maneuver rather than a flawed decision per se, as the fatal mechanism was the inability to recover from the rotations and deploy.

L0_description: The jumper impacted subterminal near the exit with no parachute out, indicating a total malfunction or failure to deploy. However, the narrative provides no details about the specific cause—whether it was a deployment failure, equipment issue, exit error, or other factor. The limited information ('no parachute out,' found on a ledge 200m below exit) does not allow clear identification of a human error pathway versus an equipment or other cause. While the jumper's low experience (1 season, 40 jumps) and progression against advice are noted, the actual mechanism of the fatality cannot be determined from available information.

The jumper deployed from a cliff (slider-down, BOC) and experienced an off-heading opening that resulted in multiple impacts with the wall. The narrative indicates the jumper scraped the wall around opening and then had an off-heading, suggesting issues with deployment technique or heading correction that constitute human error pathways. The domain reference identifies off-heading with object strike as a known failure mode addressable through proper technique, brake settings, and heading corrections.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to correct off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed the pilot chute and experienced contact with the wall around opening, followed by an off-heading opening. He failed to adequately correct the off-heading, resulting in multiple impacts with the wall. The first jumper experienced a similar wall scrape but flew away successfully, indicating the situation was survivable with proper execution of heading correction.

L0_description: The jumper impacted terrain during wingsuit flight, but the narrative explicitly states they were flying alone and exact information is unavailable. No details on conditions, flight path, equipment state, or specific cause of the terrain impact are provided, making it impossible to determine whether human error or other causes were involved.

The narrative states the probable cause was that Jay misjudged the position of a large boulder while making a left turn into his intended flight path, resulting in terrain impact without deploying his pilot chute. This was a new line for all participants, and the report explicitly concludes with the lesson to 'fly it high the first time' when flying a new line, indicating the jumper flew too close to terrain on an unfamiliar line. This constitutes a human error in terrain assessment and flight path execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Terrain misjudgment during flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jay misjudged the position of a large boulder to the right of his line while making a left turn into the intended flight path shortly after exit. This misjudgment resulted in terrain impact at terminal velocity without pilot chute deployment, indicating he did not recognize the proximity of the terrain in time to take any corrective action. The error was in spatial judgment during execution of the flight, not in the decision to fly the line itself.

L0_description: The narrative is absent and structured fields provide minimal detail. The cause of death is subterminal impact near the exit, which could result from multiple causes (exit error, stall, equipment issue), but without a narrative, experience data, weather information, or witness accounts, neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be reliably identified.

The jumper deployed his pilot chute too late, resulting in ground impact before the canopy could inflate. The witness observed the jumper tracking past well below pull altitude, reaching for the PC late, holding it for approximately one second before releasing, and impacting at full speed with the canopy only partially extracted. This indicates a failure in altitude awareness leading to a fatally late deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to deploy his parachute at a survivable altitude. The witness observed the jumper tracking past and below after the witness's canopy was already open, indicating the jumper continued tracking well beyond the appropriate pull altitude. When he finally reached for his pilot chute, he held his hand on it for approximately one second before pulling, and the canopy had no time to inflate before he impacted trees at full speed. This represents a failure to monitor altitude and initiate deployment in time — an attention/execution error during a task he had performed many times before.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper tracked past and below his partner without apparent awareness that deployment was overdue, even as his partner had already opened. The multi-way tracking format and the slight convergence toward his partner's side may have channelized his attention on the track itself or on maintaining separation, to the exclusion of monitoring altitude. The approximately one-second hesitation with his hand on the pilot chute further suggests he was not monitoring altitude throughout the track and recognized the situation late.

L0_description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with no canopy deployment. The pack job was found intact, indicating no deployment attempt was made. The narrative offers a theory of a medical event (stroke) but explicitly states 'we do not know exactly what happened,' there were no cameras, and the exit was not filmed. There is no positive evidence confirming a medical event (which would support 002OC) nor clear evidence of a human error pathway (which would support 001HE). The information is insufficient to support either classification.

The jumper flew a wingsuit line over a prominent ridge that more experienced jumpers had warned against, in conditions that were less favorable than the previous day (no wind/lift on exit versus the prior day's headwind and lift). Evidence indicates he impacted terrain in terminal freefall near the beginning of his chosen line, with only an emergency deployment attempt, indicating insufficient separation from terrain. The decision to fly this line in degraded conditions constitutes identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose high line over ridge with inadequate margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a high line over a prominent ridge that more experienced jumpers had specifically warned against, rather than the traditional terrain line. The previous day's flight over this ridge benefited from favorable wind conditions (5-10 mph quartering headwind and significant lift on the face), but on the accident day conditions were notably worse — no wind at the exit, hot temperatures with thermals producing, and the jumper who went first reported being noticeably lower than the day before. Despite having been warned about this specific line and despite degraded conditions, the jumper committed to the same ridge-crossing line, resulting in insufficient altitude to clear the terrain and impact in terminal freefall.

L2_preconditions.label: Reduced lift and headwind vs prior day
L2_preconditions.description: On the accident day there was no wind at the exit and hot thermals were producing, compared to the prior day's 5-10 mph quartering headwind and significant lift on the face. The first jumper reported being noticeably lower than the day before under the same conditions. The absence of the headwind component and anabatic lift that had been present the previous day mechanically reduced the jumper's effective glide performance and available lift over the ridge, directly contributing to the fatal terrain impact.

L0_description: The jumper depleted forward speed by maneuvering to regain altitude after a deep start arc, then lost speed and altitude rapidly after aborting onto an alternate line. The narrative describes a trajectory consistent with flying too slowly (near or at stall), a recognized human error in wingsuit BASE, resulting in terminal freefall impact without successful deployment. The decision to use speed to regain altitude rather than maintain energy reserves, combined with apparent loss of altitude awareness, constitutes identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Speed management error during terrain flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After experiencing a deeper start arc than his partner, Dawid used his speed to regain altitude and catch up, which depleted his forward speed. When he then aborted the planned line and veered right around the ridgeline, he lacked sufficient airspeed to maintain flight. The narrative and video evidence indicate he was losing forward speed and altitude rapidly, consistent with approaching or entering a stall, ultimately impacting terrain in terminal freefall without deploying a parachute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Despite rapidly losing altitude and airspeed in a deteriorating flight situation, Dawid did not successfully deploy his parachute before ground impact. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall with no canopy deployed. Whether he attempted an emergency pull too late or failed to initiate one, the result was a failure to achieve deployment when the situation demanded it.

L0_description: The narrative is absent ('None'), providing no details about the circumstances leading to the 180° off-heading and fatal object strike. While a 180° off-heading from a cliff with slider-down configuration resulted in object strike, the cause of the off-heading (packing, body position, PC issues, wind, or equipment) cannot be determined. Without a narrative or additional details, neither a specific human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be reliably identified.

The jumper performed a weak push with bent knees resulting in an immediately head-low, near-front-flip exit from which he never recovered, impacting terrain subterminal directly below the exit point. He had acknowledged a series of bad exits in this wingsuit and expressed wanting to stop jumping it, yet chose to jump a technical exit despite known performance issues. This constitutes identifiable human error in both the exit execution and the decision to jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed a weak push with bent knees and immediately went very head-low, nearly entering a front flip. This unstable exit from a technical cliff in a wingsuit failed to establish the flight attitude needed to generate lift and separation from the object. The resulting lack of separation led to subterminal impact near the exit point.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit problem
L2_preconditions.description: Thor had experienced a series of bad exits in his Alpine 3 wingsuit in the weeks prior and was aware of the problem, explicitly stating he wanted to stop jumping the suit and was waiting for a new one. Despite this known recurring deficiency, he continued jumping the same suit from a technical exit rather than correcting the problem first.

L2_preconditions.label: Misplaced motivation to fix exits
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes Thor declaring 'I have to fix my exits!' before proceeding to jump solo from a technical exit point, despite acknowledging problems with the suit and having a series of bad launches. This suggests a pressure or motivation to resolve the issue by jumping through it rather than stepping back to a more forgiving environment, which directly contributed to the unstable exit.

L0_description: The second jumper performed a gainer that resulted in a bridle/PC entanglement around his body, extending his delay and causing him to impact Jorge's open canopy. This bridle entanglement during an aerial is a human error in execution. The multi-way jump planning that placed an aerial-performing jumper above a jumper with a shorter delay also represents a decision error in briefing and sequencing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle entanglement during aerial
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The second jumper performed a gainer and during the deployment sequence had his bridle and PC entangle around his body. This execution failure during an aerial maneuver extended his deployment time and caused him to fall into Jorge's open canopy below, collapsing it and causing Jorge's fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way planning
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was briefed as a two-way with the second jumper performing a gainer above Jorge, who jumped first with a short delay. This plan placed an aerial-performing jumper directly above a jumper who would be under canopy shortly after exit, with insufficient vertical/horizontal separation to protect against the foreseeable consequence of a delayed deployment during the aerial. The briefing and jump order created the conditions that made the bridle entanglement fatal to Jorge.

L0_description: The jumper misrouted the bridle through the leg lateral on his rig while setting up for a TARD deployment. Video evidence clearly shows the bridle was laid on the ground during gearing up, and the misrouting was not caught before exit. This rigging error constituted a human error that directly caused the fatal malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle misrouting during rigging
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Nathan misrouted the bridle for the static line/TARD deployment, routing it through the leg lateral on his rig. This rigging error directly caused the fatal malfunction. He had performed 10-15 static lines previously and understood the correct procedure, but failed in execution — the narrative attributes the error to a habitual practice of gearing up with the bridle outside the BOC, which on this occasion led to misrouting.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Nathan 'seemed to be in a rush' while gearing up, which is consistent with haste contributing to the bridle misrouting. Rushing through rigging procedures reduces the deliberate attention needed to correctly route the bridle, directly enabling the skill-based execution error.

L2_preconditions.label: No gear check by qualified person
L2_preconditions.description: Nathan's companion was not a BASE jumper and could not have noticed the misrouted bridle. For a TARD/static line deployment, accepted practice requires a knowledgeable person to verify the jumper is clear of rigging before jumping. The absence of a qualified gear check meant the misrouting went undetected.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct habitual rigging practice
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Nathan had a 'habit of gearing up with the bridle outside of the BOC,' which created recurring exposure to misrouting risk. This habitual practice over multiple jumps was a known deficiency that was never corrected and directly enabled the fatal misrouting on this occasion.

L0_description: The narrative states only 'Awaiting more details.' The cause of death is listed as 'Fall before jump,' indicating the fatality occurred before the jump itself, but there is insufficient information to determine whether human error or other causes were responsible. No meaningful analysis can be performed with the available data.

The narrative indicates the jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC during wingsuit flight. The PC was likely still in the BOC upon impact, and the canopy was found close to the container with no deployment. This represents a human error in the deployment process — a failed or missed pull attempt that led to loss of stability and uncontrolled spinning until impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC during wingsuit flight. Evidence indicates the PC was likely still in the BOC at impact, the canopy was found close to the container unpacked, and a witness observed the wingsuit spinning without a canopy deploying. The failed pull attempt likely led to instability and uncontrolled spinning, resulting in ground impact at full speed.

L0_description: The rigger's report concludes the pilot chute was never deployed before water impact, resulting in a subterminal closed-container fatality. However, the reason the jumper did not deploy is unknown. The narrative mentions possible health issues and cold/night conditions, but no definitive cause for the failure to deploy can be established from the available evidence. Whether the jumper suffered a medical event, lost consciousness, became disoriented, or experienced some other factor cannot be determined.

The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening (possibly with line twists) on a slider-up cliff jump and struck the wall under canopy. Rear camera footage shows very little input was made to avoid the cliff, indicating a failure to execute the heading correction needed to avoid object strike. The narrative also notes a short delay and high opening that reduced separation from the wall, and the jumper's previous experience at this site was limited (one prior jump 5 years ago with a shorter delay).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate off-heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a 180-degree off-heading opening (possibly with line twists) facing the cliff wall. Rear camera footage shows very little input was made to avoid the cliff, suggesting she froze or failed to react quickly enough to correct heading and fly away from the object. The failure to execute a timely heading correction resulted in object strike under canopy.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Rear camera footage shows very little input was made to avoid the cliff. The narrator states 'I can only think she possibly froze or just didn't react quick enough.' This suggests a startle response or freeze reaction upon experiencing the off-heading opening facing the wall, which prevented her from executing the heading correction skills she had recently been practicing.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 100 BASE jumps and only one prior jump from this specific cliff (5 years earlier), the jumper was attempting a slider-up terminal cliff jump. Her previous jump from Brento involved only a sub-5-second delay with a slow slider, and she herself recognized she needed more airspeed for better separation. The combination of limited cliff experience and the demands of a terminal slider-up cliff with mandatory tracking contributed to her inability to execute an effective off-heading correction under pressure.

L0_label: Altitude awareness loss and failure to deploy
L0_description: The jumper failed to deploy his parachute despite approximately 7.5 seconds of freefall, impacting terrain in terminal freefall with nothing out. Multiple human errors are identifiable: using a Velcro container (contraindicated for wingsuit and terminal/slider-up applications per accepted practice), jumping a technical wingsuit cliff after a long hiatus with insufficient currency, jumping without any experienced BASE jumping partners for gear checks or site evaluation, and apparent loss of altitude awareness or object fixation leading to complete failure to attempt deployment as terrain approached.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Kenn flew his wingsuit for approximately 7.5 seconds without any attempt to deploy his pilot chute, impacting talus in terminal freefall with nothing out. The video shows no pitching attempt whatsoever despite approaching terrain. The narrator suggests he may have become object-fixated and froze, representing a failure in the fundamental execution task of deploying the parachute at an appropriate altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The video analyst states 'it honestly looked like he got object fixated, once his body was heading towards the rock, and he just froze. There was not any attempt at pitching at all.' This describes a freeze response — a temporary inability to act in the face of rapidly approaching terrain — that directly prevented Kenn from executing any deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator states Kenn 'looked extremely tired from the long climb/hike to the exit' and that it was 'one of the first hot days this season' compounding the fatigue. Physical exhaustion from the approach in heat would have degraded his cognitive and physical capacity, plausibly contributing to his failure to initiate deployment or respond to the developing situation.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator explicitly references 'a long hiatus' and states that 'being uncurrent can have a huge impact on decision making.' While exact duration is not specified, the narrative treats his lack of currency as a significant contributing factor, and the narrator — his only BASE contact — had no idea of his current state. Lack of currency would degrade the practiced, automatic deployment responses needed in a wingsuit BASE environment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Kenn used a Velcro container for a slider-up wingsuit BASE jump. The domain reference explicitly states Velcro rigs are 'contraindicated for terminal, wingsuit, and aerobatic jumps due to risk of premature opening at high airspeeds' and should 'only be used for low-airspeed slider-down applications.' While the fatal outcome was failure to deploy rather than premature opening, this configuration choice reflects a fundamental preparation failure. The possible factor 'unable to locate pilot chute' suggests the equipment configuration may have contributed to his inability to deploy.

L0_label: Poor exit led to stall and impact
L0_description: The jumper executed a steep, asymmetric exit with kicking legs from a site requiring consistent exits. His attempt to correct resulted in a stalled wingsuit and fatal terrain impact. The narrative explicitly identifies multiple human error factors: jumping a site beyond his demonstrated consistency, choosing to jump despite known exit instability issues throughout the course, and the decision to jump into a situation requiring perfect performance he had not demonstrated.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and failed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's exit was 'quite steep with asymmetric and kicking legs.' When he attempted to correct and turn right, he panicked, bent his legs, and dug in his arms, causing the wingsuit to stall almost immediately. This execution failure — an unstable exit followed by incorrect recovery inputs that deepened the stall rather than restoring flight — directly produced the fatal impact. The narrative explicitly notes his exits throughout the course 'had not been great but manageable' in more forgiving terrain, indicating a persistent execution deficiency rather than a decision problem.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped beyond demonstrated ability at unforgiving site
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump an unfamiliar site with only ~170m of vertical relief, steep terrain below, and a mandatory 30-degree turn after inflation — a site the instructors agreed was 'only acceptable for a few students with consistent exits.' The narrative establishes that the jumper's exits throughout the course had not been consistent, and the instructors had been working to improve his start stability with only 'moderate improvement.' Choosing to jump this site despite known inconsistent exits and the site's narrow margin for error was a decision clearly inappropriate given the information available to him. He bypassed the planned sequence (another instructor was gearing up to go first) and insisted he was ready.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was expecting to jump that day and had hiked since 5am with that intention. When the planned site did not materialize and a marginal alternative was found, he was 'very fast to gear up' and 'suddenly appeared over my shoulder on exit ready to jump,' bypassing the planned sequence where another instructor was gearing up to go first. The narrative explicitly identifies that 'expectations did not meet reality for the day, and B. was expecting to jump,' indicating a commitment to jumping that drove his decision to proceed despite the site exceeding his demonstrated ability.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'chose to jump into a situation he hoped he would perform perfectly, without enough repetition and consistency' and that 'experience or confidence gained over 10 years ago should not equate to modern wingsuit jumps.' Despite having 1500 BASE jumps and 200 WS BASE jumps, his exits throughout the course had not been consistent. He overestimated his ability to execute at this unforgiving site, insisting he was ready when the instructors had not cleared him as one of the few students suitable for this exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE currency/proficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Despite 20 BASE seasons and 200 WS BASE jumps on paper, the jumper's exits throughout the multi-day course 'had not been great but manageable' with only 'moderate improvement' despite instructor intervention. The narrative explicitly states that 'experience or confidence gained over 10 years ago should not equate to modern wingsuit jumps,' indicating his current wingsuit exit proficiency was insufficient for the task. This degraded execution capability directly produced the unstable exit and failed recovery.

L2_preconditions.label: Bypassed planned jump sequence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper geared up rapidly and appeared at the exit ready to jump while an instructor was still gearing up to go first. He insisted he was ready, bypassing the coordinated sequence that would have provided him additional information about the site's demands. This failure in coordination contributed to his decision to jump a site that exceeded his demonstrated ability.

L0_description: The official police report states the jumper jumped 'ignoring dangerous weather conditions,' with strong winds reported. His companions declined to jump due to conditions. The decision to jump alone in conditions that other jumpers refused constitutes a human error in judgment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in dangerous weather
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited in strong winds and cloud cover conditions that his companions judged too dangerous to jump. The official police report states he jumped 'ignoring dangerous weather conditions.' This was a wingsuit BASE jump from a cliff where wind conditions directly determine survivability, and the decision to jump when all other jumpers declined due to weather was a clearly inappropriate risk acceptance that produced the fatal outcome (terminal freefall impact).

L0_description: The jumper had an unstable exit and tumbled for approximately 6 seconds before an emergency pull, resulting in insufficient forward separation from the cliff. The canopy opened with a 180° off-heading, and the jumper struck the wall fatally. The unstable exit and inadequate separation are identifiable human error pathways contributing directly to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Francesco had an unstable exit and tumbled for approximately 6 seconds before performing an emergency pull. The unstable exit resulted in inadequate forward separation from the cliff and poor body position, directly leading to the conditions that produced the fatal object strike. With 425 BASE jumps and 1 season of experience from a slider-up cliff object, the exit execution failed to achieve the stable body position and tracking needed for object separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Compressed progression timeline
L2_preconditions.description: With 425 BASE jumps compressed into a single season, the jumper's progression was extremely rapid. The domain reference establishes that time in the sport is as important as jump numbers and that rushed progression is a recognized risk factor. The 6-second tumble without recovery to a tracking position from a slider-up cliff indicates that the body control and recovery skills required for cliff jumping had not matured sufficiently despite high jump numbers, consistent with skills that require time and varied experience to consolidate.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states 'We are awaiting more details on this fatality' and that the jumper was alone with rescue taking over 4 days. The possible factors listed (clipped ledge, not enough separation, unstable exit) are speculative inferences from the outcome (subterminal impact near exit) rather than observed or reported evidence. Without witness accounts, video, or detailed investigation findings, there is insufficient information to establish a specific human error pathway or to confirm a non-human cause.

The jumper attempted to fly a proximity line between terrain features (shark fins) but was too low and too far from the intended line, passing the point of no return before recognizing the problem. The attempted bailout turn resulted in rapid altitude loss, an emergency pull, and fatal object strike. This represents human error in flight path management and line selection during proximity flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew line beyond capability/margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to track between the shark fins but was too low and too far from the fins, passing well beyond the no-return point before recognizing the problem. This represents a decision to fly a proximity line that exceeded available margin — the jumper committed to a trajectory requiring performance (altitude and glide) that was not achievable, placing himself in a no-pull zone from which the attempted bailout (hard right turn) resulted in rapid altitude loss and fatal object strike on emergency pull.

L0_description: The jumper was proximity flying a wingsuit line near terrain and impacted the ground in full flight. The two most likely scenarios both involve decision/execution errors: either flying too slowly (at best glide) resulting in insufficient speed and glide to complete the intended line, leading to loss of terrain clearance, or making an aggressive turn into alternate terrain that was unrecoverable. Both scenarios reflect human error in flight management — either inadequate speed management near terrain or an unrecoverable maneuvering decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide leading to terrain impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit proximity line and lost adequate speed and/or glide performance during flight, resulting in inability to clear terrain. The most likely reconstruction indicates he flew at best glide (too slowly) to reach the intended waterfall line, lost energy, and found himself too low to either continue the line, execute an adequate escape, or deploy his parachute. He impacted terrain in full flight. The fatal mechanism was inadequate execution of the wingsuit flight — either flying too slowly (a recognized performance error that bleeds energy and reduces available lift), misjudging the flight path relative to terrain, or failing to execute a timely escape maneuver when the line became untenable.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had no links to skydiving or BASE jumping, indicating no training or experience. The cause of death is listed as malfunction due to misrigging. An untrained individual purchasing and attempting to use BASE equipment without any skydiving or BASE training represents clear human error in both the decision to jump without prerequisite skills and the misrigging of equipment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping without training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had no identified connection to skydiving or BASE jumping, no USPA membership, and no apparent training or experience. He purchased a BASE rig and attempted to use it without the foundational skills (skydiving proficiency, FJC, mentorship) required to survive a BASE jump. The cause of death was malfunction/misrigging, which is the direct and predictable consequence of attempting to rig and jump BASE equipment without any training. This decision to jump without any prerequisite knowledge or skill was the producing mechanism for the fatality.

L2_preconditions.label: No skydiving or BASE training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had no identified connection to skydiving or BASE jumping, no USPA membership, and no apparent training or experience of any kind. He lacked all foundational skills—skydiving proficiency, first jump course, mentorship—required to rig, pack, and deploy BASE equipment. This complete absence of training directly enabled the misrigging that caused the fatal malfunction.

L0_label: Slow flight and stall into terrain
L0_description: The jumper had a known problem with flying too slowly, had a head-high exit resulting in a bad start, and impacted terrain in terminal freefall with no canopy deployed, consistent with a wingsuit stall into terrain. A close friend confirmed persistent issues with speed and head-low exits, and the jumper was using a suit (C-Base) intended to compensate for skill deficiencies rather than addressing them through training. Multiple human errors in technique and equipment selection for the terrain are clearly indicated.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor exit and slow flight technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed a head-high exit that pendulumed head-down, resulting in a 'bad start' — a known recurring problem for this jumper. Combined with his documented chronic issue of flying too slowly, this poor execution led to insufficient speed and available lift to clear terrain on the flight line, resulting in what witnesses described as a stall at the point of impact. The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with no canopy deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew demanding proximity line beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to lead a group on a demanding proximity line through a V-shaped gulley that required strong speed and glide performance to survive. His close friend confirmed he had chronic problems with speed and head-low exits, and even 2 of 5 subsequent jumpers in higher-performance suits (Corvid 2s) found the line offered little healthy margin with good exits. Choosing to fly this line first — in a suit he was using to compensate for rather than fix his speed deficiency, on terrain where a bad start made survival 'nearly impossible' — was a decision clearly inappropriate for his known skill limitations.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct chronic speed/exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's close friend confirmed they had discussed his problems with head-low exits and slow flying speed for many months. Rather than addressing the root cause through training, he attempted to compensate by purchasing a C-Base wingsuit. This persistent failure to correct a known, recurring performance deficiency directly contributed to the poor exit and slow flight that led to the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence in leading demanding line
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper chose to lead five other jumpers on a demanding proximity line despite chronic, well-documented problems with exit technique and flight speed. His willingness to go first and guide others on terrain where even skilled pilots in higher-performance suits found little margin indicates he unreasonably overestimated his ability to manage this specific line given his known deficiencies.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit skill for terrain
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had a documented chronic inability to achieve adequate speed and proper exits in wingsuit BASE, as confirmed by his closest jumping partner. His friend explicitly stated he needed to 'fix your problems with training and not just changing a suit,' indicating his skill level was insufficient for the demanding proximity flying he was attempting. This skill deficit directly produced the poor exit and slow flight that caused the fatal impact.

L0_description: The jumper flew a technical proximity line requiring precise altitude management at a gate entrance. Evidence indicates he arrived too low at the gate to execute the required right turn, resulting in terrain impact. This was his first wingsuit flight from this exit (previous jumps were in a less powerful tracking suit on a different, less technical line), indicating a decision to fly a technical line without adequate experience in the specific equipment and configuration.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose unsuitable proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a technical proximity line requiring passage through a gate formed by a rock tower and entry into a steep trench, on his first wingsuit flight from this exit point. His prior two jumps from this spot were with a less performant suit (Onesie) on a different, less technical line (left line, outside the tower). The decision to fly this technical line—which required precise altitude management and a properly timed right turn at the gate entrance—on a first wingsuit jump from this exit constituted a clearly inappropriate selection of course of action, as the line's demands exceeded what could be reliably managed without prior wingsuit experience on that specific trajectory.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late turn caused insufficient altitude
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative indicates the jumper set his left turn too late, arriving too low at the gate entrance to execute the necessary right turn to enter the trench safely. This late timing of the turn was a technique/execution error that directly produced the fatal loss of altitude and separation, resulting in impact with rocks just after the gate.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience for line
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 2 BASE seasons and this was his first wingsuit jump from this exit point. His prior experience at this site was limited to two jumps with a less performant Onesie suit on a different, less technical line. Flying a technical proximity line requiring precise altitude management through a gate and into a steep trench demands site-specific wingsuit experience that he did not possess, directly contributing to his selection of an unsuitable line.

L0_description: The narrative reports a 180° off-heading opening resulting in object strike under canopy, but provides no information about the specific cause of the off-heading (packing, body position, equipment issue, environmental factor, etc.). The possible factors listed (180 off-heading, not enough separation) describe the outcome mechanism but do not establish whether human error or another cause produced it. Without details on the jumper's experience level relative to the object, exit quality, packing, body position at deployment, or site-specific conditions, neither 001HE nor 002OC can be supported.

The narrative states only that more information is to be released and provides no substantive account of the accident. While 'Unstable exit' is listed as a possible factor and cause of death is subterminal impact near the exit, the narrative itself contains no details to support a specific human error pathway or to identify a non-human cause. The available structured fields suggest a possible human error but without corroborating narrative detail, classification cannot be reliably determined.

The jumper was found at the bottom of the north face, with the narrative indicating he likely failed to clear a ledge during a wingsuit proximity flight. The possible factors listed (loss of speed-glide, proximity flying) combined with the terrain strike evidence indicate a human error pathway involving flight management near terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to maintain adequate speed/glide for terrain clearance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was performing a wingsuit proximity flight along the north face of Aiguille du Plat de la Selle and was found at the bottom of the face, with the supposition that he did not clear a ledge. The possible factors listed are 'Loss of speed-glide' and 'Proximity flying,' indicating that during execution of the flight he failed to maintain sufficient speed and glide ratio to clear terrain along his chosen line. This is a performance failure in managing airspeed and angle of attack during wingsuit terrain flight, not a decision error in selecting the line itself, given his extensive experience (1500 BASE jumps, 15 seasons, having opened 15 new jumps that summer in the same mountain range).

L0_label: Wingsuit slow flight and late pull
L0_description: The jumper flew his wingsuit too slowly (near stall), failed to follow the planned flight path along the wall for thermal assistance, and lost altitude awareness, resulting in an emergency low pull that was too late to prevent fatal impact. The narrative explicitly identifies flying near stall speed, taking a longer flight path, and lacking altitude awareness as root causes. These represent decision and skill-based errors in wingsuit flight management.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew too slowly near stall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a clean exit, the jumper progressively flattened out and lost forward speed, flying near the stall point of his wingsuit. This slow flight degraded glide performance and available lift, consistent with the well-documented failure pattern of flying too slowly in wingsuit BASE. The loss of speed and glide resulted in insufficient altitude to clear terrain between landing zones.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy at safe altitude
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to recognize that he lacked the altitude and glide to reach the second landing zone and did not deploy his parachute at a survivable altitude. Ground images confirmed he pulled only seconds before impact after crossing the hill before the forest, constituting an emergency low pull with insufficient altitude for the canopy to inflate and arrest descent. The narrative indicates he was likely fixated on reaching the distant landing area rather than monitoring his safety altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper was 'probably too much focused on reaching the second landing area of the jump' and 'lacked complete awareness of the safety altitude.' This channelized attention on the distant landing goal degraded his monitoring of altitude and terrain clearance, directly contributing to his failure to deploy at a survivable altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had approximately 200 BASE jumps and 100 wingsuit skydives, with wingsuit BASE jump count not reported. The narrative explicitly states he 'didn't have yet the experience to assess the speed range of the suit, nor to realize he was flying too close to the stall point.' This specific knowledge and skill gap — inability to recognize and manage the suit's speed envelope — directly contributed to his flying too slowly near stall. Per accepted progression norms, a wingsuit BASE jumper must have full command of the suit's speed range, which requires extensive experience with the specific suit; the narrative positively establishes this experience was lacking.

L0_description: The narrative states the circumstances are 'Unknown at this stage' and provides only that the jumper died from impact in terminal freefall while proximity flying in a wingsuit. No details are available regarding what specific errors or equipment failures occurred. The possible factors listed (emergency low pull, proximity flying) suggest a scenario but provide insufficient detail to deduce a specific human error pathway or to identify a non-human cause.

The jumper impacted terrain approximately 500 ft from the exit and 800 ft below during a wingsuit BASE flight. The guide speculates about possible causes (tinted goggles reducing visibility, inability to turn sharply enough, or intentional proximity flying), but no camera was recovered and no witnesses observed the flight. The narrative does not contain sufficient evidence to definitively identify a human error pathway or to establish a non-human cause. Multiple speculative possibilities exist but none is clearly supported over others.

The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall, meaning the parachute was never deployed. The narrative provides only external news links and no details about what occurred during the jump — whether there was a deployment attempt, equipment malfunction, loss of consciousness, tracking failure, or other factor. With no information about possible factors, weather, or witness accounts of the sequence of events, there is insufficient information to determine whether human error or another cause led to the closed-container impact.

After canopy opening with a slight 15-degree off-heading, the jumper released toggles causing a canopy surge, then initiated a strong left toggle turn that developed into an aggressive 270-degree rotation resulting in high-speed ground impact. The narrative explicitly describes a continued, increasingly aggressive turning maneuver at insufficient altitude, commonly referred to as 'hooking it in,' which constitutes a clear decision/skill error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Aggressive low-altitude turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After toggle release and canopy surge, the jumper initiated a strong left-hand toggle turn that developed into an aggressive 270-degree rotation. The final 90 degrees occurred at very fast speed, resulting in a high-speed impact with the ground. The jumper appeared focused on returning to the landing area and continued the turn despite its increasing aggressiveness, failing to recognize or respond to the rapidly deteriorating altitude and speed situation. This is an execution/attention error — the intended goal (returning to the landing area) was reasonable, but the physical execution of continuing an aggressive low-altitude turn through 270 degrees constituted a failure to manage airspeed and altitude that directly produced the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 25 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. The aggressive 270-degree low-altitude turn demonstrates a lack of canopy piloting judgment specific to BASE environments — managing a canopy surge after DBS toggle release and executing appropriate corrections in the extremely limited altitude available from a bridge jump. With only 25 BASE jumps, the jumper had insufficient experience to have developed the canopy handling skills and altitude awareness needed to recognize and arrest an escalating low-altitude turn before it became unrecoverable.

L0_label: Wingsuit terrain impact from poor flight planning
L0_description: The jumper flew a flat, highly committed wingsuit line at an unfamiliar site on only his second jump there, with approximately 20 WS BASE jumps total. He flew near stall speed without maintaining adequate airspeed, made a right turn that consumed his remaining altitude and energy, and impacted terrain. Multiple human errors are evident: flying a committed proximity line without adequate site knowledge, flying too slowly (near stall), making a turn that eliminated remaining margin, and insufficient preparation and progression for the flight attempted.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committed proximity line beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a flat, highly committed proximity line requiring precise terrain knowledge at a site he had visited only once before, without having studied videos, Google Earth, or other planning tools. He flew left across ridgelines at near-stall speed with minimal speed reserve, then committed to a right turn into terrain he could not see, relying on hoped-for terrain steepness rather than verified information. This decision to fly a committed proximity line without adequate site knowledge or speed margin was the producing mechanism for the fatal terrain impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had not studied any previous videos, Google Earth, or other tools before his first jump, and was on only his second jump at this site. After making the right turn, he was 'pretty blind to what was coming next' — he did not know the terrain geometry beyond the second ridge. This lack of terrain awareness meant he could not accurately perceive his position relative to the terrain during flight, directly contributing to his committed line into unseen terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: No site reconnaissance or flight planning
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to study any videos, Google Earth imagery, or other planning tools before jumping the site. Accepted practice for wingsuit BASE requires intimate knowledge of the entire flight line from exit to landing, including reconnaissance on foot or via other means. This failure to perform basic site reconnaissance directly enabled the decision to fly a committed proximity line into terrain he had not verified.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 20 wingsuit BASE jumps, the jumper attempted a flat, highly committed proximity line requiring precise terrain knowledge and energy management at an unfamiliar site. Accepted progression norms require extensive wingsuit BASE experience before flying near terrain, with gradual progression over many sessions at known sites. Twenty wingsuit BASE jumps is clearly insufficient for committed proximity flying, and this experience deficit directly contributed to his inability to manage speed, glide, and terrain clearance.

L0_description: The jumper was unable to locate and deploy his pilot chute during wingsuit flight, resulting in a full-speed impact (total malfunction). The narrative identifies insufficient preparation and experience for wingsuit BASE jumping at a technical site, with the proximate cause being a failed deployment attempt (mis-pull). This represents human error in both the deployment execution and the progression decisions leading to the jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and deploy pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Philippe had a clean exit and clean flight but failed to extract and deploy his pilot chute, resulting in full-speed impact with the ground. The narrative states he 'didn't pull his pilot chute' and that 'in the precipitation Philippe most likely didn't find his pilote chute, panicked, never found it.' This is an execution failure — the jumper intended to deploy but was unable to locate the BOC handle, constituting a mis-pull that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe had approximately 90 BASE jumps over 4 years, almost all slider-down and slick, before attempting wingsuit BASE with a Freak suit. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 BASE jumps with a long record of perfect exits and comfort with sub-terminal tracking before first wingsuit BASE. His BASE experience was overwhelmingly in a fundamentally different discipline (slider-down slick) that does not develop the skills required for wingsuit deployment, including reliable BOC location at speed in a wingsuit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience for Site
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe had approximately 40 wingsuit BASE flights before attempting Magland, which the narrative describes as 'much more technical than what he was used to' — shorter, more positive terrain, with civilization directly below. This was only his second flight from this exit. The combination of very limited wingsuit BASE experience and an unfamiliar, more demanding site contributed to his inability to execute the deployment sequence under pressure.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Philippe 'most likely didn't find his pilote chute, panicked, never found it.' The initial failure to locate the BOC handle appears to have triggered a panic response that prevented him from recovering and completing the deployment sequence through alternative attempts, contributing directly to the mis-pull.

L2_preconditions.label: Task Saturation
L2_preconditions.description: Magland's more technical and visually demanding environment — shorter, more positive terrain with civilization directly below — combined with Philippe's limited wingsuit BASE experience at this site likely increased cognitive demands during the deployment phase. The unfamiliar visual environment and terrain proximity may have saturated his capacity to execute the BOC reach while managing flight and altitude awareness.

L0_description: The jumper attempted a wingsuit terrain flight requiring a ridge crossover at an exit he had never jumped before, with only 1 BASE season and 160 BASE jumps. The narrative explicitly states he would have benefited from more experience before attempting this flight, and the exit 'requires specific conditions to safely reach the main landing area and the ability to be decisive about committing to a ridge crossover or a parachute deployment over rough terrain.' The jumper's decision to attempt this technically demanding flight without sufficient site-specific experience, combined with possible wind conditions reducing glide performance below what was needed to clear the ridge, constitutes human error in flight planning and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted flight beyond experience level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jonathan, with only 1 BASE season and 160 BASE jumps, attempted a demanding wingsuit terrain flight at a new-to-him exit that required crossing a ridge with narrow margins and rapid decision-making. The narrative explicitly states he 'would have benefited from more experience before attempting this flight to inform the intuition needed for making such a quick judgement call off exit.' The exit required specific conditions and decisive commitment to either cross the ridge or deploy over rough terrain — judgment that depends on site-specific experience he did not have. This was his first attempt at this particular flight path, at a site with a known prior close-call incident, and the decision to commit to the ridge crossing rather than deploying over rough terrain produced the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Jonathan had only 1 BASE season and 160 BASE jumps. The narrative explicitly states he 'would have benefited from more experience before attempting this flight to inform the intuition needed for making such a quick judgement call off exit.' This exit required site-specific intuition for rapid ridge-crossover-or-deploy decisions that develops through accumulated wingsuit BASE experience at demanding terrain sites, which he had not yet built.

L0_description: The jumper flew a conservative line and initiated deployment at adequate altitude, but could not extract his pilot chute. The narrative lists multiple possible factors (late PC extraction, gear failure, health issues, PC entanglement/in tow) without confirming any single cause. The 360 video was distorted and did not allow comfortable analysis. The PC was found out with a folded bridle, but whether this resulted from a human error (e.g., inability to locate BOC, weak throw) or an equipment malfunction independent of human error cannot be determined from the available information.

The jumper opened with a significant off-heading (120-150° to the right) combined with line twists and insufficient forward separation from the cliff, resulting in object strike. The possible factors of inadequate tracking/separation and the resulting inability to correct the off-heading before striking the wall indicate human error in the deployment sequence. The conditions (medium winds, rain) were observable before the jump, and the decision to jump in those conditions contributed to the outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Witnesses observed that the jumper's distance from the wall after opening indicated he did not track very far, despite wearing a Sumo 3 tracking suit designed for separation. The insufficient object separation meant the 120-150° off-heading combined with line twists left inadequate distance to correct heading before cliff strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Adverse weather conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports medium winds and rain at the time of the jump. Rain and wet conditions can degrade tracking performance by affecting body position stability and suit aerodynamics. The jumper had been waiting two days of rain to jump, and the witnesses noted conditions were wet, which may have contributed to degraded tracking execution.

L0_description: The jumper was found dead near the exit after an impact at subterminal speed. The narrative focuses almost entirely on the safety message about informing others of jump plans. Possible factors listed (katabatic airflow, loss of speed-glide, insufficient separation) are speculative labels without supporting detail in the narrative. There is no description of the exit, flight, or specific conditions that would allow identification of a human error pathway or confirmation of an unforeseeable environmental cause. The information is insufficient to support either 001HE or 002OC.

L0_label: Loss of control after wingsuit gainer
L0_description: The jumper performed an aerial (gainer) with a wingsuit and lost control of the suit after the rotation, experiencing strong uncontrolled turns before impacting the cliff in terminal freefall. Performing aerials with a wingsuit is an advanced technique that exponentially increases danger, and the loss of control following the rotation constitutes a human error pathway.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of control after aerial
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a gainer exit with his wingsuit and, while the rotation appeared approximately correct, he failed to recover stable flight after the rotation. He experienced uncontrolled left and right turns, was unable to regain control of the wingsuit, and impacted the cliff in terminal freefall. The intended maneuver was a gainer he had performed on previous days; the failure was in execution — specifically the inability to transition from the aerial rotation into controlled wingsuit flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Repeated gainer exits with wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'was used to jumping a gainer with his wingsuit' in the previous days, indicating a pattern of performing aerials from this exit. Aerials with wingsuits are explicitly identified as exponentially dangerous due to bridle entanglement risk, loss of altitude awareness, and instability. The repeated practice of this high-risk maneuver without apparent corrective action after normalizing it represents a failure to correct a known deficiency — the inherent control recovery challenge of combining aerials with wingsuit flight.

L0_description: The jumper was performing wingsuit proximity flying and clipped a tree while entering her line, resulting in a fatal spiral into trees. The possible factors listed include 'Altitude awareness' and 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late,' and the cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating the jumper failed to deploy in time. Proximity flying near terrain with insufficient margin and late deployment constitute human errors in judgment and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was performing a wingsuit proximity flight and failed to deploy her parachute in time, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. The narrative states she clipped a tree during her line, which sent her into a downward spiral into the trees. The listed possible factors include 'Altitude awareness' and 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late,' and the cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating she failed to execute a timely deployment despite using a PCA setup. The execution of the proximity flight line brought her into contact with a tree, representing a technique/execution error in flying the chosen line.

L0_description: The narrative reports a 180° off-heading and line twists leading to object strike, but the jumper was alone with no witnesses, and there is insufficient detail to determine why the off-heading occurred or whether a specific human error (packing, body position, site selection, equipment configuration) versus an unforeseeable equipment or environmental factor was responsible. The possible factors listed are outcomes, not confirmed causes, and the evidence does not clearly support either a human error pathway or a non-human cause.

L0_label: Wingsuit line selection with degraded start
L0_description: The jumper committed to a more complex flight line (right turn over a shoulder) despite a poor start and progressive speed loss caused by backwind conditions. FlySight data shows constant speed degradation that should have prompted aborting to the simpler straight-line exit, but the jumper continued the planned line until the suit stalled at low altitude near terrain, resulting in an emergency pull with insufficient altitude to fly clear of the slope.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committed to complex line despite poor start
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to continue flying the more complex right-turn line over the shoulder despite having a poor start with progressive speed loss from the beginning, rather than aborting to the simpler straight-down-the-channel line. The FlySight data shows constant and progressive speed loss from the first moment, yet the jumper continued the planned complex line requiring adequate speed and glide to clear the shoulder and separation wall. This decision to maintain the complex line when conditions clearly called for the simpler abort line was the producing mechanism: it placed the jumper over the shoulder at very low altitude and low speed with no escape options, leading to suit stall, emergency deployment with insufficient altitude, and fatal ground impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perceive speed loss
L2_preconditions.description: The FlySight data shows constant and progressive speed loss from the first moment, yet the narrative states Nacho 'should not have noticed' this degradation and continued the complex line. His failure to perceive the deteriorating airspeed — the critical cue that should have triggered an abort — directly enabled the decision to continue the complex line rather than redirecting to the simpler channel exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical overexertion from approach
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly identifies fatigue from the 4-hour, 1300m+ winter alpine approach with full climbing equipment (ice axe, crampons, harness, ropes) through abundant snow in poor conditions as an intrinsic factor. This physical exertion likely degraded cognitive and physical performance, contributing to reduced situational awareness and the failure to recognize the poor start and progressive speed loss that should have prompted an abort.

L2_preconditions.label: Katabatic backwind degraded flight performance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative identifies strong backwind (katabatic airflow) as a factor that caused descent and further height loss on the recovery arc. All three jumpers who jumped that day had poor start numbers, attributed to the backwind. This environmental condition mechanically degraded the wingsuit's flight performance by reducing effective airspeed and glide, directly contributing to the progressive speed loss that made the complex line unviable and should have triggered the abort decision.

L0_description: The jumper used a sled exit method that deviated from a straight path, striking a protruding rock at the cliff edge. This stopped his forward momentum and caused him to fall onto a ledge approximately 30 meters below, rendering him unconscious and unable to deploy his parachute. The decision to use a sled exit on a path with a known protruding rock and known ledge hazard, in sticky snow conditions with less coverage than previous attempts, constitutes a human error in exit planning and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Sled exit from inadequate launch
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to perform a sled exit using a wooden sled with metal rails from a snow-covered exit where the sled tracks deviated approximately 2 meters off-line and struck a protruding rock at the cliff edge. The jumper had prior experience at this exit where he came close to the first ledge 30 meters below, and he expressed fear of that ledge before this jump, yet proceeded with the sled exit despite known marginal conditions (sticky snow, less snow coverage than previous visits, and a launch method with limited directional control). The decision to use this launch method under these specific conditions produced the fatal outcome: the sled veered into the rock, stopping the jumper's momentum and causing him to fall onto the ledge below, rendering him unconscious and unable to deploy his parachute.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was most likely rendered unconscious upon striking the ledge approximately 30 meters below the exit after the sled struck the protruding rock. The witness observed the jumper's head resting against his chest with no effort to correct instability or deploy his parachute, indicating loss of consciousness that prevented any recovery action. While this incapacitation was a consequence of the exit failure rather than a pre-existing condition, it is the physiological state that made the L1 error fatal by eliminating any possibility of corrective action.

L0_description: The jumper performed a TARD (unpacked deployment) from a cliff exit he had never done a TARD from before, in a cycling crosswind, and appeared rushed and nervous on exit. He experienced an immediate 180-degree off-heading opening and struck the cliff within approximately 2 seconds with no appreciable corrective inputs. The choice to perform an unfamiliar TARD variant at an unfriendly cliff in crosswind conditions, combined with the poor exit execution, constitutes identifiable human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed off-heading correction
