L1_unsafe_acts.description: After an immediate 180° off-heading opening, no appreciable corrective inputs were observed before the jumper struck the cliff within approximately 2 seconds. The failure to execute heading correction (via rear risers or brakes) was the direct mechanism that allowed the object strike and subsequent fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: TARD from unfamiliar exit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper performed a TARD from a cliff exit he had not done a TARD from before, and the narrative notes the cliff was 'not a very friendly one for TARDs.' This unfamiliar and suboptimal deployment method from this specific exit created conditions that contributed to the immediate 180° off-heading opening, which the jumper then failed to correct.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste from self-imposed goal
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had set a goal of 365 BASE jumps in the year, had fallen behind, and was trying to catch up by doing multiple jumps per day. The narrative describes him appearing 'a little rushed' during preparation and 'nervous on exit,' exiting 'quietly and without a count.' This self-imposed pressure and resulting haste degraded his preparation and exit quality, contributing to the conditions that produced the off-heading and his failure to respond.

L0_description: The jumper exited from a cliff profile that post-accident measurements confirmed was non-jumpable for wingsuit flight. He either mistook this location for the classic exit or attempted to open a new exit without adequately assessing the terrain profile, constituting a decision error. He struck the snow talus 4.5 seconds after exit due to insufficient vertical relief and forward separation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped non-jumpable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Fabrice exited from a 60m vertical pillar followed by a steep snow talus — a cliff profile that post-accident measurements confirmed was non-jumpable for wingsuit flight. He selected this exit point without adequately assessing whether the terrain profile provided sufficient vertical relief and clearance for wingsuit flight, resulting in impact with the snow talus 4.5 seconds after exit. The decision to jump from this specific exit was the producing mechanism: no execution quality could have overcome a fundamentally inadequate cliff profile.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Fabrice exited from a point ~100m northeast of the known 'classic' exit, and investigators could not determine whether he was trying to open a new exit or mistakenly believed he was at the classic exit. The possibility that he misidentified his position on the mountain directly explains why he jumped from a non-jumpable cliff profile without adequate assessment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate exit point assessment
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'we suppose he didn't really measure the place' — Fabrice jumped from a cliff profile that post-accident measurements confirmed was non-jumpable, indicating he failed to adequately assess the terrain profile before committing to the exit. He had visited the area twice before without his rig but apparently did not verify the specific exit point's jumpability on this occasion.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of currency on opening exits
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Fabrice 'was not current on opening exits' — evaluating and opening new or unfamiliar exit points is a specific skill requiring recent practice. This lack of currency directly contributed to his failure to recognize that the cliff profile was non-jumpable before committing to the jump.

L0_description: The narrative states a head-down exit, early pull around 4-5 seconds, and a 180 off-heading resulting in cliff strike. However, the narrative provides insufficient detail to determine why the off-heading occurred. A head-down exit is not inherently an error for an experienced wingsuit/tracking pilot, and the brief description does not clarify whether the off-heading resulted from a specific human error (body position at deployment, packing issue, equipment problem) or from factors outside the jumper's control. Without clearer information identifying the causal mechanism, neither 001HE nor 002OC can be adequately supported.

The jumper was found 150m from the exit with no camera footage and no flight sight recovered. The narrative explicitly states 'we can only guess what caused the accident.' Multiple possible factors are listed (altitude awareness, late/unable to locate PC, health issues, rotor wind) but none can be confirmed. There is insufficient evidence to establish either a specific human error pathway or a non-human cause.

The jumper was unable to locate the pilot chute handle on his new container system (different BOC placement from his previous rig), resulting in a destabilized body position during the reach attempt and a fatally late deployment. Contributing factors include jumping a new container system without sufficient familiarization and reduced currency.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC of his new LD5 container, which had a different BOC placement than his previous Zack rig. The witness observed him reaching backwards unsuccessfully, after which he destabilized (dipped right shoulder, pitched head down), and by the time the canopy began to emerge he was too low for it to deploy. The failed execution of the pull — a technique/execution error rather than a decision to pull late — was the direct mechanism producing the fatal low deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Unfamiliar container not adequately prepared for
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was flying a new LD5 container he had never jumped before, which had a different BOC placement than his previous Zack rig. Accepted practice requires thorough familiarization with new equipment before use in demanding applications. The witness confirmed that the LD5's BOC was difficult to reach if body position wasn't 100% correct, and the jumper had not adequately prepared for this difference, directly enabling the failed PC extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper was 'not very current at the moment' because that year he had been focusing on a paragliding course rather than BASE jumping. Reduced currency would degrade the fine motor skills and muscle memory needed for a smooth PC extraction, particularly when adapting to unfamiliar equipment, directly contributing to the failed pull attempt.

L0_label: Speed loss and failed canopy response
L0_description: The jumper gradually lost speed during wingsuit flight, a recognized error pattern (flying too slowly/high AoA). After opening with 360-degree line twists and the canopy flying toward the wall, he cleared the twists but made no input to turn away from the cliff for 8-9 seconds, resulting in object strike. The speed loss and failure to execute heading correction after clearing twists indicate human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to execute heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After clearing line twists, the jumper had 8-9 seconds of flight under an open canopy heading toward the cliff wall but made no input to turn away. Despite having cleared the malfunction and having time and control authority available, he failed to execute any heading correction, resulting in object strike. The narrative explicitly states he 'hit the cliff without any input to turn the canopy away from direction to the cliff.'

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: After spending 7 seconds fighting line twists following a 360-degree twist on opening, the jumper cleared the twists but then made zero control inputs for the remaining 8-9 seconds before cliff impact. This complete absence of any corrective action despite having a flyable canopy and time available is consistent with a freeze response — a temporary inability to act following the stress of the line twist emergency and the realization of proximity to the wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Injury During Impact Sequence
L2_preconditions.description: While speculative, the narrative describes the jumper losing speed gradually during flight and pulling after a degraded flight. The 360-degree line twists and 7 seconds spent clearing them while the canopy flew toward the wall may have resulted in a body position or physical state (e.g., disorientation, exhaustion from fighting twists) that impaired his ability to execute inputs in the remaining seconds. However, this is insufficiently supported to code with confidence.

L0_description: The jumper performed multiple gainers (aerials) and over-rotated the last rotation, ending up in a standing/facing-cliff position. This required additional time to reorient and track, resulting in insufficient forward separation from the cliff at deployment. The canopy struck the cliff during inflation due to inadequate object separation, directly caused by the failed aerial maneuver and consequent poor body position.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Over-rotation on aerial exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper intended to perform 3 gainers followed by a track for separation, a maneuver he had successfully completed previously on this trip. On this jump he over-rotated the last gainer, ending up in a standing position still rotating, which left him facing the cliff and required additional time to reorient before he could initiate a track. This execution error directly consumed the time and altitude needed for adequate object separation, resulting in deployment with insufficient horizontal distance from the cliff and fatal object strike during canopy inflation.

L0_description: The jumper had only 12 skydives (never completing AFF), no formal BASE training, no mentor, no packing skills, and attempted a solo jump from a 195ft antenna described as potentially not jumpable at all—surrounded by guy wires, trees, fences, and power lines with no viable platform. He was found with only partial canopy out, indicating a deployment too late or incomplete. Multiple human errors in progression, object selection, and deployment are clearly supported by the narrative.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped unjumpable object
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump a 195ft antenna that the report describes as 'not beginner friendly by any means and may not be jumpable at all,' surrounded by trees, fences, power lines, with protruding antennas making it extremely snaggy, no platform, and requiring climbing outside the structure. This object selection was clearly and specifically inappropriate regardless of execution quality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was found at the base of the antenna with only partial canopy out, and 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late' is listed as a possible factor. On a 195ft antenna with slider-down configuration and BOC deployment, the jumper failed to deploy the pilot chute with sufficient time for the canopy to fully inflate before ground impact, indicating a critical execution failure in deployment timing.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Derrick had only 12 skydives, never graduated from AFF, and was banned from his DZ. The accepted minimum before beginning BASE is 200-250 skydives. This profound lack of foundational training left him without the knowledge and judgment framework needed to evaluate object suitability.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: Derrick had no mentor, no first jump course, and taught himself BASE jumping entirely on his own. He had 'huge gaps in his theoretical knowledge and skills that he seemed oblivious to,' including inability to assess object suitability. He took a packing class only one week before death and lied about his skydiving experience to the instructors.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 12 skydives and no AFF graduation, Derrick lacked the foundational freefall and deployment skills that skydiving training develops. On a 195ft antenna requiring precise BOC deployment timing with minimal margin, this absence of basic deployment proficiency directly contributed to the late extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: Without formal BASE training or mentorship, Derrick had not developed proper deployment technique for packed BOC deployments from low objects. He had exclusively performed unpacked rollover jumps and only took a packing class one week prior. The narrative indicates he did not know how to pack a parachute, suggesting this may have been among his first packed BOC deployments.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Despite 12 skydives, no AFF graduation, no BASE training, being banned from his DZ, multiple mishaps including a cliff strike one month prior, and repeated warnings from skydivers, BASE jumpers, and community members online, Derrick continued jumping increasingly dangerous objects solo. He told his AFF instructor he was 'fully aware of the risks,' demonstrating a persistent and unreasonable overestimation of his ability that far exceeded what any reasonable jumper would exhibit.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Derrick had been repeatedly warned by multiple community members, AFF instructors, and online contacts that he needed to stop BASE jumping and get proper training. He had experienced multiple mishaps including a cliff strike at Bixby Bridge one month before death and a dangerous low turn under canopy at the DZ. Despite awareness of these deficiencies through direct feedback and personal near-misses, he continued jumping progressively more dangerous objects without corrective action.

L0_description: The jumper was found 200 meters below the exit with an open parachute on a snowy ledge. The narrative lists multiple possible factors (180 off-heading, clipped ledge after exiting, fell before setting up, not enough forward separation on exit) but the witness did not observe the exit or flight, the camera was not recovered, and the cause of death is listed as unknown. There is insufficient evidence to definitively identify a human error pathway or to confirm a non-human cause.

The jumper was flying a wingsuit in proximity to terrain and impacted near the tree line past a shaded grass field. The narrative indicates the jumper was attempting or setting up for a proximity line (corkscrew over a ridge to reconnect with terrain) and likely lost adequate speed/glide in sinky, shaded conditions, resulting in terrain impact with no deployment attempt (pins not popped, PC in BOC). Flying a proximity line through conditions that exceeded available margin constitutes human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to maintain adequate speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit proximity line near terrain and appears to have lost adequate speed and glide performance while attempting to reconnect with terrain after corkscrewing over a ridge into a shaded, sinky area. He impacted terrain with pins unpopped and pilot chute still in BOC, indicating he never attempted or had time to deploy. The execution of the proximity flight failed to maintain sufficient energy (available lift) to clear or reconnect with the terrain, resulting in controlled flight into terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Sinking air in shaded terrain
L2_preconditions.description: The grass section of the mountain was still shaded at the time of the jump, creating cold, sinky air conditions on that side of the ridge. This atmospheric condition directly degraded the jumper's glide performance and available lift as he came around the corkscrew maneuver, contributing to his inability to maintain adequate separation from terrain.

L0_description: The narrative states 'Unknown at this time' with no details about the accident circumstances, possible factors, or cause of death. There is no information to identify a human error pathway or to support a non-human cause.

The jumper exited head-low and entered a scorpion/vertical position that progressed into an unrecoverable front flip, resulting in an emergency pull with insufficient separation from the cliff wall. The narrative and possible factors explicitly identify an unstable exit with insufficient forward separation as the mechanism leading to object strike and death.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited head-low and transitioned into a vertical/scorpion position despite intending a normal flat wingsuit exit. She did not recover from the resulting instability and appeared to go into a front flip, ultimately requiring an emergency pull at insufficient altitude/separation from the wall. The execution of the exit was the producing mechanism: inadequate body position led to loss of control, insufficient forward separation, and object strike during or immediately after emergency deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Marta had not jumped her wingsuit for about three months due to an ankle injury. While she made some wingsuit skydives the week before, a three-month gap in wingsuit BASE jumping represents a significant currency deficit for a discipline where precise motor patterns degrade without regular practice. This reduced currency directly relates to the head-low exit and failure to recover stable flight, a skill-based error in execution.

L0_description: The narrative provides only that the jumper died after striking a rock face during a wingsuit BASE jump. No details are reported regarding weather conditions, equipment state, flight path, deployment attempt, or any specific factors contributing to the impact. There is insufficient information to determine whether human error or another cause led to the fatality.

The jumper was unable to locate and extract his pilot chute during the deployment sequence, resulting in a total malfunction and closed-container impact. The narrative explicitly identifies a mis-pull as the cause of death, notes he had experienced a previous mis-pull the day before potentially related to a new flare technique, and describes him attempting to find his PC in a fetal position before impacting. This represents a human error in the deployment process.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and extract pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper initiated his deployment sequence at the top of his flare but was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute. He continued attempting to find the PC, transitioning into a fetal position and rolling onto his back before impact. The narrative explicitly states 'mis-pull' as cause of death and notes he was 'still attempting to find his PC,' indicating a failure in the physical execution of the BOC reach rather than a decision error. He had experienced a previous mis-pull the day before, suggesting a recurring execution problem with his deployment technique during the new flare method.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known mis-pull issue
L2_preconditions.description: James experienced a mis-pull the day before the fatal jump and discussed it with peers, acknowledging the need to return to skydiving to perfect the deployment technique associated with his new flare method. Despite this awareness of a known deficiency, he continued competing without correcting the problem, and the same failure mode recurred fatally.

L0_description: The jumper impacted subterminal near the exit with the canopy still in the container, and the possible factors explicitly identify 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late' and 'Altitude awareness' as contributing factors. This indicates a human error in failing to deploy the pilot chute in time, resulting in ground impact before canopy extraction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit with the canopy still in the container. The possible factors explicitly cite 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late' and 'Altitude awareness,' indicating a failure in execution timing rather than a deliberate decision not to deploy. The cause of death was impact with canopy in container, confirming no deployment was achieved.

L0_description: The jumper exited in a known strong left-to-right crosswind from a position more vulnerable to that crosswind (right side, pushing straight out into a quartering tailwind over more demanding terrain), rather than positioning to mitigate the crosswind as the first jumper did. The narrative also notes the jumper exited without gauging the wind in the moment and with arm wings already outstretched, which made him immediately susceptible to being rolled by the crosswind, resulting in loss of control and subterminal impact. These constitute identifiable human errors in exit technique and decision-making given the known hazardous conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate exit position and direction in crosswind
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Logan chose an exit platform on the right side pushing straight out, rather than the left-side platform pushing into the headwind that the first jumper had used successfully. This positioned him over more demanding terrain with a crosswind/quartering tailwind, making him significantly more vulnerable to being rolled by the known strong left-to-right crosswind. The narrator explicitly notes this choice made him 'more vulnerable to the crosswind/quartering tail and over a more demanding terrain profile.'

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Assess Wind Before Exit
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator states that Logan appeared to be 'already in go mode' when positioning on his exit platform, 'exiting without gauging what the wind was doing in the moment.' This indicates a failure to maintain situational awareness of the immediate wind conditions at the critical moment of exit, directly contributing to his choice of exit position and timing.

L0_description: The jumper committed to flying into a canyon notch despite agreeing to bail early if conditions were not favorable, and despite being warned the canyon was bumpy. He stalled before reaching the first notch and impacted terrain while attempting a late bail, indicating a decision error in continuing the terrain line beyond the point where safe abort was possible.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued into canyon despite plan to bail
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper agreed to bail on the upper canyon early if conditions were not favorable, and was specifically advised to avoid the canyon entirely. Despite this agreement, he continued flying into the canyon line rather than bailing early. He stalled before reaching the first notch and impacted terrain while attempting a late bail to the left, indicating he committed to the canyon line beyond the point where a safe exit was possible.

L0_description: The jumper impacted terrain in terminal freefall while flying a wingsuit. The possible factors explicitly identify 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late' and 'Altitude awareness' as contributing factors, and a witness observed the jumper 'didn't have enough speed,' suggesting inadequate airspeed management leading to insufficient glide performance and failure to deploy in time. The late or absent deployment constitutes a human error that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of terrain impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted a glacier during a wingsuit BASE flight. A following jumper observed that Michael 'didn't have enough speed,' and possible factors list altitude awareness and late pilot chute extraction. However, the narrative provides insufficient detail to determine whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., choosing an inappropriate line or flying conditions) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., poor flight technique leading to insufficient speed, or failure to deploy in time). The mechanism connecting insufficient speed to the glacier impact — whether it was a flight execution problem, a misjudged line, or a late deployment decision — cannot be reliably distinguished from available evidence.

L0_description: The jumper had only 1 prior wingsuit BASE jump, which is drastically below the accepted minimum of 100+ regular BASE jumps and 100+ wingsuit skydives before first wingsuit BASE. The narrative indicates the jumper likely stalled his wingsuit subterminal and lost control, impacting terrain approximately 600-700 meters below the exit. The guide's observation that the jumper used unfamiliar terminology ('beat the suit') and the extremely low experience level point to inadequate progression as a human error pathway. The cause of death was terminal freefall impact, consistent with a wingsuit stall and loss of control.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wingsuit flight execution failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a great exit and body position but appears to have lost control of the wingsuit during flight, likely stalling the suit subterminal — possibly by attempting to turn right too early or flying at too high an angle of attack. With only 1 prior wingsuit BASE jump, the jumper lacked the execution skills to manage the wingsuit flight, resulting in insufficient separation from the object and terminal freefall impact into a ledge approximately 600-700 meters below the exit. The canopy was found deployed, suggesting an emergency pull attempt that came too late or was ineffective due to proximity to terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 prior wingsuit BASE jump. Wingsuit BASE accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 regular BASE jumps before a first wingsuit BASE jump, with many subsequent jumps needed to develop proficiency. With only 2 BASE seasons and 1 prior WS BASE jump, the jumper lacked the experience to manage wingsuit flight near terrain, directly contributing to the loss of control and stall.

L0_description: A bystander witnessed the jumper exit unstable, fail to gain controlled flight, and impact the cliff without deploying a parachute. The narrative identifies inconsistent exits as a known issue for this jumper, and the group notes explicitly state he was recommended to work on exits at simpler objects earlier in the season. The unstable exit from a technical cliff where a poor launch was 'simply not survivable' constitutes a clear human error pathway.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: A bystander witnessed Busto exit unstable and fail to gain controlled flight. He did not deploy a parachute and impacted the cliff multiple times. The narrative indicates he intended a poised exit but failed to execute it with adequate technique, resulting in an unrecoverable body position from a technical exit point with insufficient margin for error.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient exit consistency for technical object
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Busto's exits were known to be inconsistent, and on at least two occasions earlier in the season he was recommended to remain at Brento to work on exits rather than participate in other jumps. The notes further state he was 'jumping beyond personal capability' at a technical exit where 'a poor launch was simply not survivable.' This established pattern of insufficient exit proficiency directly contributed to the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Busto was aware of his inconsistent exits through direct feedback from peers who recommended he stay at Brento to work on exits on at least two occasions earlier in the season. Despite this recurring feedback, he proceeded to a technical cliff exit requiring consistent performance rather than continuing remedial practice at a more forgiving object.

L0_description: The jumper impacted terrain mid-flight while wingsuit proximity flying, with the canopy still in the container. The possible factors listed include altitude awareness, loss of speed-glide, and proximity flying, indicating the jumper failed to maintain adequate separation from terrain and/or failed to deploy in time. This constitutes human error in flight management and/or deployment decision-making.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Terrain impact during wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted terrain mid-flight with the canopy still in the container, indicating a failure during the wingsuit flying phase. The narrative lists possible factors of altitude awareness, loss of speed-glide, and proximity flying, but provides no specific detail about what the jumper did wrong — whether this was a decision error (e.g., choosing an inappropriate line or flying too close to terrain) or a performance error (e.g., failing to maintain adequate speed or misjudging terrain clearance). Insufficient information exists to discriminate between decision and execution failure.

L0_description: The narrative provides only a news link and minimal structured data. The cause of death is impact in terminal freefall, indicating no canopy deployment, but there is no information about what caused the failure to deploy — whether it was a human error (e.g., loss of altitude awareness, unstable flight, deployment failure) or an equipment malfunction or other cause. Without details on the jumper's experience, the flight path, conditions, or deployment attempt, neither 001HE nor 002OC can be supported.

The jumper was unable to locate and deploy the pilot chute, resulting in a closed-container impact. The narrative and possible factors indicate the jumper failed to successfully execute the pull, with contributing factors of altitude awareness issues and focus on filming his companion rather than on flight management and deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed parachute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate or extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket, resulting in no parachute deployment and closed-container impact into trees. The possible factors listed include 'Unable to locate pilot chute' and 'Focusing on filming,' suggesting an execution failure during the deployment attempt rather than a decision not to deploy. The jumper intended to pull but failed to complete the action.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's intent was to film his buddy during the two-way wingsuit flight. The possible factors explicitly list 'Focusing on filming' and 'Altitude awareness,' indicating that attention directed toward the filming task diverted focus from altitude monitoring and timely deployment preparation, contributing to the failure to locate the pilot chute.

L0_description: The jumper performed a solo night jump from a cliff with severely reduced visibility (well after sunset), likely involving aerials, and was found close to the wall with a deployed canopy and toggles still stowed, indicating object strike before achieving controlled flight. The decision to jump at night, the likely performance of aerials in darkness (compromising altitude awareness and body position), and documented below-average tracking skills (reducing object separation) constitute identifiable human errors. The jumper also had a prior history of a nearly identical failure mode at the same exit point (180 off-heading with object strike while focused on aerials).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Night aerial from insufficient-separation object
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Sergey chose to jump solo at night (well after sunset) from High Ultimate, a slider-up cliff requiring forward separation, while plausibly performing aerials. This combination of factors—nighttime conditions degrading orientation and depth perception, aerials that consume altitude and compromise body position/tracking, and his documented below-average tracking skills—constituted a decision that was clearly and specifically inappropriate. The decision to jump in these combined conditions was the producing mechanism: inadequate separation from the wall led to object strike under canopy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient forward separation from cliff
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff face, as evidenced by his body being found on the valley floor close to the wall with toggles still stowed (never achieving stable canopy flight). His documented below-average tracking skills—tracking at a very shallow, almost positive angle of attack with poor performance—directly contributed to insufficient object clearance. Whether aerials were involved or not, the execution failed to produce the separation needed to survive the opening.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded spatial awareness at night
L2_preconditions.description: The jump occurred well after sunset in dark conditions. Reduced visibility would have impaired Sergey's ability to perceive his proximity to the cliff face and assess his separation during freefall and deployment, directly degrading his ability to execute adequate forward separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Redemption motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Sergey had experienced a serious accident at High Ultimate in 2023 involving aerials and a 180 off-heading resulting in severe injuries. He had expressed a strong desire to return for 'redemption,' and reports indicate he was progressively getting into 'sendy mode' during the trip. This motivation plausibly drove his decision to attempt a late solo night jump from the same object where he had previously been seriously injured performing aerials.

L2_preconditions.label: Below-average tracking skills
L2_preconditions.description: Sergey's tracking skills were documented as below average, with a very shallow, almost positive angle of attack resulting in poor forward separation performance. This skill deficiency directly contributed to his inability to achieve adequate object clearance on a slider-up cliff that requires effective tracking for safe separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Night conditions degrading visual reference
L2_preconditions.description: The jump took place well after sunset with limited natural light. Even with a 75% moon, conditions were quite dark. These conditions directly impaired Sergey's ability to maintain orientation, judge terrain proximity, and assess his separation from the cliff during freefall and under canopy, as evidenced by toggles remaining stowed suggesting he never achieved stable canopy flight.

L0_description: The jumper was unable to extract his pilot chute despite multiple attempts over approximately 6 seconds, maintaining a de-arched/head-down body position throughout that prevented successful grasp of the clearly exposed handle. The possible factors identify the jumper extracted the pilot chute too late and was unable to locate/grasp it properly. The failed deployment technique and the body position adopted during extraction attempts constitute human error contributing directly to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Eric was unable to grasp and extract his pilot chute despite multiple attempts over approximately 6 seconds. The handle was clearly exposed and accessible, but he could only get a finger on it, indicating a failure in the physical execution of the deployment reach. He maintained a de-arched, head-down position throughout his attempts rather than adjusting his body configuration to facilitate the reach, and ultimately failed to deploy his parachute before impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Flyby
L2_preconditions.description: Eric formulated a last-minute flyby plan while zipping up his wingsuit at the exit point, and during flight he directed his attention toward executing the flyby (turning 15-20 degrees right, narrating instructions to his partner). This channelized attention on the flyby task likely contributed to him initiating his flare and deployment reach later and lower than he otherwise would have, leaving him in a degraded position (low altitude, post-flare deceleration) when he needed to execute the PC extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate Flyby Planning
L2_preconditions.description: The flyby was formulated last-minute at the exit point while Eric was zipping up his wingsuit, with no specific coordination on height, location, or communication technology. This inadequate planning resulted in Eric flying the flyby at approximately 150ft above terrain over the last ledge of the talus, leaving critically insufficient altitude and time for deployment when he subsequently struggled with PC extraction.

L0_label: Aerial exit led to object strike
L0_description: The jumper performed a gainer (aerial maneuver) that rotated very slowly, resulting in an unstable/poor body position at deployment. This led to a 180-degree off-heading opening with insufficient object separation, causing fatal object strike under canopy. Aerials exponentially increase danger and require careful planning; a slow-rotating gainer indicates inadequate execution, constituting human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit from gainer
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a gainer that rotated very slowly, resulting in an unstable or incomplete rotation. This poor execution led to inadequate separation from the object and a body position that contributed to the 180-degree off-heading opening, which produced the fatal object strike under canopy at high speed.

L0_description: The jumper exited in a wingsuit and quickly entered a scorpion position (feet passing over head), indicating a failed exit that produced an unstable, unrecoverable body position. This instability resulted in insufficient forward separation from the cliff and a subterminal impact with the rock face before or during a last-resort deployment. The scorpion position represents a human performance failure in exit execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Despite a reportedly correct push-off, the jumper immediately adopted a forward-arched torso that progressed into a scorpion position (feet passing above head), representing a catastrophic loss of body control during the wingsuit exit. This prevented establishment of controlled wingsuit flight and resulted in impact with the cliff face approximately 2-5 seconds after exit, before any deployment could arrest the trajectory.

L0_description: The jumper slipped during the exit push, resulting in a heavily head-down unstable exit with insufficient separation from the cliff. He impacted terrain without deploying his parachute. The slip during exit constitutes a human performance failure that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit due to slip
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper slipped during his exit push, causing him to pitch heavily head-down and remain extremely close to the ledge below. This unstable exit resulted in insufficient separation from the object and no parachute deployment before impact with terrain.

L0_description: The jumper attempted an inverted (backwards) static line jump, a complex deployment method that was new to him. He impacted with nothing out approximately 40 seconds after the previous jumper's exit, with the bridle and pilot chute wrapped around his leg near the legstrap. The report explicitly cites incorrect rigging setup, canopy entanglement, and lack of knowledge in complex static line jumping as factors, indicating human errors in rigging, technique, and/or decision-making.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted unfamiliar complex deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to perform an inverted static line jump (falling backwards off the bridge), a complex and unpacked/unconventional deployment method that was new to him. The domain reference establishes that unpacked jumps and aerials require careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object, and that deploying from non-standard body positions (head-up, head-down, back-to-earth) risks bridle entanglement. The narrative and updated investigation confirm he lacked sufficient knowledge of complex static line jumping ('I don't know what I don't know'), and the result was a total malfunction with nothing out and bridle/pilot chute wrapped around his leg near the legstrap — a direct consequence of the decision to attempt this deployment method without adequate knowledge. The decision to attempt this specific jump type was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The investigation conclusion explicitly states 'Lack of knowledge in complex static line jumping (I don't know what I don't know).' While the jumper had 300 BASE jumps and 3 seasons, the narrative confirms the inverted static line technique was 'still new to him.' Accepted practice requires specific instruction and supervised progression for unpacked/unconventional deployments; the jumper's knowledge gap regarding the specific rigging, body position, and entanglement risks of an inverted static line jump directly enabled the decision to attempt it.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate rigging and exit setup
L2_preconditions.description: The updated investigation identifies multiple preparation failures: the anchor point was positioned on the handrail vertical cable rather than the stepping surface, which was not ideal for the exit position and introduced asymmetric loading risk. Additionally, big gloves were hanging from his wrists, creating a snag/entanglement hazard during a deployment where bridle routing through the legs was required. These configuration choices created the physical conditions enabling the bridle/PC entanglement with his leg that produced the total malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste
L2_preconditions.description: The investigation conclusion explicitly identifies 'nervousness and in a rush' and 'external influence' as contributing factors. Being in a rush at the exit point — particularly for a complex, unfamiliar deployment method — is directly contrary to accepted practice, which emphasizes taking time at the exit point and not rushing checklists. This haste likely degraded the quality of his pre-jump verification and exit execution for a technique requiring meticulous preparation.

L0_description: The narrative provides only external links and minimal structured data. The cause of death is subterminal impact near the exit during a wingsuit BASE jump, but there is no descriptive narrative explaining what went wrong — no information about exit quality, flight path, equipment state, weather conditions, or decision-making. Without substantive detail, neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be reliably identified.

The jumper flew an aggressive proximity line on a new exit, turning toward flatter terrain with trees rather than following the safer straight/steep line discussed with his partner. He struck a leafless tree that was difficult to see, lost control, and impacted terrain. The narrative explicitly identifies overconfidence, aggressive flying from the first second, deviation from the planned safe line, lack of proper wingsuit training progression, and a pattern of risk-taking as contributing factors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Aggressive proximity line on unfamiliar exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly an aggressive proximity line on his very first jump from this exit, turning left toward flatter terrain with a tree-covered hill rather than flying the safer straight-out line toward steeper terrain as briefed by his partner. The narrative explicitly states he was 'going aggressive since the first second' and chose to aim through a 'tree gate' on a line he had only seen on YouTube the day before, despite repeated advice to keep it high and safe. This decision to fly a low-margin proximity line through trees on an unfamiliar exit was the producing mechanism for the fatal tree strike and subsequent loss of control.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chronic disregard for progression norms
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had almost no wingsuit skydiving experience yet was flying proximity wingsuit BASE, having essentially started wingsuit jumping directly at Brento. The domain reference requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives before first wingsuit BASE jump. His partner and multiple community members repeatedly warned him about his approach. This fundamental progression deficiency meant he lacked the skills to recognize and manage the hazards of the aggressive line he chose, directly contributing to his inability to identify the leafless tree in his flight path.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states he felt 'really good (too good)' at the exit and was 'overconfident and willing to try this line immediately.' His partner and multiple community members had warned him repeatedly over years about his approach, but he dismissed all concerns. This overconfidence drove his decision to fly an aggressive proximity line on his first jump from this exit rather than following the conservative briefing.

L2_preconditions.label: Visual illusion from leafless trees
L2_preconditions.description: The updated narrative describes a specific visual illusion: leafless winter trees are nearly invisible from above, causing the jumper to perceive greater clearance than actually existed. This perceptual degradation directly contributed to his failure to see the dry tree he struck, enabling the aggressive line choice to produce the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that a similar incident with an unseen dry tree had happened to him just weeks prior ('this happened to him already few weeks back but he got away without anything'), and that multiple people had spoken to him 'thousand times over the years' about his jumping behavior. He had many close calls throughout his BASE career yet consistently failed to correct his approach, representing a sustained pattern of ignoring known recurring risks.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states he had 'almost no wingsuit skydiving experience' and 'essentially did his first wingsuit jumps there [at Brento].' The domain reference requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives before first wingsuit BASE jump. This severe training deficiency meant he lacked the foundational skills in flight efficiency, speed management, and glide assessment needed to recognize the hazards of the aggressive proximity line he chose.

L0_label: Slip on snowy exit without crampons
L0_description: The jumper cleared snow from the exit rock but was not wearing crampons in winter/snowy conditions. The most probable cause of death was slipping during the exit push or approach to the edge, resulting in an uncontrolled fall with no flight initiated. Jumping from a snow-covered exit point without crampons constitutes a human error in managing the physical environment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Slip during exit on snowy terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper slipped either while pushing off or while moving toward the edge of the exit point on a snow-covered cliff in winter conditions without crampons. The narrative states he was found at the base of the cliff 'as if he had never even taken flight,' and the reporter's most probable hypothesis is a slip ('zipette'). The execution of the exit — maintaining footing on a snowy/wet rock surface — failed, resulting in an uncontrolled fall rather than a wingsuit launch.

L2_preconditions.label: Snowy/wet exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The exit point was covered in snow in winter conditions. Despite the jumper's efforts to clear and dry the rock, the surrounding terrain remained snow-covered and the rock surface had been wetted. The snowy/icy surface directly degraded the jumper's ability to maintain footing during the exit push, causing the slip.
